Document 341 of 500

Leaving political office with wisdom and grace

Author: Snobelen, John

Publication info: Snobelen, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaving-political-office-with-wisdom-grace/docview/3076354354/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaving+political+office+with+wisdom+and+grace&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Snobelen%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I was just shy of 30 when I had the dubious job of telling someone he was past his prime.
The gentleman in question was an icon. He had forgotten more than I will ever know. Worst of all, he was a friend.
But father time is undefeated and it fell on my young shoulders to tell this amazing man he needed to step aside. He could have continued to perform for a while, but his diminishing capabilities would have done damage to his legacy. He deserved better.
So, we had the conversation. He was a gentleman. But I swore as I got into my pickup truck that I would never make someone tell me I was past my prime.
Decades later, another friend announced he was leaving the leadership of an institution he had led brilliantly.
He could have hung on for another decade. But he stepped aside.
I called him and asked him why.
He said the sweet spot in leadership of a big organization is about five years. It takes two years to find the bathrooms and three years to implement a strategy. After that, it's just a long cycle of explaining, rationalizing and defending decisions.
My wise friend made the difference he could and then left before the explaining, justifying and defending began.
That is the cycle of mastery - learning, contributing and then leaving room for the next generation. It can be done with dignity and honour.
Mostly it's not.
Most people want to contribute and make a difference. But few have the wisdom to leave the stage before the lights go down.
The few who leave before they have exhausted their moment on the stage will exit with a unique grace.
There are no better examples than Mike Harris and Brad Wall.
Brad Wall is the proud son of Swift Current. He followed an interest in politics to the Saskatchewan legislature, helping to form and then becoming leader of the Saskatchewan Party.
He won three consecutive majority governments and was the most popular premier in Canada. And then he quit.
Mike Harris came to the Ontario legislature from North Bay. When the Ontario PC party slipped from decades in power to third-party status, Harris sought and won the party's leadership. He then won two consecutive majority governments and ... quit.
Harris and Wall turned around their respective provinces. They each had big agendas that they successfully implemented. And they both left office with gas left in the tank.
They had the wisdom and courage to leave before the inevitable cycle of explaining, justifying and defending. They left the stage with a measure of dignity.
My favourite definition of a leader is someone who causes something's time to come. But the brilliance of leadership isn't constrained to championing a future. It's knowing when the time has come to make way for the next generation.
Whatever you may think about the leadership skills of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. President Joe Biden, they clearly lack the wisdom, courage and humility to exit the political stage on their own terms. They aren't just out of gas - they are in the ditch.
Biden and Trudeau have at least one thing in common - their overriding objective has always been beating the other guy (initially Trump and Harper respectively). Maybe they should have set their sights higher.
But it's too late now. They will never enjoy the wisdom and grace of Wall and Harris.
Snobelen was a cabinet minister in the Conservative government of Ontario premier Mike Harris from 1995 to 2002

Subject: Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: dubious; telling; someone; prime; gentleman; question

Title: Leaving political office with wisdom and grace

Document 342 of 500

Dry summer or dried up?; LCBO strike puts privatization on agenda

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dry-summer-dried-up-lcbo-strike-puts/docview/3076354238/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dry+summer+or+dried+up%3F%3B+LCBO+strike+puts+privatization+on+agenda&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unionized liquor store workers say they are striking to save their jobs. They may end up losing them instead. The world has changed for alcohol retailing in Ontario and the LCBO, and its striking workers don't seem to realize that.
"Tonight, Ford's dry summer begins," Colleen MacLeod, chair of the union's bargaining team, said on Thursday ahead of the strike.
MacLeod said that if Ontario residents can't get the alcohol they want this summer, it will be because of Premier Doug Ford. Actually, because of several moves Ford made over the last several years, residents of the province will still mostly be able to get the drinks they want despite the strike.
Since the depths of the pandemic, any restaurant or bar that sells alcohol has been able to sell alcohol to go. That means every restaurant or bar in the province can act as a de facto liquor store.
While in most instances these outlets charge more, there will likely be some enterprising operators who take advantage of the strike to sell booze at a reasonable price from their locations.
There are also craft distillers, craft brewers and wineries across the province that can sell their products from their own production facilities.
The province will even be promoting locations where residents will be able to find alcohol to purchase, from The Beer Store to local Wine Rack locations, local producers to pop-up bottle shops.
This will not be the dry summer the union has been promising.
Oh, and starting in less than a month, the expansion of sales in grocery and convenience stores will begin.
Does the union realize they aren't fighting a losing battle, but a battle the province has already won? Do they not realize their actions are proving why having a unionized government monopoly of alcohol sales is a bad idea? Government can make money without a government store The union relied heavily on the idea that alcohol sales at the LCBO fund health care and education and without the sales coming through LCBO stores, the funding for those services would be lost.
"The revenues from those sales should stay in the LCBO, they shouldn't help billionaires buy another yacht," said JP Hornick, president of the Ontario Public Service Employees union.
It's true that the LCBO turns over $2.5 billion to the province each year and that money does help fund services. Yet, if we look at other provinces, we can see that the government can still earn money from alcohol sales without controlling all aspects of the sale, warehousing and wholesaling of the product.
Quebec has a hybrid model of alcohol sales with beer and wine widely available in private stores while also operating the SAQ, the government-run liquor store chain. Alberta has a fully private model for retail and has for more than 30 years.
Both Quebec and Alberta earn significantly more per capita from alcohol via taxes and fees than Ontario does via the LCBO. And often, the prices in those provinces are lower than they are in Ontario.
If having a government monopoly on booze doesn't bring consumers lower prices and it doesn't bring the government more revenue, then what is the point of having one? With this strike, the LCBO workers who think they are protecting their jobs are laying the groundwork for their elimination.
Alberta moved towards a fully private liquor retailing system starting in 1993 and they won't look back. Saskatchewan closed their last government liquor store in 2023 and customers are better served now than they were under the old system.
The Ford government has never looked to eliminate the LCBO, it simply wasn't on their agenda. This strike and the inconvenience to consumers, as well as the bar and restaurant industry will put this issue on the table.
As the government begins a planned review of rules, regulations, taxes and wholesale pricing they can add another item to the discussion - privatization.

MESSAGE IN A BOTTLE: LCBO staffers, booze buyers adjust as strike begins

Author: Boland, Jack

Publication info: Boland, Jack. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/message-bottle-lcbo-staffers-booze-buyers-adjust/docview/3076299705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MESSAGE+IN+A+BOTTLE%3A+LCBO+staffers%2C+booze+buyers+adjust+as+strike+begins&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Boland%2C+Jack&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The temperature for the first two weeks of July will be hot and sticky - while the LCBO forecast for the next 14 days will be dry.
For the first time in the Crown corporation's history, 9,000 LCBO workers hit the picket lines and 685 stores across the province were closed as talks with the province and the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) shut down Thursday night.
LCBO retail stores will remain closed for 14 days, pending an agreement. Should the strike last beyond two weeks, the LCBO will open 32 stores three days a week - Friday, Saturday and Sunday - with limited hours. Online ordering systems will remain available.
Outside a Danforth Ave. LCBO location just east of Victoria Pk. Ave., about 40 picketers walked the line for the first day on strike and were greeted by pedestrians and motorists honking horns and high-fiving them for their work action.
Janice Ariza, a secretary with OPSEU Local 5111 and a fixed-term employee, explained their fight isn't about wages. Rather, it's about job security.
"I was optimistic, then almost in tears last night (Thursday) because this is the first time in LCBO history that we were forced to strike," Ariza said.
"It's not about wages. It's about securing good jobs, about benefitting communities for generations," she added.
LCBO workers picket outside the LCBO location on Danforth Ave. just east of Victoria Park Ave. in Scarborough on Friday, July 5, 2024. JACK BOLAND/TORONTO SUN
The LCBO generates about $2.5 billion in public money each year that goes towards funding hospitals, education and community programs.
Danielle, who hails from Newfoundland and Labrador, says in her home province liquor or beer stores don't exist - residents get booze from the corner store.
She said she isn't a liquor drinker but supports the LCBO workers' right to strike.
'I DO BELIEVE IN WHAT THEY ARE FIGHTING FOR'
"I do believe in what they are fighting for. So thumbs up to them and I hope they get what they are looking for," she said.
Sylvie drove into the LCBO parking lot with her husband on Friday and wasn't aware of the strike. An LCBO staffer informed her of the details.
"It's ridiculous. It's ridiculous and I don't blame them for going on strike," she said, adding she would be going to the nearby Wine Rack or Metro grocery store hoping to purchase her favourite Chilean wine.
On Thursday night, OPSEU bargaining committee chair Colleen MacLeod said that job security is at the forefront as the Ontario Conservative government is pushing forward a plan to bring spirits into "mom and pop" stores, thus potentially eliminating LCBO jobs in the future.
Speaking to reporters Thursday night, MacLeod said "LCBO workers are ready to make history. Tonight, (Premier Doug) Ford's dry summer begins."
LILLEY: LCBO strike puts privatization on agenda
LILLEY: LCBO picks fight with distillers and you end up paying more
One worker picketing at the Danforth and Victoria Park Ave. store in Scarborough said she has been involved with union talks in the past and wages hadn't even been mentioned.
She said on Friday her go-to East York LCBO location at Coxwell Ave. near O'Connor Dr. looked like something you would see at Christmas as people jammed the store stocking up on booze - many perhaps expecting more than a 14 day strike.
At the nearby Metro grocery store at Shoppers World Danforth, the shelves were stacked with beer and wine and consumers bumped into each other with their shopping carts while filling up with wine.

Document 344 of 500

SNOBELEN: Leaving political office with wisdom and grace

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/snobelen-leaving-political-office-with-wisdom/docview/3076295622/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SNOBELEN%3A+Leaving+political+office+with+wisdom+and+grace&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I was just shy of 30 when I had the dubious job of telling someone he was past his prime.
The gentleman in question was an icon. He had forgotten more than I will ever know. Worst of all, he was a friend.
But father time is undefeated and it fell on my young shoulders to tell this amazing man he needed to step aside. He could have continued to perform for a while, but his diminishing capabilities would have done damage to his legacy. He deserved better.
So, we had the conversation. He was a gentleman. But I swore as I got into my pickup truck that I would never make someone tell me I was past my prime.
Decades later, another friend announced he was leaving the leadership of an institution he had led brilliantly. He could have hung on for another decade. But he stepped aside.
I called him and asked him why. He said the sweet spot in leadership of a big organization is about five years. It takes two years to find the bathrooms and three years to implement a strategy. After that, it's just a long cycle of explaining, rationalizing and defending decisions.
My wise friend made the difference he could and then left before the explaining, justifying and defending began.
That is the cycle of mastery - learning, contributing and then leaving room for the next generation. It can be done with dignity and honour. Mostly it's not.
Most people want to contribute and make a difference. But few have the wisdom to leave the stage before the lights go down.
The few who leave before they have exhausted their moment on the stage will exit with a unique grace. There are no better examples than Mike Harris and Brad Wall.
Brad Wall is the proud son of Swift Current. He followed an interest in politics to the Saskatchewan legislature, helping to form and then becoming leader of the Saskatchewan Party.
He won three consecutive majority governments and was the most popular premier in Canada. And then he quit.
Mike Harris came to the Ontario legislature from North Bay. When the Ontario PC party slipped from decades in power to third-party status, Harris sought and won the party's leadership. He then won two consecutive majority governments and ... quit.
Harris and Wall turned around their respective provinces. They each had big agendas that they successfully implemented. And they both left office with gas left in the tank.
They had the wisdom and courage to leave before the inevitable cycle of explaining, justifying and defending. They left the stage with a measure of dignity.
My favourite definition of a leader is someone who causes something's time to come. But the brilliance of leadership isn't constrained to championing a future. It's knowing when the time has come to make way for the next generation.
Whatever you may think about the leadership skills of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. President Joe Biden, they clearly lack the wisdom, courage and humility to exit the political stage on their own terms. They aren't just out of gas - they are in the ditch.
Biden and Trudeau have at least one thing in common - their overriding objective has always been beating the other guy (initially Trump and Harper respectively). Maybe they should have set their sights higher.
But it's too late now. They will never enjoy the wisdom and grace of Wall and Harris.
LILLEY: Trudeau won't give up on idea of taxing your home
Most Canadians think Trudeau will stay on to lead Liberals in next election: Poll

LILLEY: LCBO strike puts privatization on agenda

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-lcbo-strike-puts-privatization-on-agenda/docview/3076292471/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+LCBO+strike+puts+privatization+on+agenda&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

If having a government monopoly on booze doesn't bring consumers lower prices and it doesn't bring the government more revenue, then what is the point of having one?

Document 346 of 500

Folded like a cheap tent; Anti-Israel protesters exit - U of T leadership should too

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/folded-like-cheap-tent-anti-israel-protesters/docview/3075817981/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Folded+like+a+cheap+tent%3B+Anti-Israel+protesters+exit+-+U+of+T+leadership+should+too&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All it took was a little leadership and the Tentefadeh at the University of Toronto pulled up stakes and left campus. The protesters standing up for Hamas at U of T left ahead of Wednesday's 6 p.m. deadline laid out by Judge Markus Koehnen of the Ontario Superior Court.
If only U of T president Meric Gertler had shown some leadership earlier, the campus and surrounding communities and the families showing up for convocations would not have had to put up with this.
As soon as protesters tried to set up encampments at the University of Calgary, the University of Alberta and York University, police were called, protesters were told they were trespassing and they were removed. Gertler tried to reason and negotiate with people who had no interest in either.
Like spoiled children, they made their demands to the university and would not waver in the slightest.
They denounced Israel as an apartheid state, a terrorist state, the source of all evil in the world. They demanded Israel be isolated, that an arms embargo be imposed upon them, that U of T boycott Israeli academia, disclose their investments and then divest of any companies related to Israel.
What these so-called pro-Palestinian protesters didn't do was demand Hamas release hostages, demand Hamas accept a ceasefire or lay down their arms.
They took over an area that had been fenced off by U of T to prevent a protest and used it to their advantage. That included setting up a checkpoint at the entrance and deciding who could and couldn't enter based on their politics.
Gertler needs to answer to the governing council as to why he let this protest fester so long.
There are people who want to blame the Toronto Police Service for not clearing the encampment immediately. Others want to blame Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, whichever is their least favourite politician.
The failure here lies solely at the feet of president Gertler and his senior administration at U of T.
It was their decision not to call police for weeks. It was their decision to allow campers to stay, erecting semi-permanent structures and installing portable toilets.
By doing that, the school administration effectively granted the Hamasnik campers permission to stay, thus the police decision that they would not remove them without a court injunction.
Had Gertler acted earlier, none of this would have been necessary.
The protesters were outraged that Koehnen sided with the owners seeking to protect their private property. They were also aghast at the idea that he would shut down their freedom of expression by ending the encampment.
"Case law is clear that exercising freedom of expression is not a defence to trespass," Koehnen wrote in his decision. "The university has suffered irreparable harm because of the protesters'continued appropriation of Front Campus and their exclusion of others from Front Campus."
As they left, the protesters denounced the school and police and said they would not give them then the spectacle they wanted.
"We refuse to be props in their performance of law and order," the group said in a statement. "But make no mistake: We are not leaving this fight. We are evolving."
To the rest of us, the important part is that they are leaving.
The next steps going forward should be all about strengthening the leadership at U of T. That's a process that may require replacing the president. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Trespassing; Truces & cease fires

Location: Israel

Document 347 of 500

Grits' blame misdirected; PM, not local candidate, at root of byelection loss

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grits-blame-misdirected-pm-not-local-candidate-at/docview/3075817468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grits%27+blame+misdirected%3B+PM%2C+not+local+candidate%2C+at+root+of+byelection+loss&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals have done some deep thinking about why they lost the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection and have decided it was Leslie Church's fault. Forget the fact the Liberals have been trailing badly in the polls for the past year. Forget that the prime minister is unpopular. Let's blame the local candidate.
That's what Liberal MP Karina Gould is doing.
The Ontario co-chair for the next election, the person in charge of finding new candidates, is showing gratitude to Church by blaming her. Good luck with that recruitment for the next election! "I think the lesson here is that she was nominated a week or two before the byelection was called," Gould told The Canadian Press in an interview.
"And so she needed more time to be able to get out there and get known in the riding."
The way Gould and CP tell the story, the Conservatives had the advantage because they nominated their candidate earlier and so he could campaign and get to know voters in the riding. Meanwhile, says Gould, Church couldn't campaign because she wasn't nominated until May 1, a little more than two weeks before the byelection was called.
That's a nice piece of fiction, a story to explain away a bad loss, but here is what really happened.
Carolyn Bennett, who had held the seat since 1997, announced in July of last year that she would not run in the next election, but would stay on as an MP. By December, she had resigned her seat and in January was appointed Canada's ambassador to Denmark.
Meanwhile, Church, who had been Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff, resigned from her job in October 2023 and moved back to Toronto to try to secure the nomination.
Despite being a loyal soldier as a party volunteer and high-level staffer for years, despite visiting Bennett to try to kiss the ring and get the retiring MP's endorsement, some in the party didn't want Church. The party delayed the nomination, hoping in vain for a star candidate. They didn't get one and, in the meantime, Church was working the riding hard to secure the nomination.
The Liberals could have held the nomination meeting at any point, but chose to delay. They also chose to hold the vote on June 24, the day before the capital gains tax changes took effect.
It was the Liberals who controlled every aspect surrounding the timing of this byelection and they completely screwed it up. Now, faced with a bad loss in a riding the Conservatives should never have won, the Liberals are blaming the local candidate.
There are calls from inside the Liberal fold for the PM to dance PM 10 hold a caucus meeting to discuss the byelection loss, but Gould doesn't support that idea.
"I think what's important is that caucus members are really engaging their community, really hearing what's on people's minds," she told the CP.
Well, in Toronto-St. Paul's, the local candidate and her volunteers just spent weeks knocking on doors and talking to residents to hear what is on their minds and one recurring theme is Justin Trudeau has to go. There were past Liberal voters telling Church's team that while they liked her, they couldn't vote for her while Trudeau was leading the party.
That's the message voters sent to the Liberals last week, but it's a message they don't want to hear, which could cost them another safe Liberal seat.
There is another coming byelection for the Montreal riding of LaSalle-Emard-Verdun to replace David Lametti. It's been a Liberal seat for decades except for the 2011-15 period when the NDP represented the area after the Orange Wave. It could go NDP again with local councillor Craig Sauve running as a candidate for the New Democrats.
The Trudeau Liberals lost the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection for many reasons, but Justin Trudeau and his policies were at the core of it all. The sooner they realize that the better off they and the rest of the country will be.

Subject: Nominations; Voters; Elections

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 348 of 500

LILLEY: Anti-Israel protesters exit, now U of T leadership should too

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-anti-israel-protesters-exit-now-u-t/docview/3075761021/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Anti-Israel+protesters+exit%2C+now+U+of+T+leadership+should+too&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: All it took was a little leadership and the Tentifada at the University of Toronto pulled up stakes and left campus. The protesters standing up for Hamas at U of T left ahead of Wednesday's 6 p.m. deadline laid out by Justice Markus Koehnen of the Ontario Superior Court of Justice.
If only U of T president Meric Gertler had shown some leadership earlier, the campus and surrounding communities and the families showing up for convocations would not have had to put up with this.
As soon as protesters tried to set up encampments at the University of Calgary, the University of Alberta and York University, police were called, protesters were told they were trespassing and they were removed. Gertler tried to reason and negotiate with people who had no interest in either.
Like spoiled children, they made their demands to the university and would not waver in the slightest.
They denounced Israel as an apartheid state, a terrorist state, the source of all evil in the world. They demanded Israel be isolated, that an arms embargo be imposed upon them, that U of T boycott Israeli academic, disclose their investments and then divest of any companies related to Israel.
What these so-called pro-Palestinian protesters didn't do was demand Hamas release the hostages, they didn't demand that Hamas accept a ceasefire or lay down their arms.
They took over an area that had been fenced off by U of T to prevent a protest and used it to their advantage. That included setting up a checkpoint at the entrance and deciding who could and could not enter based on their political point of view.
Protesters at the University of Toronto anti-Israel encampment vacated King's College Circle by 5 p.m., an hour before the injunction was to kick in on Wednesday, July 3, 2024. Robert Norman "Doc" Higgins, 71, who received his PhD in 1992 at U of T, shows his solidarity towards the protesters and rips up his diploma. JACK BOLAND/TORONTO SUN
Gertler needs to answer to the Governing Council as to why he let this protest protest fester so long and get out of hand.
There are people who want to blame the Toronto Police Service for not clearing the encampment immediately. Others want to blame Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, whichever is their least favourite politician.
The failure here lies solely at the feet of president Gertler and his senior administration at U of T.
It was their decision not to call the police for weeks. It was their decision to allow the campers to stay on campus erecting semi-permanent structures and installing portable-potties.
By doing that, the school administration had effectively granted the Hamasnik campers permission to stay on campus. Thus the police decision that they would not remove the encampment without a court injunction.
Had Gertler acted earlier, none of this would have been necessary.
Anti-Israel protesters at the University of Toronto encampment finally vacate King's College Circle on Wednesday, July 3, 2024. JACK BOLAND/TORONTO SUN
The protesters were outraged that Justice Koehnen had sided with the owners of private property in seeking to protect their property. They were also aghast at the idea that he would shut down their freedom of expression by ending the encampment.
"Case law is clear that exercising freedom of expression is not a defence to trespass," Justice Koehnen wrote in his decision. "The University has suffered irreparable harm because of the protesters' continued appropriation of Front Campus and their exclusion of others from Front Campus."
Judge grants injunction against pro-Palestinian U of T encampment
U of T prof infiltrated encampment and calls protesters 'mostly non-students'
As they left, the protesters denounced the school and police and said they would not give them then the spectacle they wanted.
"We refuse to be props in their performance of law and order," the group said in a statement. "But make no mistake: We are not leaving this fight. We are evolving."
To the rest of us, the important part is that they are leaving.
The next steps going forward should be all about strengthening the leadership at U of T. That's a process that may require replacing the president.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Trespassing; Injunctions; Truces & cease fires

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310; Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: little; leadership; tentifada; university; toronto; pulled

LILLEY: Liberals blame local candidate, not Trudeau for loss

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-liberals-blame-local-candidate-not-trudeau/docview/3075683078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Liberals+blame+local+candidate%2C+not+Trudeau+for+loss&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals have done some deep thinking about why they lost the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection and have decided it was Leslie Church's fault. Forget the fact that the Liberals have been trailing badly in the polls for the last year, forget that the prime minister is unpopular, let's blame the local candidate.
That's what Liberal MP Karina Gould is doing.
The Ontario co-chair for the next election, the person in charge of finding new candidates, is showing gratitude to Church by blaming her. Good luck with that recruitment for the next election!
"I think the lesson here is that she was nominated a week or two before the by-election was called," Gould told Canadian Press in an interview.
"And so she needed more time to be able to get out there and get known in the riding."
The way Gould and CP tell the story, the Conservatives had the advantage because they nominated their candidate earlier and so he could campaign and get to know voters in the riding. Meanwhile, says Gould, Church couldn't campaign because she wasn't nominated until May 1, a little over two weeks before the byelection was called.
That's a nice piece of fiction, a story to explain away a bad loss, but here is what really happened.
Carolyn Bennett, who had held the seat since 1997, announced in July of last year that she would not run in the next election but would stay on as an MP. By December she had resigned her seat and in January was appointed Canada's ambassador to Denmark.
Canada's Minister of Crown-Indigenous Relations Carolyn Bennett speaks during question period in Ottawa February 18, 2020.
Meanwhile, Church, who had been Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff, resigned from her job in October 2023 and moved back to Toronto to try to secure the nomination.
Despite being a loyal soldier as a party volunteer and high-level staffer for years, despite visiting Bennett to try to kiss the ring and get the retiring MP's endorsement, some in the party didn't want Church. The party delayed the nomination hoping in vain for a star candidate, they didn't get one and, in the meantime, Church was working the riding hard to secure the nomination.
The Liberals could have held the nomination meeting at any point, they chose to delay. They also chose to hold the vote on June 24, the day before the capital gains tax changes took effect.
It was the Liberals who controlled every aspect surrounding the timing of this byelection and they completely screwed it up. Now, faced with a bad loss in a riding the Conservatives should never have won, the Liberals are blaming the local candidate.
There are calls from inside the Liberal fold for the PM to hold a caucus meeting to discuss the byelection loss, but Gould doesn't support that idea.
"I think what's important is that caucus members are really engaging their community, really hearing what's on people's minds," she told CP.
Well, in Toronto-St. Paul's, the local candidate and her volunteers just spent weeks knocking on doors and talking to residents to hear what is on their minds and one recurring theme is Justin Trudeau has to go. There were past Liberal voters telling Church's team that while they liked her, they couldn't vote for her while Trudeau was leading the party.
That's the message voters sent to the Liberals last week, but it's a message they don't want to hear which could cost them another safe Liberal seat. There is a coming byelection for the Montreal riding of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun to replace David Lametti.
It's been a Liberal seat for decades except for the 2011-15 period when the NDP represented the area after the Orange Wave. It could go NDP again with local councillor Craig Sauvé running as a candidate for the New Democrats.
The Trudeau Liberals lost the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection for many reasons, but Justin Trudeau and his policies were at the core of it all. The sooner they realize that the better off they and the rest of the country will be.
blilley@postmedia.com

DEEGAN: Ontario helping sustain quality journalism

Author: Shantz, Ian

Publication info: Shantz, Ian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/deegan-ontario-helping-sustain-quality-journalism/docview/3075661773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=DEEGAN%3A+Ontario+helping+sustain+quality+journalism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Shantz%2C+Ian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Canada, governments have supported journalism for generations. Since the founding of Canada's first newspaper, the Halifax Gazette, which dates to 1752, government advertising has been an important source of revenue for newspapers. Even prior to Confederation, direct supports, like the former Publications Assistance Program, which subsidized the postal delivery of non-daily newspapers, ensured that Canadians had access to high-quality Canadian news.
Today, community, regional and national news publishers are struggling with declining advertising revenue. In 2012, Canadian newspaper advertising revenue stood at $3.55 billion. Today, it is under $1 billion. Yet, four out of five Canadians still read newspapers, regardless of format, at least once a week - essentially unchanged since 2012.
While things will never go back to the way they were, and nor should they, governments can do a much better job supporting our domestic news businesses by addressing the advertising revenue challenge through their own advertising spending and procurement policies.
We were very encouraged by the recent news coming out of Premier Doug Ford's government in Ontario that they are directing Crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation, the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation and Metrolinx to invest 25% of their advertising spend to Ontario-based news businesses. This is forward-looking, smart policy that other provinces, municipalities and the federal government can follow, and it does not involve any additional taxpayer dollars.
Ford recognizes the importance of journalism to Ontario's economy. According to the Government of Canada's Job Bank, there are 4,350 journalists working in Ontario. The impact of those journalists goes beyond the economy as they keep communities across the province connected, informed and engaged.
One of the most powerful tools in any government's policy toolkit is procurement. Procurement can help governments advance socioeconomic policy objectives, including creating jobs and delivering better outcomes. When the Government of Canada spends more per year on advertising with China-based TikTok ($1.1 million) than it does on all of Canada's print publications combined, something is seriously wrong with government advertising procurement policies. The same holds true for other orders of government.
Ontario isn't the first jurisdiction to adopt an "earmark" approach when it comes to government advertising. New York City's Local Law 83 mandates the creation of an annual spending report on advertising, displaying each agency's spending and compliance toward spending 50 per cent of advertising on ethnic and community media.
Subscription revenue alone may be enough to fund websites devoted to intelligent commentary, but fact-based, fact-checked coverage of schools, cops, courts, politicians and businesses - holding the powerful to account - costs real money and demands advertising revenue.
We encourage governments at all levels, as well as our leading corporations like the large banks, telcos and retailers, to take a cue from the Government of Ontario and follow its lead in supporting our domestic news publishing industry by earmarking 25% of their advertising spend to news businesses. This will allow us to reinvest in high-quality Canadian journalistic content and in innovative techniques that harness the power of digital tools to engage audiences and sustain viable news businesses that serve the public interest.
-Paul Deegan is president and CEO of News Media Canada.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Journalism; Advertising revenue; Newspapers; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising revenue

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: canada; governments; supported; journalism; generations; founding

Title: DEEGAN: Ontario helping sustain quality journalism

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 352 of 500

LILLEY: Ford government takes bold move to support journalism

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-government-takes-bold-move-support/docview/3075661729/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+government+takes+bold+move+to+support+journalism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is making a major move in support of journalism in the province.
Premier Doug Ford has instructed his ministers that as of September, a significant portion of the province's advertising budget must be spent with Ontario media outlets.
Perhaps more importantly, Ford has instructed his ministers in charge of the province's four biggest agencies, in terms of advertising, to do the same.
Letters have now been sent to the chairs of the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Commission, the Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Metrolinx, and the Ontario Cannabis Store. Starting later this summer, each of these agencies will be required to set aside 25% of their advertising budget for provincially based sources.
"Ontario's major government agencies are some of the largest advertisers in the province, together spending well over $100 million on marketing each year," the letters state. "These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province."
In the letters to the organizations, the Ford government states that they want the Crown agencies to spend more advertising with "Ontario-based corporations, trusts or partnerships that have been designated as Qualified Canadian Journalism Organizations by the Canada Revenue Agency."
The list of organizations qualified by the federal government includes small and large publications including titles owned by Postmedia, owner of the Toronto Sun as well as other such newspapers as the National Post, London Free Press and Ottawa Citizen. Community publications such as The Creemore Echo or specialized publications such as The Trillium newsletter covering Queen's Park also qualify.
Governments of all stripes and at all levels across the country have said that they want to support journalism, but few have been willing to do so, putting most of their money into Google and Facebook products. The latest annual report from the federal government shows that they spent $1.1 million advertising on TikTok in fiscal year 2022-23 despite all the security concerns around the social media app which has been banned from government devices.
By comparison, the Trudeau government spent just $944,602 on any and all print advertising across Canada in the same year.
TikTok received more advertising from the federal government that fiscal year than every Postmedia newspaper, the Toronto Star, the Globe and Mail, Journal de Montreal and every community newspaper combined. It's an odd message for the federal government to send to the media industry, saying that we are vital for news organizations' future but we're just going to spend all of our advertising with digital giants.
The federal government, which currently spends 71% of their advertising budget on digital, should consider a similar move to what Ford is pushing through. It would be a far better way to support journalism and homegrown media outlets that giving all your money to Facebook and Google while sending subsidy cheques to media companies.
In the latest federal advertising report, $9.9 million is spent on search engine marketing, the same amount the government spent on all television advertising across Canada. The bulk of that money goes to Google, while most of the $13 million the feds spend on social media marketing goes to Meta, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram.
In the most recent Review of Government Advertising by the auditor general for the province of Ontario, $4.5 million out of $28.8 million of Ontario government ad spending went to print advertising. That works out to roughly 16% of all reviewable Ontario government ads.
While the directive letters are aimed at the four main agencies and their advertising budgets, the Ford government will go further and require the same 25% of all government advertising spending be placed with Ontario publishers.
"When in place across Ontario's major agencies, this new initiative promises to flow over $25 million to Ontario-based publishers, helping to protect jobs and promote local content and culture," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy wrote to LCBO Chair Carmine Nigro.
"The Government of Ontario is making similar commitments with our own advertising spending, helping to provide even more support for Ontario jobs and promote Ontario culture."
Canada's media industry has gone through incredible upheaval over the past decade or more. That said, there is still an audience for news that informs us what is happening in our country, our province and our hometowns with a local point of view.
You don't get that by spending your entire advertising budget with organizations based in Menlo Park or Mountain View, Calif. You get that by supporting quality local journalism by advertising with the publishers making the investment in your own community.
Premier Doug Ford's bold move is not only a welcome one, it's also a move that should be emulated by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau across the federal government and by provincial and municipal governments across the country.
blilley@postmedia.com

Document 353 of 500

'MEANINGFUL MOMENT': Ford gov't supports Ontario-based news publishers with $25M in advertising

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/meaningful-moment-ford-govt-supports-ontario/docview/3075659657/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27MEANINGFUL+MOMENT%27%3A+Ford+gov%27t+supports+Ontario-based+news+publishers+with+%2425M+in+advertising&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Ford government will direct one-quarter of its advertising budget towards news publishers in Canada's largest province, a targeted boost for a major domestic industry facing unprecedented pressure from internationally owned technology giants.
That should amount to about $25 million in advertising each year going to Ontario publishers, including Postmedia Network Inc., which owns National Post, the Toronto Sun, Ottawa Citizen and community papers in the province.
"This is important. Everyone understands the value of journalism and a free press and its criticality to Canada and to democracy," said Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer.
"I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier (Doug) Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a North Star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations."
In a letter, sent to Metrolinx (the public transit Crown corporation), the Ontario Lottery Gaming Corporation, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy asks the government agencies to have the policy implemented by Sept. 3.
"Our government believes strongly in the importance of supporting and promoting Ontario-made products, services and workers," Bethlenfalvy's letter says.
The initiative is in addition to the Progressive Conservative government's Building Ontario Businesses Initiative, which uses government procurement spending to support Ontario businesses, the Ontario Made Manufacturing Investment Tax Credit and initiatives aimed at boosting sales of Ontario-made beer and wine.
"In addition to these actions, we believe there's a compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content," the letter says.
Ontario's major government agencies spend more than $100 million each year on advertising.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," the letter says.
Publications eligible for the government advertising must be designated "Qualified Canadian Journalism Organizations" by the federal Canada Revenue Agency and must be Ontario-based corporations, trusts or partnerships.
"The Government of Ontario is making similar commitments with our own advertising spending, helping to provide even more support for Ontario jobs and promote Ontario culture," the letter says.
MacLeod said the initiative is something the publishing industry has been lobbying for with the Ontario government and other governments in Canada, including the federal government, and that it "didn't make sense" that advertising dollars, paid for by taxpayers, were going to Google and Facebook.
"As I've said countless times, the single biggest challenge facing our industry is that 90 per cent of the digital advertising budgets flow to foreign platforms," MacLeod said. "This is the first major, material step where we're seeing a significant institution, the province of Ontario, allocating a material portion of their spend on Ontario publishers, which supports Ontario journalism, Ontario jobs and the Ontario economy."
Companies, including Postmedia, will need to wait to figure out just how much money they could get and what it will mean, MacLeod said.
"It's a hugely meaningful moment for our industry, certainly from an Ontario perspective, and to the extent that it serves as a beacon or a catalyst for other provincial governments and for the federal government to do the same," MacLeod said.
"What does it mean for Postmedia? I think time will tell. But we're one of the larger players, and we should have a meaningful pro rata portion of that based on the audiences that we reach in Ontario."
The directive will be reviewed quarterly, the letter says. If it is not working to meet the objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs," the government notes it may withdraw the funding directive.
Postmedia, the largest newspaper company in Canada, saw its revenues drop to $97.3 million for the three-month period ending Feb. 29, 2024. It was mostly attributable to a 20.8 per cent fall in advertising revenue.

'Meaningful moment'; Ford gov't makes major investment in Ontario-based news publishers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/meaningful-moment-ford-govt-makes-major/docview/3075549427/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Meaningful+moment%27%3B+Ford+gov%27t+makes+major+investment+in+Ontario-based+news+publishers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 355 of 500

Ontario helping sustain quality journalism

Author: Deegan, Paul

Publication info: Deegan, Paul. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-helping-sustain-quality-journalism/docview/3075549330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+helping+sustain+quality+journalism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Deegan%2C+Paul&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Canada, governments have supported journalism for generations. Since the founding of Canada's first newspaper, the Halifax Gazette, which dates to 1752, government advertising has been an important source of revenue for newspapers. Even prior to Confederation, direct supports, like the former Publications Assistance Program, which subsidized the postal delivery of non-daily newspapers, ensured that Canadians had access to high-quality Canadian news.
Today, community, regional and national news publishers are struggling with declining advertising revenue. In 2012, Canadian newspaper advertising revenue stood at $3.55 billion. Today, it is under $1 billion. Yet, four out of five Canadians still read newspapers, regardless of format, at least once a week - essentially unchanged since 2012.
While things will never go back to the way they were, and nor should they, governments can do a much better job supporting our domestic news businesses by addressing the advertising revenue challenge through their own advertising spending and procurement policies.
Smart policy
We were very encouraged by the recent news coming out of Premier Doug Ford's government in Ontario that they are directing Crown corporations like the LCBO, the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation, the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation and Metrolinx to invest 25% of their advertising spend to Ontario-based news businesses. This is forward-looking, smart policy that other provinces, municipalities and the federal government can follow, and it does not involve any additional taxpayer dollars.
Ford recognizes the importance of journalism to Ontario's economy. According to the Government of Canada's Job Bank, there are 4,350 journalists working in Ontario. The impact of those journalists goes beyond the economy as they keep communities across the province connected, informed and engaged.
One of the most powerful tools in any government's policy toolkit is procurement. Procurement can help governments advance socioeconomic policy objectives, including creating jobs and delivering better outcomes. When the Government of Canada spends more per year on advertising with China-based TikTok ($1.1 million) than it does on all of Canada's print publications combined, something is seriously wrong with government advertising procurement policies. The same holds true for other orders of government.
Ontario isn't the first jurisdiction to adopt an "earmark" approach when it comes to government advertising. New York City's Local Law 83 mandates the creation of an annual spending report on advertising, displaying each agency's spending and compliance toward spending 50 per cent of advertising on ethnic and community media.
Cost of quality
Subscription revenue alone may be enough to fund websites devoted to intelligent commentary, but fact-based, fact-checked coverage of schools, cops, courts, politicians and businesses - holding the powerful to account - costs real money and demands advertising revenue.
We encourage governments at all levels, as well as our leading corporations like the large banks, telcos and retailers, to take a cue from the Government of Ontario and follow its lead in supporting our domestic news publishing industry by earmarking 25% of their advertising spend to news businesses. This will allow us to reinvest in high-quality Canadian journalistic content and in innovative techniques that harness the power of digital tools to engage audiences and sustain viable news businesses that serve the public interest.
Paul Deegan is president and CEO of News Media Canada.

Subject: Journalism; Advertising revenue; Newspapers; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising revenue

Location: Canada

Document 356 of 500

Tories take a stand; Ford government makes bold move to support journalism

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-take-stand-ford-government-makes-bold-move/docview/3075549197/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+take+a+stand%3B+Ford+government+makes+bold+move+to+support+journalism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is making a major move in support of journalism in the province.
Premier Doug Ford has instructed his ministers that as of September, a significant portion of the province's advertising budget must be spent with Ontario media outlets.
Perhaps more importantly, Ford has instructed his ministers in charge of the province's four biggest agencies, in terms of advertising, to do the same.
Letters have now been sent to the chairs of the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Commission, the Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Metrolinx, and the Ontario Cannabis Store. Starting later this summer, each of these agencies will be required to set aside 25% of their advertising budget for provincially based sources.
"Ontario's major government agencies are some of the largest advertisers in the province, together spending well over $100 million on marketing each year," the letters state. "These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province."
In the letters to the organizations, the Ford government states that they want the Crown agencies to spend more advertising with "Ontario-based corporations, trusts or partnerships that have been designated as Qualified Canadian Journalism Organizations by the Canada Revenue Agency."
The list of organizations qualified by the federal government includes small and large publications including titles owned by Postmedia, owner of the Toronto Sun as well as other such newspapers as the National Post, London Free Press and Ottawa Citizen. Community publications such as The Creemore Echo or specialized publications such as The Trillium newsletter covering Queen's Park also qualify.
Governments of all stripes and at all levels across the country have said that they want to support journalism, but few have been willing to do so, putting most of their money into Google and Facebook products. The latest annual report from the federal government shows that they spent $1.1 million advertising on TikTok in fiscal year 2022-23 despite all the security concerns around the social media app which has been banned from government devices.
By comparison, the Trudeau government spent just $944,602 on any and all print advertising across Canada in the same year.
TikTok received more advertising from the federal government that fiscal year than every Postmedia newspaper, the Toronto Star, the Globe and Mail, Journal de Montreal and every community newspaper combined. It's an odd message for the federal government to send to the media industry, saying that we are vital for news organizations'future but we're just going to spend all of our advertising with digital giants.
The federal government, which currently spends 71% of their advertising budget on digital, should consider a similar move to what Ford is pushing through. It would be a far better way to support journalism and homegrown media outlets that giving all your money to Facebook and Google while sending subsidy cheques to media companies.
In the latest federal advertising report, $9.9 million is spent on search engine marketing, the same amount the government spent on all television advertising across Canada. The bulk of that money goes to Google, while most of the $13 million the feds spend on social media marketing goes to Meta, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram.
In the most recent Review of Government Advertising by the auditor general for the province of Ontario, $4.5 million out of $28.8 million of Ontario government ad spending went to print advertising. That works out to roughly 16% of all reviewable Ontario government ads.
While the directive letters are aimed at the four main agencies and their advertising budgets, the Ford government will go further and require the same 25% of all government advertising spending be placed with Ontario publishers.
"When in place across Ontario's major agencies, this new initiative promises to flow over $25 million to Ontario-based publishers, helping to protect jobs and promote local content and culture," Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy wrote to LCBO Chair Carmine Nigro.
"The Government of Ontario is making similar commitments with our own advertising spending, helping to provide even more support for Ontario jobs and promote Ontario culture."
Canada's media industry has gone through incredible upheaval over the past decade or more. That said, there is still an audience for news that informs us what is happening in our country, our province and our hometowns with a local point of view.
You don't get that by spending your entire advertising budget with organizations based in Menlo Park or Mountain View, California. You get that by supporting quality local journalism by advertising with the publishers making the investment in your own community.
Premier Doug Ford's bold move is not only a welcome one, it's also a move that should be emulated by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau across the federal government and by provincial and municipal governments across the country. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Journalism; Federal government; Marketing; Print advertising; Budgets; Advertising expenditures; Social networks

EDITORIAL: A moment of karma for Liberal MPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-moment-karma-liberal-mps/docview/3075409620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+A+moment+of+karma+for+Liberal+MPs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's amusing that the federal Liberal caucus is now experiencing what many Canadians have experienced when it comes to getting Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to listen to them.
That is, he seems to be ignoring them.
Worried their political survival in the next election is at stake given the Liberals' surprise loss to the Conservatives in last week's byelection in what had previously been the Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's, some Liberal MPs are calling for an urgent, national, in-person caucus meeting with the PM to discuss next steps.
Of course, one of those steps could be Trudeau resigning as national leader, as a handful of current and former Liberal politicians have publicly called for him to do in the face of months of national polls all showing the Liberals trailing badly behind Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives.
Instead, with Parliament now adjourned for the summer recess, Trudeau has been carrying on with business as usual, attending political and community events, including dancing up a storm in front of a food stall at the annual Taste of Asia festival in Markham, Ontario near Toronto on Friday.
On Monday, during a Canada Day interview with CBC, Trudeau said while there will be "lots of reflection" among Liberals about the loss in Toronto-St. Paul's, "I am committed to doing the work of building a better Canada every single day, so I look forward to next year's Canada Day and I look forward to many more Canada Days."
On Tuesday, the prime minister's office announced he will be attending the NATO summit meeting in Washington next week from July 8 to 11.
So, for the prime minister, at least, his motto for now appears to be "keep calm and carry on."
GUNTER: Trudeau staying will benefit Conservatives in election
GOLDSTEIN: Trudeau Liberals attacking free speech on climate change
In some ways, that's understandable.
While there is clearly rising concern about Trudeau's continued leadership of the Liberals within the Liberal caucus after three election wins and nine years as PM, most Liberal MPs remain publicly supportive or silent on the issue of his continued leadership.
Things haven't reached the level of an open caucus revolt against the PM and the next federal election is still more than a year away, assuming Trudeau's confidence-and-supply agreement with NDP leader Jagmeet Singh until next June, holds.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: amusing; federal; liberal; caucus; experiencing; canadians

Title: EDITORIAL: A moment of karma for Liberal MPs

Document 358 of 500

Yonge-Dundas renaming proves council isn't listening

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/yonge-dundas-renaming-proves-council-isnt/docview/3074884730/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Yonge-Dundas+renaming+proves+council+isn%27t+listening&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give Toronto city council credit for its stunning honesty.
Elected officials often at least pretend to be listening to the people and doing what is best for the community.
When change was in the wind, they would "listen to the people" and "meet with stakeholders" and hire an outrageously compensated consultant or three to tell them what they wanted to hear. Then they would go ahead and do what they wanted to do in the first place.
An exception is Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
Ford has racked up a list of ideas and projects started and reversed. He has been criticized for that but, whether you like it or you don't, he is listening to the people.
In Toronto, under Mayor Olivia Chow, council members have decided that ramming things down an unwilling public's throat is a better way to go.
To make it even more fun for them, they have taken to insulting the public while they are at it.
Renaming Yonge-Dundas Square has been agenda-driven and against public will from the start. The whole thing began when someone got together a petition of 14,000 names asking for a renaming, based on a weak interpretation of the history of Henry Dundas and slavery.
Council jumped on that so quickly, one would have to wonder if the idea was percolating at city hall already.
Yet, when presented with a counter-petition more than twice that, of over 30,000 names, Councillor Chris Moise called the petitioner a racist.
When I discussed the issue with Councillor Shelley Carroll, she yelled at me as she went out the door, "Thirty thousand people signed that petition. That's how many racists there are."
She doubled down on that position later during council.
The vast majority of people polled - by professionals, not councillors talking to their friends - are against the change and the chosen name Sankofa Square, as that term comes from Ghana, a slave state.
Are all of those people racist? Or are they just people who disagree with increasingly hateful, agenda-driven councillors? A man called my radio show to say he disagreed with me on this issue. He said he is Black and now with the renaming, he will finally have a place where he can take his family and be proud.
Carroll tells us Black people have been in pain over this issue, so I asked my caller to tell me honestly if he had been in pain over the fact a street and a square had been named after Dundas before the issue was raised at city hall.
He said, "Well, I didn't know about it."
I asked if he was okay with the name Sankofa as it came from a nation whose economy was based almost entirely on the slave trade, going back before the Atlantic slave trade. He said he wasn't aware of that either. Exactly. A well-meaning man who was sold a victim story when he had not seen himself as a victim until Carroll and others told him he was one.
What sort of people do that? What sort of people manufacture a contentious, city-dividing, race-baiting issue and then, against almost all public will, ram it through into law? I won't ram an answer down your throat.
You decide.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Listening; Councils; Petitions

Document 359 of 500

Chow i've seen everything!; T.O.'s mayor has spent past year looking as natural in Technicolor costume as on council floor

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-ive-seen-everything-t-o-s-mayor-has-spent/docview/3074884710/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+i%27ve+seen+everything%21%3B+T.O.%27s+mayor+has+spent+past+year+looking+as+natural+in+Technicolor+costume+as+on+council+floor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-07-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The past year has been like one giant costume party for Toronto's new mayor - and dressing up suits her well.
Mayor Olivia Chow is living her best life as Toronto's chief magistrate, often dressed up as not herself. Move over Mr. Dressup and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, there's a new wardrobe maven in town.
Sworn in following a byelection a year ago, Toronto's mayor has taken the same ceremonial approach to the job as her predecessor John Tory, in that she is available to attend the opening of almost anything, including - jokingly - an envelope.
But she does it in style, and seems to revel in it.
So do the people she parties with. This is not a bad thing or something to mock. It's a good thing and something to compliment - as long as you are doing the rest of your job well.
Torontonians will have their chance to judge Chow's performance in the October 2026 municipal election. For now, though, she doesn't just wear the chains of office but attire from many other parts of the world as well.
If you look through her social media - or what we have compiled for you here - you will notice everybody is smiling and happy.
Her social snaps appear endless.
Members of different communities who invite her to their special events get a kick out of a mayor who does not mind hamming it up and partaking in the fun.
Speaking of fun, that is what Premier Doug Ford was channelling with his latest X posting, in which he congratulated Chow "as she marks her one year since her election as Toronto's 66th mayor."
What did he get her? Well, a Don Bosco Eagles football jacket like the one his brother, late mayor Rob Ford, used to wear proudly around town.
The gold and green always stood out, and that team had a brand like no other. Premier Ford arranged to have the number 66 and her name put on it, recognizing Chow as the 66th Toronto mayor. Tory, who she replaced in a June 26, 2023 byelection, was Toronto's 65th mayor, while Rob Ford took his place in the city's history as the 64th.
This is all very interesting because before Toronto voted to embark on Route 66 with Chow, the premier loudly warned city residents against casting ballots for her. He said her election would be an "unmitigated disaster."
The premier has since clearly changed his tune. A lot of that goodwill goes back to Chow and her council supporters agreeing to rename the football stadium at Centennial Park in Etobicoke as Rob Ford Stadium.
Now, before the critics start jumping on the mayor for crime or traffic problems - or the premier for the Ontario Science Centre or Greenbelt issues - it's got to be said that celebrating the lighter, human side of things is part of the job, too. If every story and every comment is negative or through a political lens, then there is no spirit or happiness.
With so many divisive remarks about people they don't agree with, this is something Trudeau and Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland have forgotten. It's not us and them. It's just us.
For the most part, Chow has taken on that vibe and like Tory before her is very good out in public. She wants to be a mayor for everybody and even walk a mile in their clothes.
As mayor, she dresses for the part - whatever part they offer her. On X, Chow told Ford she loves the jacket and "can't wait to try it on." The civic leader will add the jacket to her growing list of costumes that she's worn - not just at Halloween, but every day.
On social media, it's not hard to find a year's worth of Chow in a different ethnic, religious, business or sports attire. If she's not wearing a costume, she's posing for a picture with people who are.
Now, there is a lot to criticize Chow for in her first year in office: a 9.5% tax hike, the Sankofa Square fiasco or her attending just about everything in town but the largest ever Walk for Israel event. However, when it comes to celebrating Toronto, its diverse communities and diving in to join in the fun, Chow is always game.
She can gussy it up with the best of them.
In recent times, she has got into full dress to celebrate a truck pull, a running race, cycling events, Eid and Ramadan, and LGBTQ events. And don't forget that she posed with C-3PO in Hollywood, went swimming in a city pool and appeared on social media in a Maple Leafs sweater.
Whether it's celebrating holidays or events marked by the Dutch, Chinese, Tamils, Sikhs, Hindus or the Indigenous community, Chow appears willing to dress the part. The mayor's job in 2024 seems to now be one big photo op where you dress up, play a part and try to make people happy.
Chow may be the best of any of Toronto's mayors when it comes to the art of playing pretend. As they say, when it comes to celebrating Toronto, she wears it well, and she wears it often. jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Mayors; Prime ministers; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

People: Ford, Rob

Former Don Bosco coach, Mayor Rob Ford takes in action between Don Bosco and Cardinal Newman in 2013.
