Document 362 of 500

'I'm misunderstood'; Controversial U.K.'activist'arrives in Toronto after border delay

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/im-misunderstood-controversial-u-k/docview/3073824335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+misunderstood%27%3B+Controversial+U.K.%27activist%27arrives+in+Toronto+after+border+delay&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tommy Robinson has arrived in Toronto.
Turns out the controversial British figure, whom critics consider a far-right activist, is welcome in Canada and allowed to travel freely within the country after all.
The Canada Border Services Agency - after arresting him Monday, taking away his passport and, after a short time in custody, releasing him with conditions to stay within Calgary's city limits - has reversed its decision and is permitting Robinson to complete the rest of his speaking tour.
His first stop is Toronto, where he's to speak Sunday and also appear for a taping of Jordan Peterson and his wife Tammy's podcast.
"I don't know why they did what they did, but it all worked out in the end," Robinson said Saturday at Pearson Airport. "It's great to be in Toronto. I have always wanted to come here. I was surprised I was delayed, but grateful it worked out."
Robinson, who has criminal convictions in his past for assault, mortgage fraud and attempting to gain entry to the United States using his friend's passport, stepped off a plane Saturday afternoon after getting the news he can continue his visit to Canada.
He was given his passport back and, while the CBSA said it doesn't comment on individual cases, it has been confirmed Robinson has a flight booked to return to England once his Toronto commitments have been met.
"I'm unbelievably happy. We won," Robinson reported on X, adding: "I am now able to go wherever I want as an honorary Canadian."
Born as Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, the 41-year-old co-founder of the English Defence League who has been widely criticized by Muslim groups in Canada and around the world, didn't hold back on giving Prime Minister Justin Trudeau a verbal shot.
"To all my detractors, my haters and supporters of Castro's illegitimate man baby, have a wonderful day. I will," Robinson wrote on X. Ouch.
This kind of stuff gets under the skin of those administering today's woke society, in which words are considered violence by some, and differing opinions thought of as blasphemy.
Certainly, Premier Doug Ford didn't appreciate his MP Goldie Ghamari meeting with Robinson over their shared view of human right's violations in Iran and at the urging of the National Council of Canadian Muslims, who said there is no room for "hate or Islamophobia" in Ontario, removed her from caucus.
Everybody has a boss and Ford is entitled to make this decision based on what he feels is best for his party.
Robinson, however, is his own boss and entitled to hold his own views and express them as long as they don't cross over into hate speech as defined by the criminal code.
Robinson hasn't been charged with any crimes in Canada. What he has shown is that in Canada, no matter how it might be offensive to some, every person has free speech.
Sometimes, however, you have to fight for it.
Rebel Media's Ezra Levant, who organized some of Robinson's speaking events during the tour, hired top Calgary immigration lawyers Michael Greene and Navi Dhaliwal to do battle on the grounds that this man's views or opinions aren't enough to restrict his travel.
Eventually, the lawyers were able to convince the CBSA, and Robinson was given back his passport. And although he ended up missing his planned stop in Edmonton, he was permitted to fly to Toronto.
"I was surprised, but I am pleased. It's like David beating Goliath," said Levant, who, like Robinson, said the Calgary officials were very respectful and merely following orders coming from their superiors in Ottawa.
What this does show is Canada still has due process, which is vital to a free society.
Robinson is a character and not everybody's cup of tea, but with thousands of illegals crossing the border into Canada every year not getting the same scrutiny, it's important to ensure there are no double standards or twotiered enforcement going on.
Robinson, who says his past criminal convictions are the result of a witch hunt, told the Toronto Sun he's peaceful, respectful and was never intending to cause any trouble in Canada - he was only here to do his speaking commitments, meet some great fellow activists who've done battle with the government like Tamara Lich, Chris Barber and Peterson, see the "beautiful country" and meet "wonderful Canadians."
"I do think I am misunderstood," Robinson said at the airport. "We should be free to express our opinion on crime as a result of mass migration or any issue we want to."
It was good to see them loosen the restrictions. Holding back someone from moving around freely, blocking them from speaking or censoring their thoughts only draws more attention to the situation.
So credit has to be given to the CBSA, which made a mistake originally in overreacting, but got it right in the end by finding a reasonable compromise.
People are free to disagree with Robinson, but that doesn't mean he loses his freedom of speech or movement.
Interesting, because while Robinson's arrest was a big story in the United Kingdom and in Canada, the new developments of the CBSA decision being reversed has so far not been.
"Zero media outlets have picked up on that news," Robinson commented on his X account.
Well, not zero. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Convictions; Speaking; Airports

Business indexing term: Subject: Airports

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada; United Kingdom--UK

LCBO workers could be on strike next week, stores would close for 14 days

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-workers-could-be-on-strike-next-week-stores/docview/3073621373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+could+be+on+strike+next+week%2C+stores+would+close+for+14+days&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Workers at LCBO stores across the province could potentially be on strike as early as next week.
While talks between the liquor retailer and Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) continue on a new contract, approximately 10,000 employees will be in a legal strike position as of 12:01 a.m. on July 5.
In mid-June, union members voted 97% in favour of a strike.
"LCBO's focus remains on achieving a deal that is fair to bargaining unit employees and helps the LCBO continue to operate effectively and efficiently for Ontario in a new marketplace," the alcohol retailer said in a statement Thursday
"We also have an obligation to be ready for any potential strike action and remain committed to providing the best service we can during this time."
However, the LCBO says it will not be business as usual in the event of a strike.
If union workers walk off the job, the LCBO says it will close all retail locations for 14 days as it prepares to transition to a new operations model.
Following the closure, the LCBO says it will reopen all locations on July 19 with limited hours and a reduced operating week - Friday, Saturday and Sunday.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The union says it has presented proposals at the bargaining table that would modernize alcohol sales, increase convenience, protect good jobs, and allow the province to keep investing the LCBO's revenues.
"LCBO workers don't want a dry summer, we love how busy it is and being a part of Ontarians' celebrations," said OPSEU Liquor Board Employees Division Bargaining Team Chair Colleen MacLeod recently.
"But we can't stand by while Doug Ford gives away the LCBO's revenues to big box and convenience chain CEOs. It's on Premier Ford and the LCBO to make sure that public services and good jobs don't get left behind."
Earlier this week, the LCBO extended store hours across the province in anticipation of a strike and suggested customers shop early and stock up on their products ahead of a potential strike.
GOLDBERG: On alcohol, it's time to truly treat Ontarians as adults
DRY SUMMER? LCBO workers can legally go on strike July 5
TIME TO STOCK UP? LCBO workers vote to back strike if needed, union says

Document 364 of 500

KHAN: Bigotry increases fears of violence amongst Canadian Muslims

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/khan-bigotry-increases-fears-violence-amongst/docview/3073574473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=KHAN%3A+Bigotry+increases+fears+of+violence+amongst+Canadian+Muslims&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Canada's two million Muslims marked Eid ul-Adha, an annual celebration of devotion to God that coincides with the final day of the Hajj, a pilgrimage to the House of Abraham in Mecca. Unfortunately, celebrations this year were tempered by several high-profile instances of anti-Muslim bigotry on Canadian streets.
On June 17, police in Hamilton announced that they had charged a 54-year-old man with criminal harassment after he allegedly entered a local Islamic school, desecrated a copy of the Quran and made bigoted statements in front of students and teachers. Just a few days later, a truck registered to Rebel News Network was spotted driving through Toronto displaying anti-Muslim messages and images.
This was on top of a string of recent hateful attacks against Canadian Muslims that have included an arson attempt in London and assaults on visibly Muslim women in Toronto, Ottawa and Halifax.
Let's not forget that it's been just over seven years since six worshippers were killed and five others seriously wounded in an attack carried out at the Islamic Cultural Centre of Quebec City. Four years ago this month, a family of Pakistani Muslim heritage, just like mine, was run down by a pickup truck in what was London, Ontario's deadliest mass killing ever.
Both incidents have been described by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as acts of terror carried out on Canadian soil.
While violent attacks like those mentioned above have rightly drawn public condemnation from political leaders across the partisan divide, there remains too much tolerance for anti-Muslim prejudice. A 2023 Senate report revealed that Canada leads the G7 in terms of targeted killings of Muslims motivated by Islamophobia.
To their credit, Premier Doug Ford, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie all opposed the recent decision by the Speaker of Ontario's legislature to ban the wearing of keffiyehs within the legislative precinct. Yet many MPPs, political activists, and media commentators across the ideological spectrum felt that it was perfectly fine to ban an article of clothing associated with Arab and Muslim culture in a province whose population, according to Statistics Canada, is 7% Muslim, outnumbering Franco-Ontarians.
Recent months have also seen a shocking increase in anti-Semitic incidents, including acts of violence against synagogues and Jewish schools.
Like Muslims, Canadian Jews deserve to feel safe in their places of worship, schools and communities. It is incumbent upon all Canadians to denounce this and the perpetrators of such illegal acts must be held to account by law enforcement. Still, some commentators have implied collective blame for such incidents at the feet of Canada's growing Muslim community, with some advocating for revisiting Canada's official multiculturalism policy, as a result.
While perhaps not intentional, this rhetoric causes extra concern to Canada's Muslims as it mirrors calls to curb or ban Muslim immigration by an ascendant far right in Europe and the United States.
Like it or not, Canadian Muslims are here to stay.
We contribute just as much to Canadian life as anyone else, with numerous community members active in politics, journalism, entertainment, literature, and sports.
Like all communities, Canada's Muslims are not a monolith. While some are religiously devout and others live a more secular lifestyle, one thing that unites most is a persistent fear of being targeted by hate-based violence simply because of who they are.
Given recent history, and the growing normalization of anti-Muslim rhetoric, this fear is more than justified.
- Omar Khan is a Canadian Muslim political strategist with over two decades of experience advising federal, provincial, and municipal politicians.

Subject: Violence; Bans; Political activism; Muslims; Massacres

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: earlier; month; million; muslims; marked; annual

Document 365 of 500

No stalking horses; Fellow Grits distancing themselves from Trudeau

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-stalking-horses-fellow-grits-distancing/docview/3073481641/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+stalking+horses%3B+Fellow+Grits+distancing+themselves+from+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's Liberal brand is so toxic now that the leader of Ontario's Liberal Party is pretending not to know the federal leader.
Bonnie Crombie may as well have said, "Justin? Justin who?" when asked about Trudeau this week.
During an interview with Global News, Crombie was asked about Trudeau's impact on her efforts to turn around provincial Liberal fortunes. Crombie stressed she was a different type of Liberal, a more centrist one.
She also denied the claim made in Ontario Progressive Conservative Party attack ads against her that she's a carbon taxloving friend of Trudeau.
"I think the bigger friend is Doug Ford, is the closer friend of Justin Trudeau. You see them in photographs and in meetings together," Crombie said.
"I probably speak to the prime minister less than once a year."
Crombie sat in the same Liberal caucus as Trudeau from late 2008 until the spring of 2011. She worked with him closely over Trudeau's time in office while she was mayor of Mississauga.
The comments look awkward.
They make Crombie look like she's making a rookie mistake when she's far from a rookie. But still, bad as the comments are for Crombie, they are far worse for Trudeau.
For her to now distance herself from him is telling on how much of an anchor Trudeau has become for the Liberal brand, provincially and federally, in Ontario.
Monday's byelection is still sending shock waves through the Liberal Party. That the Conservatives could win what was considered a safe Liberal seat in the heart of Toronto means other Liberal seats in the Fortress Toronto, and elsewhere in the province, are vulnerable.
At this point, the Conservatives could easily be picking up seats in the Ottawa region such as Kanata, Nepean or Orleans. In southern Ontario, they could pick up seats from the Liberals in Hamilton, Kitchener, Ajax, Pickering, London, Cambridge and more.
That's on top of the more than 10 seats that would be in play in Toronto proper.
In the last federal election, Trudeau's Liberals took 78 seats in Ontario with 39% of the popular vote across the province. In the latest Leger poll for Postmedia, the Poilievre Conservatives were getting the support of 42% of voters. That's similar to what Ontario Premier Doug Ford got in the 2022 provincial election and his party took 83 seats.
Essentially, if you are a Liberal MP in Ontario, then you need to be worried about keeping your job.
Perhaps that's why some, like Health Minister Mark Holland, are lashing out, making wild claims about people gargling with gasoline and saying byelection votes don't matter. If the Conservatives keep their momentum, Holland is likely to lose his seat again, just like he did in 2011.
The Trudeau Liberals are out of ideas and out of gas. They have also worn out their welcome with Canadians.
Darrell Bricker, global CEO of public affairs at Ipsos, the world's largest polling firm, said after the byelection that the big problem for Trudeau is he's fighting against his toughest opponent yet - the desire for change.
"A lot of people are saying don't underestimate Trudeau, that's what people did when he boxed Patrick Brazeau and won," Bricker said during a chat Wednesday. "Well, Trudeau isn't fighting Brazeau, he's fighting the desire for change and that's like fighting Mike Tyson."
There's been plenty of speculation over whether Trudeau stays or goes after months of bad polling and the disastrous byelection result. Another question to ask is how many Liberal MPs, including cabinet ministers like Holland, start looking elsewhere.
Expect a lot of people in the Liberal caucus to find new employment over the summer, to announce that they will try new ventures or spend more time with their families. It will be their way of putting distance between themselves and Trudeau, much like Bonnie Crombie is trying to do. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

People: Trudeau, Justin

Ontario funding program to provide 'critical' mental-health support to cops

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-funding-program-provide-critical-mental/docview/3073307058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+funding+program+to+provide+%27critical%27+mental-health+support+to+cops&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police across the province will soon have more mental-health support available to them thanks to an "innovative" project bankrolled by Ontario's Skills Development Fund.
The provincial government announced it had earmarked $2.7 million to train about 160 peer support workers through the SDF's training stream to provide better mental-health support to the thousands of officers across Ontario.
The best ideas come from those willing to take a bold step forward, working with a willing partner in Government. @PeelPolice & @ChiefNish have always been a step ahead!
Our SDF program is ensuring the best mental health wrap around supports for front line officers!
- David Piccini (@DavidPiccini) June 27, 2024
Premier Doug Ford said the funding will provide "critical mental-health support for our men and women in uniform to help them face the significant challenges and occupational stress that come with the job.
"The brave men and women in our police services put their lives on the line to keep Ontario communities safe and our government is proud to support them," Ford said.
The announcement came less than a week after the first Ontario Police Suicide Memorial was held at Queen's Park, where those who have died "because of the line of duty" were honoured.
Get A-Head Inc., a company that helps create efficiencies in the care process through secure text, audio and video sessions, is leading the project, which will introduce a digital platform to help peer support workers provide mental-health care to police service employees and their families.
'Enduring legacy' of officers who died by suicide celebrated at memorial
Police suicide memorial to be unveiled, ensuring dead are 'never forgotten'
The platform will use AI and other "cutting-edge technology" to "make it easier for peer support workers to identify colleagues in distress and deliver care better tailored to their needs.
"Our police officers put their lives on the line daily to serve and protect the people of Ontario," Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development Minister David Piccini said. "We know this can lead to trauma and mental-health challenges for these heroes.
"By investing in training and upskilling for police peer support workers, we are helping to make sure they have the latest tools to provide the best mental-health support to our first responders."
Great having Premier @fordnation & Minister @DavidPiccini visit @PeelPolice #Wellness Bureau. Opportunity to see/discuss our innovative resources for members. Vital investment by @ONgov into the development of digital platform for mental health supports.
- Chief Nishan Duraiappah (@ChiefNish) June 27, 2024
Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah applauded the announcement, saying it will better support the more than 2,000 officers under his command.
"We are pleased to participate in initiatives that support our members' mental health and well-being and to contribute to the work that Keel Digital Solution Mind and Get A-Head are doing," he said.

Document 367 of 500

Pride festival takes over Toronto and other events to see and do this weekend

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pride-festival-takes-over-toronto-other-events/docview/3073262940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pride+festival+takes+over+Toronto+and+other+events+to+see+and+do+this+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The weather for the first long weekend of the summer in the city is expected to be a little more comfortable and absolutely gorgeous.
Apart from some rain expected on Saturday, there will be no extreme heat as temperatures will be at or slightly below seasonal averages ahead of the Canada Day holiday on Monday.
And what better way to enjoy the outdoors with a variety of events happening in the city and across the Greater Toronto Area.
Pride Toronto Festival
Pride Toronto is celebrating Pride Month in the city as the Dyke March, Pride Parade, Streetfair and the Trans Pride festival will be featured this weekend.
Local and international artists, as well as DJs, will be performing throughout the heart of the gay village. There will be programming for youth and families as well.
Beginning Friday morning, Church St. will be closed to traffic from north of Dundas St. E. to south of Hayden St. Wellesley St. E. will also be closed from Yonge St. to Jarvis St.
For more information about all the events, click here
RECOMMENDED VIDEO Green Space Festival
The queer party event continues at Barbara Hall Park this weekend, which is the scene of music, food, drinks and extraordinary performers in the heart of city's gay village as part of Pride month.
The free event will also feature world-class DJs, legendary music producers and drag royalty.
For entertainment lineups, click here
Lavazza Inclucity Film Festival
The Distillery Historic District will transform into an open-air cinema featuring international films representing Canada's diverse and cultural mosaic along with special guest appearances.
The film and street festival runs from June 27 to July 21. Reservations and advance tickets are required to attend the screenings.
For more information, click here
Neil Young and Crazy Horse cancel tour due to illness
Architecture firm behind Ontario Science Centre calls closure 'political move'
Harbourfront Centre
The heart of the city's waterfront will kick off the summer with a long weekend lineup of free and ticketed events.
Outdoor concerts featuring Tyler Shaw, Fefe Dobson and Choir! Choir! Choir! tops the list of music, dance, comedy, food and family-friendly activities.
An evening with the Toronto Symphony Orchestra and award-winning comic Martha Chaves is also on tap.
For reservations and tickets, click here
Rhythms of Canada Festival
Three days of delicious food and activities will be featured at the Aga Khan Museum for the free Rhythms of Canada Festival on Saturday.
Special programming such as tote bag design and clay tile-making workshops, musical performances and the museum's world-class art collection from across the Muslim world will also entertain and inspire visitors of all ages.
The event will run from noon to 8 p.m.
For more information, click here
Taste of Somalia
Nathan Phillips Square will host the first-ever Taste of Somalia Summer Festival on Friday and Saturday.
Toronto, home to one of the largest Somali communities in the world, will get a taste of the country's food, music, heritage and culture.
Emerging local talents and some of today's leading performers in the Somali community will be featured.
The event is free to attend. For more information, click here
'Taste the World' Food Tour
The eclectic Kensington Market neighbourhood will be the scene of a guided tour where you can take a bite out of ethnic eats from places like Tibet and Jamaica.
The tour, which begins in the morning, will also feature insider knowledge of quirky mom-and-pop shops and eye-catching graffiti.
To book your spot, click here
Canada's Wonderland
The amusement park north of the city will kick off its Celebration Canada event on Friday with authentic food like lobster rolls, smoked pork ribs and Nanaimo bar milkshakes.
There will also be cultural performances by the Smoke Trail Singers, live music, kid-friendly axe throwing and other activities.
Also on Sunday, the park will host a night of fireworks ahead of the Canada Day holiday.
For tickets and more information, click here
The Big Bounce Canada Tour
The biggest touring inflatable event in the world is coming to Hamilton this weekend.
Hanes Corn Maze in Dundas is hosting seven massive inflatable attractions including the newly expanded 24,000 sq. ft. World's Largest Bounce House and the new deep sea foam party inflatable OctoBlast.
The ticketed even will run from Saturday to Sunday.
To book your visit, click here
Whitchurch-Stouffville Strawberry Festival
Berry season is in full swing north of Toronto.
Whitchurch-Stouffville will host the annual strawberry festival this weekend where thousands are expected to enjoy a pancake breakfast, festival market and live entertainment.
The free event, which takes place in Memorial Park in the heart of at Stouffville, begins on Friday and ends Sunday.
For all the details, click here

Document 368 of 500

LILLEY: Fellow Liberals distancing themselves from Trudeau

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-fellow-liberals-distancing-themselves/docview/3073204510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Fellow+Liberals+distancing+themselves+from+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's Liberal brand is so toxic now that the leader of Ontario's Liberal Party is pretending not to know the federal leader.
Bonnie Crombie may as well have said, "Justin? Justin who?" when asked about Trudeau earlier this week.
During an interview with Global News, Crombie was asked about Trudeau's impact on her efforts to turn around provincial Liberal fortunes. Crombie stressed that she was a different type of Liberal, a more centrist one.
She also denied the claim made in Ontario Progressive Conservative Party attack ads against her that she's a carbon tax-loving friend of Trudeau.
"I think the bigger friend is Doug Ford, is the closer friend of Justin Trudeau. You see them in photographs and in meetings together," Crombie said
"I probably speak to the prime minister less than once a year."
Crombie sat in the same Liberal caucus as Trudeau from late 2008 until the spring of 2011. She worked with him closely over Trudeau's time in office while she was mayor of Mississauga.
The comments look awkward, they make Crombie look like she's making a rookie mistake when she's far from a rookie. But still, bad as the comments are for Crombie, they are far worse for Trudeau.
For her to now distance herself from him is telling on how much of an anchor Trudeau has become for the Liberal brand - provincially and federally - in Ontario.
Monday's byelection is still sending shockwaves through the Liberal Party. That the Conservatives could win what was considered a safe Liberal seat in the heart of Toronto means other Liberal seats in the Fortress Toronto, and elsewhere in the province, are vulnerable.
At this point, the Conservatives could easily be picking up seats in the Ottawa region such as Kanata, Nepean or Orleans. In Southern Ontario, they could pick up seats from the Liberals in Hamilton, Kitchener, Ajax, Pickering, London, Cambridge and more.
That's on top of the more than 10 seats that would be in play in Toronto proper.
In the last federal election, Trudeau's Liberals took 78 seats in Ontario with 39% of the popular vote across the province. In the latest Leger poll for Postmedia, the Poilievre Conservatives were getting the support of 42% of voters.
That's similar to what Ontario Premier Doug Ford received in the 2022 provincial election and his party took 83 seats.
Essentially, if you are a Liberal MP in Ontario then you need to be worried about keeping your job.
Perhaps that's why some, like Health Minister Mark Holland, are lashing out, making wild claims about people gargling with gasoline and saying that byelection votes don't matter. If the Conservatives keep their momentum, Holland is likely to lose his seat again, just like he did in 2011.
The Trudeau Liberals are out of ideas and out of gas. They have also worn out their welcome with Canadians.
Darrell Bricker, Global CEO of Public Affairs at Ipsos, the world's largest polling firm, said after the byelection that the big problem for Trudeau is he's fighting against his toughest opponent yet - the desire for change.
"A lot of people are saying don't underestimate Trudeau, that's what people did when he boxed Patrick Brazeau and won," Bricker said during a chat Wednesday. "Well, Trudeau isn't fighting Brazeau, he's fighting the desire for change and that's like fighting Mike Tyson."
There's been plenty of speculation over whether Trudeau stays or goes after months of bad polling and the disastrous byelection result. Another question to ask is how many Liberal MPs, including cabinet ministers like Holland, start looking elsewhere.
Expect a lot of people in the Liberal caucus to find new employment over the summer, to announce that they will try new ventures or spend more time with their families. It will be their way of putting distance between themselves and Trudeau, much like Bonnie Crombie is trying to do.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Negative campaigning

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 369 of 500

Drilling down on feds'flawed dental plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/drilling-down-on-fedsflawed-dental-plan/docview/3072970186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drilling+down+on+feds%27flawed+dental+plan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the Liberals and Justin Trudeau do a post-mortem of why they lost a rock-solid Liberal Toronto seat in a byelection this week, they should look no further than their arrogant response to criticism about their dental program.
The much-ballyhooed plan, which helps some seniors and is supposed to expand to include children and eligible adults with disabilities, hit a roadblock recently. Several groups representing dentists raised concerns about how the $13-billion program works. They have questions about the agreement, as well as concerns about patient privacy.
Asked about these quite reasonable matters, Health Minister Mark Holland dismissed them as bullying from the Conservative party.
"If you're a Conservative party and you want to run against taking dental care away, fine. But calling around and bullying and creating all this negativity, you know, I think that's what the underlying force is," Holland told a news conference last week.
Dentists have also expressed concern about the administrative costs of implementing the program. Meanwhile, presidents of several organizations representing dentists have categorically rejected the suggestion they were leaned on by the Conservatives.
This is par for the course for the Trudeau government. It introduces costly and impractical programs without thinking them through. It raises expectations for those people who believe they'll be helped by the program, then blames others when it can't deliver.
Pushed by the NDP, Liberals dangled the $10-a-day daycare carrot to hard-pressed parents. The devil is always in the details. In 2022, the federal Office of the Parliamentary Budget Officer estimated the plan would fall $1.1 billion short of the government's 2021 budget commitment to provinces over the life of the agreement.
Ontario's Financial Accountability Office, which provides independent analysis of provincial finances, estimated that by the end of the current five-year agreement, about 25% of children under six whose families seek $10-a-day spaces will be left without a spot.
The government pushed ahead anyway.
Ditto with the school lunch program, which crossed into provincial jurisdictions and duplicated programs already in place. As always, Liberals point the blame at anyone but themselves when they run into problems.
It's time for them to own their mistakes and start behaving like a mature government - for a change.

Subject: Dental insurance; Dentists; School lunches; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Dentists

Identifier / keyword: liberals; justin; trudeau; liberal; toronto; byelection

Document 371 of 500

LILLEY: Trudeau shows no sign of leaving after high-profile byelection loss

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-shows-no-sign-leaving-after-high/docview/3072324808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+shows+no+sign+of+leaving+after+high-profile+byelection+loss&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau stood before the cameras on Tuesday and said that the byelection result in Toronto-St. Paul's wasn't the result he wanted. Given that he lost a riding that had been safely in Liberal hands, that would be the political understatement of the year.
The loss on Monday - or early Tuesday morning, given how long the counting took - is a cataclysmic event for the Liberals, whether Trudeau wants to admit it or not. Given his statement, he clearly didn't.
"These are not easy times. And it's clear that I and my entire Liberal team have much more work to do to deliver tangible, real progress that Canadians across the country can see and feel," Trudeau said.
"My focus is on your success and that's where it's going to stay."
After losing St. Paul's and given the state of national and regional polling for the Liberals, Trudeau might want to focus on his own success, not ours.
Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland addresses the media during an announcement at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario office in downtown Toronto on Tuesday, June 25, 2024.
The prime minister didn't take any questions on Tuesday as he made his statement and participated in a government announcement, but breaking with usual protocol didn't interact with the media. The job of answering questions about the byelection fell to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, who was at an announcement in Toronto.
"Yes, he certainly can," Freeland said when asked whether Trudeau could stay on as leader.
"Can you explain why?" The Globe and Mail reporter Marieke Walsh asked, "because everyone we're hearing from behind the scenes believes that the result last (Monday) night means catastrophic losses across the country. If you cannot win in Toronto under Justin Trudeau, why should anyone believe you can win anywhere else under him?"
"Our government is focused on working hard for Canada and Canadians," Freeland said.
'I HEAR YOUR CONCERNS': Trudeau reflects on devastating byelection loss
LILLEY: Conservatives' shock win in Toronto-St. Paul's adds pressure to Trudeau to step down
That was the first indication that the deputy prime minister was not about to give an honest answer with even a moment of self-reflection. She went on to say that Trudeau would lead the party into the next election and has the support of the country.
Freeland's words and body language didn't exactly match up; while her words claimed confidence in Trudeau, neither her voice nor her posture did.
Just about every Liberal MP in Toronto should be worried at this point. While not all could lose to the Conservatives, there is also the NDP to worry about. It's worth noting that the Conservatives won Toronto-St. Paul's when even in the 2011 federal election, as the NDP swept much of the core of the city and the Conservatives ate at the outside by winning several suburban seats, the Liberals held St. Paul's.
If they are losing Toronto-St. Paul's, if the Conservatives are getting 42% of the vote in a "safe" Liberal riding, then which seat in Toronto is actually safe for a Liberal these days.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Any of the three Liberals holding the Etobicoke ridings - James Maloney, Yvan Baker, Kristy Duncan - has to worry that they are now in danger of losing. In York Centre, Willowdale, Don Valley North and across Scarborough, Liberal MPs and cabinet ministers are worried.
It's not just Toronto, Liberals are concerned in Ottawa, Kitchener, Hamilton, Winnipeg, Calgary, Vancouver and across Atlantic Canada.
After watching Leslie Church, a candidate with impeccable credentials and deep party roots, lose you have to be wondering who will be next. Expect a rash of candidates to decide they need to pursue other opportunities or spend more time with their families this summer.
Trudeau himself may be among them.
I've been doubtful that Trudeau will leave. He has seemed to be reinvigorated since Pierre Poilievre won the Conservative leadership and desperately wants to do battle with him. Trudeau also seems to have a messianic complex about saving Canada from the "cold," "cruel," and "small" Conservatives and the plague of misinformation and disinformation.
That said, when you get a political earthquake of this magnitude, it's bound to make people think.
Perhaps Trudeau will take time over the summer to reflect and question whether he should leave. My money is still on his viewing himself as the best chance to save Canada by leading the Liberals into the 2025 election.

Subject: Worry; Prime ministers; Elections

Location: Canada

Document 372 of 500

Summer fun at the bluffs?; Speculation about election date has opposition running hot

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/summer-fun-at-bluffs-speculation-about-election/docview/3071672340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Summer+fun+at+the+bluffs%3F%3B+Speculation+about+election+date+has+opposition+running+hot&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First it was the Liberals announcing several members of their election campaign team on Wednesday. On Saturday, the NDP were announcing their campaign team for the next election.
The thing is, the next election isn't until June 2026 - that is, of course, unless Premier Doug Ford calls an early election, which he hasn't committed to doing.
Of course, Ford hasn't ruled out the early election call, which started as media speculation a few weeks ago.
Since then, the rumours have been fast and furious with claims Ford will call an election next spring ahead of the October 2025 federal vote, that we will have an October campaign this year, that it will happen in August, or even that - as one person was assuring me this week - he'll call it in two weeks.
Ford is loving all this speculation because he knows it is driving the NDP and Liberals crazy.
"Nominations take a lot of party resources," one Liberal said while noting that in the last campaign the party had more than a dozen candidates who weren't properly vetted.
That saw the Liberals having to drop three candidates in the middle of the election campaign. It's not that they didn't know the vote was coming in June 2022, but the party was broke and, quite frankly, the infrastructure of the Ontario Liberal Party is a shadow of its former self.
While Ford might simply be stirring the pot with early election speculation, the opposition parties can't assume he won't go early. Thus, the moves to ensure election readiness this past week.
Bonnie Crombie has requested the Ontario Liberal Party declare electoral urgency and speed up the process of nominating candidates in all 124 ridings. On Thursday, Ottawa South MPP John Fraser was the first to be nominated, with the Liberal party introducing him on social media as "Bonnie Crombie's candidate for Ottawa South."
It's off to see someone like Fraser, an MPP for more than a decade, described that way, but it appears the Liberals will be branding around Crombie rather than the party identity.
Tom Allison, who ran Crombie's successful leadership bid, has been named campaign chairman while Chad Walsh and Gen Tomney have been named campaign co-chairs. Both Walsh and Tomney have extensive political experience including stints in the premier's office under Kathleen Wynne.
Crombie may be under the gun to find and nominate candidates, but she is building a team of respected operatives to plan and execute the campaign whenever the election comes.
Over at the NDP, leader Marit Stiles announced Dave Clark will take on the role of campaign director. Clark is a campaign veteran, having worked on campaigns across the country and in the U.K. as well, and recently worked with the City of Toronto on their vaccination campaign during the pandemic.
Stiles also announced Brian Topp will chair the party's election planning committee. Topp was key to the NDP's election win in Manitoba last fall, working closely with Wab Kinew, and has worked on campaigns across the country.
The party that hasn't announced a campaign team yet is Doug Ford's, though the PCs will likely see key names from the past like Kory Teneycke and Michael Diamond take key positions.
It's still my contention that there is no plan to call an early election, though some around Ford clearly like the idea. What Ford has done by revelling in the speculation is he's partly panicked his NDP and Liberal opposition, but he's also focused them.
Whether the election comes next spring or in 2026, it looks like these parties will be ready. That will make the campaign that much more interesting.
"Let's go," Stiles said when speaking to reporters.
She's telling everyone who will listen that she'll take an early election any time.
Time will tell if Ford does call early or was simply bluffing. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations; Candidates; Elections

Document 373 of 500

Will St. Paul's skip Church?; Byelection may serve as a message

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-st-pauls-skip-church-byelection-may-serve-as/docview/3071672313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+St.+Paul%27s+skip+Church%3F%3B+Byelection+may+serve+as+a+message&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Election days aren't normally filled with nerves for Liberals in Toronto-St. Paul's. The party normally wins handily in this part of Toronto and has for more than 30 years.
Monday will be a very different, nervous day for Leslie Church and her campaign team.
Church, the former chief of staff to finance minister Chrystia Freeland, is likely to squeeze out a win, but even she and her team know that Don Stewart, the Conservative candidate, could play spoiler - not that Stewart, his team or Conservative headquarters in Ottawa are playing up the idea they are suddenly going to take this riding for the first time since 1988.
Every campaign, it seems, is playing down expectations.
The final result will depend on which campaign does the best job at getting their vote out. Byelections have notoriously low voter turnout, as the Durham byelection in March saw just 27% of voters cast a ballot, while in Calgary Heritage last year it was 28%.
There are 84,442 voters on the official list, so if just 27% come out to vote, we could see the next MP elected with fewer than 10,000 votes.
It's normally thought that low voter turnout benefits the incumbent or incumbent party, but in this case it could help Stewart and the Conservatives. There are a lot of disaffected Liberals, including many who like Church but not Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, and if they stay home on Monday, it hurts Church's chance of winning.
Stewart, meanwhile, has a motivated voter base.
He needs to tap into the many professionals who live in the riding, like family doctors angry about the capital gains tax changes. Stewart could also benefit from the support of the Jewish community in the riding, centred around the Forest Hill neighbourhood, switching from Liberal to Conservative.
In the final, frenzied few days, Church was latching onto local issues like Premier Doug Ford's decision to abruptly close the Ontario Science Centre over safety concerns due to a faulty roof.
"Tomorrow, there's a clear choice: Vote for someone who will stand up for science, arts and culture and not let Conservatives tear down the spaces and organizations that celebrate Canada and our kids!" Church posted to social media.
She also posted a video saying if the Conservatives win the riding, they will take Canada backward by cutting health care and pensions. It sounded like a message from someone afraid of losing and it also made it sound like the whole government would change hands based on this byelection.
Like a lot of Liberals lately, Church speaks about problems that need to be fixed in this country as if the Trudeau Liberals haven't been in power for nearly nine years.
Stewart was focused more on door knocking and connecting with voters than social media over the weekend. His message has been about stopping the crime wave, which is primarily auto thefts in the riding, dealing with affordability and standing with the Jewish community.
The weather forecast for Monday is warm and sunny with little chance of rain, so weather should not be a factor unless people would rather head to the park than the voting booth.
If Stewart can rally people in the riding to come out, he has a shot.
Let's see if the voters of Toronto-St. Paul's send Trudeau a message that it's time for him to leave or if they tell the Liberals they are happy with the status quo. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 374 of 500

Cops lost to suicide leave 'enduring legacy'

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cops-lost-suicide-leave-enduring-legacy/docview/3071672272/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cops+lost+to+suicide+leave+%27enduring+legacy%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hundreds of officers from across the province gathered at Queen's Park on Sunday for the first Ontario Police Suicide Memorial.
The theme was, "Because of the line of duty," while the event was a celebration of the lives lost.
"The death of any police member is tragic and one we all must mourn," said York Regional Police Chief Jim MacSween, who is also president of the Ontario Association of Chiefs of Police. "A ceremony and memorial to commemorate police members who have died by suicide related to their duties offers us an opportunity to both honour their service and come together to acknowledge the immense sense of loss their families, friends and colleagues feel.
"Policing is an honourable profession. It is also one with many challenges. It is vital that all police members feel supported as they deal with not only the challenges inherent in the job, but also when they are dealing with the challenges of living in today's world. Let us resolve to ensure police members and their families are supported wherever they are on their journey."
The inaugural Ontario Police Suicide Memorial ceremony was a significant occasion, said Mark Baxter, president of the Police Association of Ontario.
"Today, we gather not only to mourn the loss of our fallen comrades who have died because of the line of duty, but also to honour their memory, their sacrifice and the enduring legacy they leave behind," Baxter said. "Each of these heroes represents a life dedicated to serving and protecting others - a noble calling that often demands everything from those who answer."
Premier Doug Ford said he was honoured to be a part of the moment.
"Today, we remember and honour the heroic police members who lost their lives because of the line of duty," Ford said. "Being a police officer is a tough job with long hours, constant threat of danger and relentless exposure to on-the-job trauma."
There have been 51 documented police suicides to date.

Full text: INFORM THE PUBLIC
Canadians need answers regarding the names of MPs who allegedly aided foreign powers. The value of newspapers and their columnists is apparent as they inform readers about a serious issue like the above while asking for transparency from our government. Today's democracies need healthy newspapers to counteract open-face lies from social media outlets, and their editorials that inform us about serious issues as our politicians avoid divulging these potential traitor's names. Peter J. Middlemore Sr.

CASH RICH TORONTO
The renaming of Dundas Square is an excellent example of just how bad our city government is at managing the city's finances. Chow is catering to a minority. Talk about divisive! She cries for financial help from the provincial and federal governments because Toronto is in such poor financial shape. This is obviously a lie because Toronto must be awash in cash if she can afford to spend hundreds of thousands of taxpayers dollars on this stupid, erroneous socialist whim.
R. Fawcett
Toronto
(Chow and her band of leftists on council are only interested in catering to the fringe. They never have, nor will they ever care about the financial health of the city)
TRAFFIC NIGHTMARE
Cucumber slices on your eyes, a bubbling hot tub, a soothing body massage and a warm and fuzzy bathrobe may make one think all is well with the world while visiting the newly proposed spa facility along the waterfront, but it won't take long for reality to set back in when trying to leave the area. Horn honking, single-finger salutes, and stop-and-go vehicular traffic everywhere along the Lake Shore and Gardiner combination will soon raise that blood pressure and erase those tranquil feelings. Does everything have to be jammed along Toronto's waterfront including this proposed new spa and the relocation of the Ontario Science Centre? Traffic isn't stop and go anymore, it's just stopped!
Don Ruttan
Cobourg
(Traffic is hell, there are no credible solutions to address it and it is only going to get worse. Might as well settle in)

Mike Swain
(Freeland is another in a long list of failed Trudeau Liberal cabinet ministers)

'Enduring legacy' of officers who died by suicide celebrated at memorial

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/enduring-legacy-officers-who-died-suicide/docview/3071583460/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Enduring+legacy%27+of+officers+who+died+by+suicide+celebrated+at+memorial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 378 of 500

LILLEY: Doug Ford's election speculation gets opposition ready

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-doug-fords-election-speculation-gets/docview/3071554760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+election+speculation+gets+opposition+ready&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: First it was the Liberals announcing several members of their election campaign team on Wednesday. On Saturday, the NDP were announcing their campaign team for the next election.
The thing is, the next election isn't until June 2026.
That is, of course, unless Premier Doug Ford calls an early election, which he hasn't committed to doing. Of course, Ford hasn't ruled out the early election call, which started as media speculation a few weeks ago.
Since then, the rumours have been fast and furious with claims Ford will call an election next spring ahead of the October 2025 federal vote, that we will have an October campaign this year, that it will happen in August, or even that - as one person was assuring me this week - he'll call it in two weeks.
Ford is loving all this speculation because he knows it is driving the NDP and Liberals crazy.
"Nominations take a lot of party resources," one Liberal said while noting that in the last campaign the party had more than a dozen candidates who weren't properly vetted.
That saw the Liberals having to drop three candidates in the middle of the election campaign. It's not that they didn't know the vote was coming in June 2022, but the party was broke and, quite frankly, the infrastructure of the Ontario Liberal Party is a shadow of its former self.
While Ford might simply be stirring the pot with early election speculation, the opposition parties can't assume he won't go early. Thus, the moves to ensure election readiness this past week.
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks at a roundtable on homelessness and health care at Brockville's Wall Street United Church on Thursday, May 9, 2024. (RONALD ZAJAC/Postmedia)
Bonnie Crombie has requested the Ontario Liberal Party declare electoral urgency and speed up the process of nominating candidates in all 124 ridings. On Thursday, Ottawa South MPP John Fraser was the first to be nominated with the Liberal Party introducing him on social media as "Bonnie Crombie's candidate for Ottawa South."
It's odd to see someone like Fraser, an MPP for more than a decade, described that way but it appears the Liberals will be branding around Crombie rather than the party identity.
Tom Allison, who ran Crombie's successful leadership bid, has been named campaign chair while Chad Walsh and Gen Tomney have been named campaign co-chairs. Both Walsh and Tomney have extensive political experience including stints in the premier's office under Kathleen Wynne.
Crombie may be under the gun to find and nominate candidates, but she is building a team of well-respected operatives to plan and execute the campaign whenever the election comes.
Over at the NDP, Leader Marit Stiles announced that Dave Clark will take on the role of campaign director. Clark is a campaign veteran having worked on campaigns across the country and in the U.K. as well, and recently worked with the City of Toronto on their vaccination campaign during the pandemic.
Stiles also announced that Brian Topp will chair the party's Election Planning Committee. Topp was key to the NDP's election win in Manitoba last fall working closely with Wab Kinew and has worked on campaigns across the country.
The party that hasn't announced a campaign team yet is Doug Ford's, though the PCs will likely see key names from the past like Kory Teneycke and Michael Diamond take key positions.
It's still my contention that there is currently no plan to call an early election, though some around Ford clearly like the idea. What Ford has done by reveling in the speculation is partly panicked his NDP and Liberal opposition, but he's also focused them.
Whether the election comes next spring or in 2026, it looks like these parties will be ready. Which will make the campaign that much more interesting.
"Let's go," said Stiles when speaking to reporters.
She's telling everyone who will listen that she'll take an early election any time.
Time will tell if Ford does call early or was simply bluffing.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations; Leadership; Candidates; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: first; liberals; announcing; several; members; election

Title: LILLEY: Doug Ford's election speculation gets opposition ready

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 379 of 500

Police suicide memorial to be unveiled, ensuring dead are 'never forgotten'

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/police-suicide-memorial-be-unveiled-ensuring-dead/docview/3071391734/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+suicide+memorial+to+be+unveiled%2C+ensuring+dead+are+%27never+forgotten%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Police Suicide Memorial will be unveiled on Sunday at 11 a.m.
Organized by Canada Beyond the Blue, the event will be at the south end of Ontario Police Memorial Park at the corner of Queen's Park Cres. and Grosvenor St. in downtown Toronto.
The memorial pays tribute to Ontario's police officers and civilian police members who have died by suicide. Family and friends of the victims will be in attendance.
The names of those who have died will be read and honoured.
Police members will meet at 10:15 a.m. at the front steps of the Ontario Legislature before marching to the event at about 10:50 a.m.
Dignitaries, including Premier Doug Ford, will join family members and police and will speak at the ceremony.
Remembrance ceremony 'stark reminder' of cost to keep communities safe, police say
WARMINGTON: Dying veteran mistaken as drunk sparked The Last Post Fund
Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw and OPP Commissioner Thomas Carrique will be in attendance.
"Police members are deserving of a formal memorial effort that recognizes the positive impact of their lives and legitimizes the grief of those who carry on their memory," police said in a release. "The relentless exposure to on-the-job trauma can significantly impact the mental health of police members.
"Establishing a distinct memorial not only dignifies their service, but also prompts stakeholders to advocate for comprehensive policies addressing the mental well-being of police members and their families. Canada Beyond the Blue is committed to ensuring these individuals are never forgotten and that their names are spoken aloud and meticulously recorded to honour their service and acknowledge their tragic loss."
For more information on the Ontario Police Suicide Memorial visit the Canada Beyond the Blue website.

Document 380 of 500

'We know what this is all about'; Ford critics set to rally after Ontario Science Centre padlocked

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-know-what-this-is-all-about-ford-critics-set/docview/3071344733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+know+what+this+is+all+about%27%3B+Ford+critics+set+to+rally+after+Ontario+Science+Centre+padlocked&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The group Save Ontario's Science Centre will be holding a rally Sunday after the sudden closure of the eastend attraction.
It will run from noon to 2 p.m. at Wells Hill Park at 145 Hilton Ave. in the Bathurst St. and St. Clair Ave. area.
"Everyone is welcome," the group posted Saturday on X. "No registration!" A news release from the Ontario NDP said that Leader Marit Stiles plans to speak at the event along with culture and heritage critic Jill Andrew and Toronto Centre MPP Kristyn Wong-Tam.
Stiles said on X Saturday she was "furious" about the Ford government's decision to immediately close the facility Friday over concerns that the building isn't structurally sound.
"We know what this is all about - his (Premier Doug Ford's) vanity Ontario Place project," Stiles said. "Join me tomorrow w/@SaveOSC, @JILLSLASTWORD and @kristynwongtam to save the centre!" As of 6:20 p.m. Saturday, more than 27,000 people out of a stated goal of 29,000 had signed a petition on the Save Ontario's Science Centre website in support of the facility, which now has permanent fencing around it to keep people out.
The science centre was suddenly closed Friday after an engineering report found the roof panels were in "distressed, high-risk condition."
The news from provincial infrastructure officials came amid government plans to move the science centre from its current location to a new home at a redeveloped Ontario Place, but that isn't slated to be open until 2028.
Engineering firm Rinkus Consulting Group said fully negating the risk would require replacing roof panels at a cost of between $22 million and $40 million. and that would take two or more years to complete with the facility closed.
"The actions taken today will protect the health and safety of visitors and staff at the Ontario Science Centre while supporting its eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said Friday in a statement.
The Ontario Federation of Labour said that by allowing the building to fall into disrepair, 300 jobs are at risk.
"Despite the centre's financial challenges, experts agree that renovating the current building is both more environmentally and socially responsible and considerably cheaper than constructing a new, smaller facility at Ontario Place," OFL president Laura Walton said in a news release. "The premier's claim that the relocation will increase attendance remains unsupported by any public documentation."
Walton said the Ford government's decision to close the 55-year-old facility is part of a troubling pattern of neglect and short-sightedness.
"Ford's cuts to health care have left hospitals struggling and patients waiting," said Walton. "Our children's education should be a priority, but Ford's actions say otherwise."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow also expressed her dismay about the closure, calling it a "painful loss" for the city.
- With files from Toronto Sun staff and The Canadian Press

Document 383 of 500

Decades of decay to blame; Ford right to close Ontario Science Centre over faulty roof

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/decades-decay-blame-ford-right-close-ontario/docview/3071163725/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Decades+of+decay+to+blame%3B+Ford+right+to+close+Ontario+Science+Centre+over+faulty+roof&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stick a fork in the Ontario Science Centre, it's done.
The province announced the abrupt closure of the facility on Friday. Rather than closing when the new science centre opens at Ontario Place in 2028, the closure was immediate.
The roof isn't safe, the boiler and air conditioning systems are at risk of failure and that's on top of issues like the bridge at the entrance being shut down for at least the last two years due to structural failure. An engineering report on the roof of the 55-year-old facility said that significant rainfall or a major snowfall could result in parts of the roof collapsing.
Reading that report was what forced the Ford government's hand in closing the Ontario Science Centre.
The report from engineering consulting firm Rinkus Consulting Group said "it is our opinion that buildings A, B and C are currently safe for occupancy until Oct. 31, 2024." Beyond that, the firm wasn't sure that the roof would hold under heavy snow.
"A significant snow or rain loading occurrence could exceed the reduced load carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said in a statement that families who have purchased memberships for admission to the science centre or who have paid for summer camps will be reimbursed.
"The actions taken today will protect the health and safety of visitors and staff at the Ontario Science Centre, while supporting its eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility," Surma said in a statement.
The cost of repairing the roof of a facility already slated to close and relocate ranged between $22 million and $40 million. Given the way inflation continues to drive up prices and the inability of government institutions to control costs, you can expect the final figure to be much higher than $40 million.
All for a facility that is at the end of its lifespan and will relocate to Ontario Place in less than five years.
If the facility is in such poor repair that the roof won't be safe over another winter, that the heating and air conditioning aren't working as they should, that the bridge has been shut down for years, then this is something that executives and the science centre board need to answer for. While the opposition at Queen's Park wants to point the finger at Premier Doug Ford, this kind of decay comes from not just years but decades of neglect and clearly those in charge - past and present - deserve the blame.
If organizations like the science centre are supposed to be truly independent, then it is up to the board and the executive ranks to ensure funds are spent in a way that ensures the building is safe. It shouldn't come as a shock to anyone that after decades of neglect, the science centre is closing.
Still, the opposition is blaming Ford for this and implying he's trying to make money off of it.
"We all know the government's real motive is to justify Doug Ford's mega-spa vanity project at Ontario Place," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in a statement.
Moving the science centre has nothing to do with the Therme Spa being built at Ontario Place; Stiles is just upset that a facility she will surely use is being built and she didn't think of it. The opposition from the Ontario Liberals was even worse.
"Doug Ford is ruining kids'summer camps to push his own agenda and benefit his rich friends," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said.
Which rich friends is Crombie talking about? Is she implying, as the progressive mob online quickly claimed, that Ford will sell off this land to developers? It's owned by the city, he can't sell the land.
There is plenty to criticize the Ford government about, including their handling of the science centre and Ontario Place. Sadly, once again, Ford's weak opposition misses the mark and goes well outside reality to attack the government.
Closing this facility over a potential roof collapse is the right move.

Subject: Science; Air conditioning

Identifier / keyword: stick; ontario; science; centre; province; announced

Document 384 of 500

On alcohol, it's time to truly treat Ontarians as adults

Author: Goldberg, Jay

Publication info: Goldberg, Jay. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-alcohol-time-truly-treat-ontarians-as-adults/docview/3071163023/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+alcohol%2C+it%27s+time+to+truly+treat+Ontarians+as+adults&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Goldberg%2C+Jay&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's hard to know for sure, but the sky probably wouldn't fall if a group of friends in Kenora, Ont. could pick up a bottle of whisky after 6 p.m.
Unless he views that risk differently, Ontario Premier Doug Ford should be thinking about the logical next step after he announced plans to end just half of the province's alcohol monopoly.
Make no mistake: Ford's decision to end Ontario's special deal with The Beer Store and open up the sale of beer, wine and cider in corner stores and at grocery stores is a good thing.
But Ford still plans to leave the Liquor Control Board of Ontario as the exclusive wholesaler for bars, restaurants and retailers. Anyone wanting booze other than beer, wine and cider is still going to have to go to a government-run LCBO store to get it.
Consumers intuitively know that government doesn't need to be involved every time someone wants to buy a bottle of Canadian Club.
In Alberta, Saskatchewan, or south of the 49th parallel, whisky aficionados have a great selection of stores that are convenient, carry more products and are open longer hours.
But here in Ontario, everyone has to go to the government-run LCBO.
Ontarians would love to be able to pick up some drinks at a liquor store around the corner, ideally after the workday is over. Yet throughout most of the province, those consumers are out of luck.
Take the city of Kenora. There's just one LCBO in the entire town. That means the 16,000 people who live in Kenora, plus anyone in surrounding areas, are stuck driving to a single liquor store.
Yorkton, Sask. has the same population as Kenora. Yet, because the government in Saskatchewan no longer runs liquor stores, there are five different stores where folks in Yorkton can pick up a bottle of whisky.
In terms of hours, there's another stark contrast.
The LCBO in Kenora closes at 6 p.m. every day of the week other than Fridays. Yorkton's liquor stores are open until 10 p.m.
Why won't the Ontario government finally put an end to this war on convenience? The reason Ford is clutching at his pearls and saving the LCBO isn't because he thinks the government can run a more efficient or convenient alcohol store than a local business.
It's because he wants the dough. In 2022, the LCBO delivered a $2.5-billion wad of cash to Queen's Park, built upon a 33% profit margin. That's in addition to hundreds of millions of dollars the government already collects in alcohol taxes.
The LCBO sells alcohol at steep markup and uses the cash to pad provincial coffers.
That's what Ford and his pals at Queen's Park don't want to give up.
But that's nonsense. The province doesn't sell cigarettes or weed, but it collects big tax revenues on their sale.
When Ford announced the end of the province's special deal with The Beer Store, he played up the convenience and maturity factors.
"People are excited they're going to have the same choice and convenience as other Canadians and other people right across the world to buy a case of beer or a bottle or wine," said Ford.
Ford said his government would "never, ever" put an end to the LCBO monopoly.
That's silly. Does Ford really think government monopolies are bad for beer and wine, but good for whisky and gin? He can't be promoting convenience and maturity on the one hand and stonewalling it on the other.
But that's exactly what he's trying to do.
Consumers shouldn't let him. It's time to demand Ford treat Ontarians as adults when it comes to alcohol. Not just some of the time and with some products, but all of the time and with all products.
It's time to end the LCBO monopoly.
Goldberg is the Ontario director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Subject: Alcohol; Liquor stores; Beer; Whiskey

Business indexing term: Subject: Liquor stores

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: probably; group; friends; kenora; bottle; whisky

Title: On alcohol, it's time to truly treat Ontarians as adults

First page: A.13

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 22, 2024

column: Guest Opinion

Section: Comment
