WARMINGTON: Quiet Toronto street stormed by 'outrageous' anti-Israel protesters

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-quiet-toronto-street-stormed/docview/3066913073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Quiet+Toronto+street+stormed+by+%27outrageous%27+anti-Israel+protesters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you peered out of your home's window, it may have looked like something from a horror movie.
This gaggle of people looked like a militia from Gaza: Their heads, faces and eyes are all covered as they walked in unison along a quiet residential Toronto street, while chanting anti-Israel and anti-Jewish slogans.
Justin Trudeau @JustinTrudeau ????
Hang your head in shame Canadians
This is 1933 Nazi Germany level pathology
- Dr Jordan B Peterson (@jordanbpeterson) June 11, 2024
"Hang your head in shame," wrote Jordan Peterson on X. "This is 1933 Nazi Germany level Pathology."
It certainly stood out in North York on Sunday.
Once a demonstrator is on a residential street with his or her face covered by a keffiyeh and wearing sunglasses so no one can recognize them, they can no longer be called a peaceful counter-protester. It sure seems like harassment, intimidation and anti-Semitism.
Pro-Palestinian protesters walk down Hove St. in Toronto in a video reportedly taken on Sunday, June 9, 2024.
An in-your-face version of it.
"You are a baby killer, that's all you are. That's what you do," says a woman to another woman in a video recorded on Hove St. on Sunday, just an hour after the United Jewish Appeal's Walk With Israel.
"You are supporting genocide," says a man. "You are a filthy Zionist rat, that's all you are."
WARMINGTON: Walk With Israel support 'overwhelming' after 'record-breaking' turnout
WARMINGTON: In Toronto, vile anti-Semitism has been allowed to become normalized
All of this is on video and within the sight of Toronto Police, who were riding on bikes alongside these mystery disrupters.
"Come officers, do something," one neighbour says.
The officers were keeping an eye on the interlopers, trying to get them to move along. There were no arrests.
Hove is a quiet residential street with families just west of Bathurst St. and just off Sheppard Ave. Those who live there had never seen anything like this. This seemed to be a provocation.
This is the definition of "disturbing the peace".
- Ben Mulroney (@BenMulroney) June 11, 2024
About 30 anti-Israel participants made their way onto this street wearing red and white or black and white keffiyeh head scarves and literally got right in the faces of people walking there.
"To have roving mobs going through neighbourhoods to harass Jewish or any residents is the sum of all fears for any city," said Councillor James Pasternak. "This outrageous and (allegedly) illegal behaviour must be met by the full force of the law and our criminal justice system. Current laws, hate and criminal, are outdated and they must updated so we give police the tools they need to stop this madness."
It's disturbing and not the Toronto we remember while growing up.
So far Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau have not responded to requests for a comment.
Toronto Police are on this.
Pro-Hamas thugs with their faces covered walking through a predominantly Jewish neighbourhood to "protest" the war on the other side of the world.
These thugs are trying to scare and intimidate Jews in Toronto.
It's disgusting.
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) June 10, 2024
"Police received one report about a theft following a confrontation between protesters and participants of the walk, who were returning to their vehicle on Hove St. A hat worn by a participant is alleged to have been taken by a protester. No assaults or injuries have been reported," said Toronto Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer. "We are investigating."
It won't be easy. It's hard to tell who's who because of what they are wearing. But those outfits could end up being what gets them arrested.
Section 351(2) of the Criminal Code of Canada says that "everyone who, with intent to commit an indictable offence, has his face masked or coloured or is otherwise disguised is guilty of an indictable offence and liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years."
Police have the laws and the power to arrest these cowardly thugs.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Meanwhile, a man in Wolfie's Deli told me he was "swarmed" by about 20 people and ended up with a broken thumb. Toronto Police are looking into it.
Earlier, I saw this crowd marching up Hove past Pasternak's office - just after a similar crew also made their way through a ravine just north of Sheppard and breached their way into where the Jewish participants were finishing up their peaceful walk. Toronto Police quickly escorted them out of the area.
In total, six people were arrested that day
This would be a good case for the hate squad to pursue. There were also other concerning images from Sunday's event. At the corner of Bathurst and Sheppard, there was a woman holding up a swastika and there are pictures of some young guys appearing to stand on a mannequin draped in an Israeli flag.
Hamas supporters attempting to disrupt the Walk for Israel last Sunday stepping on a mannequin of a Jew wearing a flag of Israel. Is this the values of the Islamic Community in Canada? The CEO of Paramount Foods promoted the pro Hamas "protest".
- neveragainlivepodcast@gmail.com (@neveragainlive1) June 11, 2024
"We think all of these things should be investigated," said Israel Now's Meir Weinstein, whose team shot video and pictures of a lot of these activities. "People should not be bothered in their neighbourhoods because they are Jewish."
But they were being bothered. And so far, little is being done about it.

Document 403 of 500

'Will be formidable'; Mississauga mayor-elect promises to shake up political landscape

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-be-formidable-mississauga-mayor-elect/docview/3066607419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Will+be+formidable%27%3B+Mississauga+mayor-elect+promises+to+shake+up+political+landscape&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A wild card has been thrown into the political deck in the GTA and it will change the game of politics as the current players know it.
The game just got a whole lot more interesting with the election of Carolyn Parrish as Mississauga's new mayor.
"As you know, I can be quite charming and quite forceful," she teased in her victory speech. "We are going to use charm and force and we are going to take over the world."
She's not kidding. Parrish has waited a long time to get this job - 35 years to be precise.
Don't look now, but Mississauga has a fighter as mayor and no one understands that more than Premier Doug Ford, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown.
They know what this means.
There's a new political heavyweight on the stage - one who will hold no prisoners nor hold back on how she feels about anything and everything that affects Mississauga.
"I just want to tell you a very important thing," she said. "Three mayors (in Peel Region) actually get along, and we will be formidable when we go to Queen's Park or Ottawa (when) we need our fair share."
Mayor-elect Parrish may have run an understated campaign to protect her front-runner status. It worked - barely.
While it was a significant win with her receiving 43,494 votes, she won with far less support than what the early indicators showed. This was partly because of the anybody-but-Parrish movement behind the scenes and partly because she was running against several very good candidates. Councillors Alvin Tedjo (35,005 votes), Dipika Damerla (27,119) and Stephen Dasko (22,408) were all so good in this campaign that they split all of the anti-Parrish sentiment out there and ended up handing the veteran politician the win.
"If one of them had thrown their support behind another, who knows what would have happened here?" Mississauga realtor Steve Jay asked. "That said, the strong showing of those three sets up one of them to perhaps challenge Parrish in the council meetings and in the next election."
If just one of them had been the main opponent, Parrish may have ended up in retirement. Instead, she's the new sheriff in town who is afraid of nobody.
One thing is for sure: If people thought her being quiet during the byelection campaign to replace Bonnie Crombie was the new Parrish, they will find out very soon that she not only has plenty to say, but she will say it loudly.
Ford, Trudeau and Chow might be wise to listen because Parrish is not one to be bullied or pushed around. She also insists she is no Liberal.
"People forget I sat for the last two years in Ottawa as an Independent MP," she told me recently.
The reason she ended up out of caucus is because of that independent streak that saw her say what was on her mind and not care who it offended. She took a lot of heat for her comments about former U.S. president George W. Bush and calling those in the war in Iraq a "coalition of idiots."
It sure was not appreciated at the time, but in the decades that have followed there are many more people who agree with her that the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan did not work so well and perhaps she was on the right side of it after all.
Parrish also got into trouble with her fellow Liberals for calling prime minister Paul Martin "weak," but in the end Stephen Harper defeated her boss in an election in which many people agreed with her. Perhaps the truth is the 77-year-old has always been more suited toward municipal politics instead of the party kind. She deserves a chance to show what she can do and Mississauga residents have given her that chance.
Parrish thinks for herself and has indicated her plan is to speak robustly for her community.
"This is a good news story for Mississauga, because no one knows the ins and outs of the city better than Carolyn Parrish," said Jay, who has been selling houses in Mississauga for 30 years. "And no one knows how to get her point across better in a meeting than her, either."
This means if Ford decides to take over the cost of maintaining the Gardiner Expressway, Parrish will be there to say, "What can you do for Mississauga?" If the federal government is going to help pay for World Cup soccer in Toronto, Parrish will be there to talk about building a new soccer stadium in Mississauga.
If there is consideration to extend transit from Toronto farther into York Region, Parrish will be on the phone trying make sure Mississauga is not neglected. This promises to be good for journalists because it won't be boring. Her election may also create a lot of action for the citizens she represents.
Parrish, whose Ward 5 seat was won by Natalie Hart in the other Mississauga byelection, may end up having the cache of characters like Mel Lastman, Rob Ford and, of course, her old nemesis - the late, iconic 36-year Mississauga mayor Hazel McCallion.
"A lot of people think we were enemies, but it's not true," Parrish told me. "We had lunch just about every week."
McCallion went to war for her city and Parrish made it very clear Monday night she plans to follow in those large footsteps. jwarmington@postmedia.com

Subject: Mayors; Soccer; Politics; Prime ministers

Location: Iraq

Identifier / keyword: thrown; political; change; politics; current; players

Title: 'Will be formidable'; Mississauga mayor-elect promises to shake up political landscape

Section: News

WARMINGTON: New sheriff in Mississauga as mayor-elect Parrish arrives with guns ablazing

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-new-sheriff-mississauga-as-mayor-elect/docview/3066572242/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 405 of 500

Letters to the Editor, June 11, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-june-11-2024/docview/3066539030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: SMITH CAN REVOLUTIONIZE EDUCATION
Re "Graduating to energy" (Brian Lilley, June 7): With respect, I disagree that Stephen Lecce was sufficiently aggressive in the education portfolio. He may fare better in energy, where there are not as many social issues. Todd Smith needs to revolutionize our education system. Ontario students are arguably our greatest asset. Education, particularly in math and sciences, is slipping relative to global standards. I challenge Smith to end the school board woke tyranny. Defund them. Allow principals greater authority to manage schools. Give teachers the curriculum employed by more successful countries. Ensure parents have a strong voice in the education of their children.
Larry Sylvester
Acton, Ont.
(With a new minister in charge, it allows for new opportunities and ideas that would far more better align with the public and parental expectations of what their kids are learning in school.)
TRUMP ON THE RISE
In response to Donald Trump's conviction in the New York hush-money case, Jonah Goldberg writes (June 9) that "if opinions indicated by the latest polling hold, one could plausibly argue the verdict will cost Trump the election." Which opinions? Whose opinions? Goldberg is a noted critic of Trump, having appeared on various "leftist" shows like CNN, MSNBC, Bill Maher, etc. And as I watch thousands and thousands gather in Las Vegas for yet another jam-packed MAGA rally and as the windfall of small donations to Trump continues to skyrocket upward, I cannot help but wonder if Goldberg is afflicted by wishful thinking.
Jake VanderKooy
Brampton
(So many pundits couldn't figure out how Trump could win in 2016 and here we are again in 2024 and with everything that has happened they are still scratching their heads over how the former president can garner so much support from all corners.)
LIBERALS HAVE TO GO
I love my country. The current government hates it. Every day, this corrupt government gets worse and worse. Throwing every Liberal out of government is not enough anymore. Justin Trudeau and Chrystia Freeland need to be charged with treason. I think they cover up scandals and shield people from the truth, which I, as a citizen of this country, have the right to know. Someone can put this government away with what they know. Blow the whistle on these useless people!
Rick Dwornikiewicz
Delhi, Ont.
(The Liberals need a sound and convincing defeat in the next election.)

Suddenly anyone's race?; Expected Parrish shoo-in campaign maybe shaping up into a true fight

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/suddenly-anyones-race-expected-parrish-shoo/docview/3066196707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Suddenly+anyone%27s+race%3F%3B+Expected+Parrish+shoo-in+campaign+maybe+shaping+up+into+a+true+fight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Polls in the race for mayor of Mississauga show that what was expected to be an easy win for veteran politician Carolyn Parrish is too close to call.
Most polls show the longtime councillor and former Liberal and Independent MP holding a onepoint lead over challenger Dipika Damerla, but with other strong candidates in the mix no one is sure which way this is going to come out when the official results are known on Monday.
Stephen Dasko and Alvin Tedjo have also put up strong showings.
Whoever wins, they will finish out the next two years as mayor after Bonnie Crombie vacated the job to lead the provincial Liberal party and eventually challenge Progressive Conservative Doug Ford for premier.
Each of the council candidates wanting to replace her has strong skills and a constituency.
There was an anybody-but-Parrish movement, but there is also a belief that other candidates could split the vote and give her the win.
The voters will decide. It's an important election in that Mississauga is one of the 10 largest cities in Canada, but has little land left to build on as it transitions from being a Toronto suburb to its own vibrant city.
For 36 years, this was the expressed goal of mayor Hazel McCallion.
Crombie had McCallion's support for mayor, but whoever the new mayor is he or she will chart their own course for the city.
A lot of eyes will be on this election Monday because it really is a jump ball and any of the top candidates could come down with the chief magistrate's chain.

ZIVO: Experts blame 'safer supply' for skyrocketing opioid abuse among students

Author: Jordan Ercit

Publication info: Jordan Ercit. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/zivo-experts-blame-safer-supply-skyrocketing/docview/3066023257/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ZIVO%3A+Experts+blame+%27safer+supply%27+for+skyrocketing+opioid+abuse+among+students&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Jordan+Ercit&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A recent report by the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health suggests that prescription opioid abuse has exploded among Ontario's youth since 2021. Circumstantial evidence strongly suggests that narcotics diverted from government-funded "safer supply" programs are contributing to this trend.
The report (Drug Use Among Ontario Students) analyzes data gathered through a survey that CAMH distributes to over 200 schools every two years. According to last year's data, which was just recently made available, a whopping 21.8% of students in grades 7 to 12 reported using prescription opioids for "non-medical purposes."
That number was only 12.7% in 2021 - which suggests that the number of students abusing prescription opioids jumped by 71% in just two years. Student prescription opioid abuse is now at the highest levels seen since 2007, the earliest year that data for this class of drugs is available in this survey.
Harm reduction activists regularly downplay the dangers of pharmaceutical opioids and often cite toxicology data showing that most drug deaths are caused by illicit substances, such as street fentanyl. Yet doctors have repeatedly emphasized that most youth begin their addictions with pharmaceuticals and, once hooked, graduate onto harder drugs that kill them.
LILLEY: Toronto's overdose numbers bad, city's solutions worse
ZIVO: Doug Ford's pushback on 'safer supply' should go farther
It is imperative that we discover why Ontario's kids are suddenly getting hooked on prescription opioids. Thankfully, addiction experts have already found one likely culprit: Canada's experimental "safer supply" programs.
These programs claim to "save lives" by distributing free addictive drugs - predominantly hydromorphone, an opioid as potent as heroin - as an alternative to riskier street substances. In reality, approximately 80-90% of recipients sell or trade ("divert") their taxpayer-funded hydromorphone on the black market to acquire stronger drugs.
This then floods surrounding communities with diverted hydromorphone, leading to new addictions and relapses, especially among youth.
The data in CAMH's survey is not robust enough to conclusively implicate safer supply - for example, it lumps together all prescription opioids (i.e. hydromorphone, Percocet, Tylenol, oxycodone), meaning that researchers cannot see the degree to which hydromorphone is driving increased use.
Yet there is more than enough circumstantial evidence to warrant concern.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Toronto-based addiction physician Dr. Michael Lester said that colleagues across Canada have been seeing a spike in hydromorphone abuse since safer supply was made widely available. "This doesn't prove causality with respect to diverted safer supply increasing opioid usage among students, but common sense would assume that it is playing a role."
Dr. Michael Cooper, another addiction physician in Toronto, concurred and noted that increased use of a drug is often tied to increased supply. "There has been a general decrease in opioid prescribing by physicians since the release of the 2017 Canadian Opioid Prescribing Guideline, which makes it more likely that the increase in this study is due to opioids from government-funded safer supply programs," he said.
CAMH's report is consistent with troubling data coming out of B.C. Last July, the B.C. Coroners Service published a report studying youth drug deaths between 2017 and 2022. The report showed that, between 2017 to 2019, 0% of these deaths involved hydromorphone - but that number rose to 5.5% in 2020, the year safer supply was expanded, and continued climbing afterward (9.6% in 2021, and 22.2% in 2022).
The federal government is not making any serious attempt to measure how safer supply is impacting youth. Most harm reduction researchers simply ask drug addicts if they sell their drugs to kids and, when told no, treat this as scientific fact.
- Zivo is a freelance writer and the executive director of the Centre for Responsible Drug Policy.

Subject: Students; Drug addiction; Prescription drugs; Harm reduction; Narcotics

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: recent; report; centre; addiction; mental; health

LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals brag of record on gun crime as shootings rise

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeau-liberals-brag-record-on-gun-crime/docview/3065702800/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau+Liberals+brag+of+record+on+gun+crime+as+shootings+rise&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For Justin Trudeau's Liberals, even something as serious as ending shootings in Toronto is about making announcements, not getting results.
After a week of shocking and outrageous shootings, Toronto Centre MP and Trudeau cabinet minister Marci Ien was bragging about what her government had done.
Of course, shootings are up again in Toronto, and we saw a brazen attack that killed two men last Sunday, but Ien was still boasting.
"It's now illegal for anyone in your community to buy, sell, transfer, or import a handgun," Ien posted to X just before 9:30 Friday night.
"We don't listen to the gun lobby. We listen to survivors and communities who've experienced gun violence, and want these weapons off our streets."
It's now illegal for anyone in your community to buy, sell, transfer, or import a handgun.
We don't listen to the gun lobby. We listen to survivors and communities who've experienced gun violence, and want these weapons off our streets.
- Marci Ien (@MarciIen) June 8, 2024
It's a nice sentiment, but it doesn't address reality.
Two men were shot and killed, several more injured after two other men opened fire last Sunday night near a Rexdale high school More than 50 shots were fired just before 11 p.m. as two teams relaxed and lingered following a late evening soccer game.
Now, Delroy George Parkes, 61, and Seymour Gibbs, 46, are both dead, their families in mourning.
Delroy "George" Parkes, 61, of Woodstock, was fatally shot on Sunday, June 2, 2024.
Earlier that day, a 14-year-old boy was randomly shot as he left his apartment building, also in Rexdale.
Neither Ien's actions nor those of the Trudeau government have done anything to stop these shootings or other gun violence. And we know about a violent carjacking with a gun at Finch Ave. E. and McCowan Rd., in Scarborough, but how many more that we don't know about have gone down in recent days and weeks?
Seymour Gibbs, 46, of Toronto, was one of two men killed in a shooting in the parking lot of North Albion Collegiate Institute, in Rexdale, on Sunday, June 2, 2024.
According to the Toronto Police Data Portal, there were 193 shootings in the city this year between Jan. 1 and June 2 - that's more than the 177 recorded in the entirety of 2014.
So, we've had more shootings in five months than we had in an entire year a decade ago before the Trudeau government came to power.
What has their response been to increasing crime?
It hasn't been to get tough on criminals. In fact, they've softened sentencing provisions for many serious crimes and in all but the rarest of exceptions, they still require judges and justices of the peace to release anyone arrested as quickly as possible with the least onerous conditions.
Toronto's major crime index is up this year by 2.4% over last year, which doesn't sound like much, but only because there has been a nearly 20% drop in auto thefts so far this year. Auto thefts are still at record levels - almost double the number of thefts we saw five years ago, but down from last year's shocking 5,425 thefts.
Many of those auto thefts now involve handguns either through carjackings or thugs with guns breaking down your door to steal your keys.
LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals trying to scare voters to protect convicts
LILLEY: Ford government's auto theft move aimed at Trudeau, not thieves
LILLEY: Ford tells Trudeau Ontario won't follow B.C.'s lead on drugs
Marci Ien wants you to know though that her government, the Trudeau government, made it illegal for licenced and RCM- vetted hunters and sports shooters to "buy, sell, transfer ,or import a handgun." But whatever they are doing, it hasn't been enough to stop criminals from being able to "buy, sell, transfer, or import a handgun."
As usual, when it comes to dealing with gun crimes, the Trudeau Liberals put targets on the backs of law-abiding gun owners rather than the people who spent last weekend shooting up Rexdale.
In her post, Ien makes a comment about not listening to the gun lobby, which is just another sad and pathetic attempt to import American culture war issues into Canada.
The gun lobby in Canada, such as it is, advocates for sensible laws including background checks, safety courses, licencing and stiff penalties for anyone who breaks the law. What Ien is telling you with that comment is that she and the Trudeau government ignore common sense in favour of misguided ideology.
The Trudeau Liberals have talked a lot about cracking down on gun crime, they just haven't done it - and the stats back that up.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Crime; Shootings; Gun violence; Decades

Location: Canada

Document 412 of 500

Show more respect for integrity of our system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/show-more-respect-integrity-our-system/docview/3065656066/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Show+more+respect+for+integrity+of+our+system&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Of all the commentary in the furious discussion about revelations that some MPs are working with foreign governments, the most asinine was from Ontario Liberal MP Jennifer O'Connell.
In a national security and intelligence committee of parliamentarians (NSICOP) hearing Thursday, the MP for Pickering-Uxbridge heckled and tried to talk over Conservative MP Frank Caputo as he questioned Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc about which MPs were working with hostile governments. That prompted Conservative MP Garnett Genius to ask the committee chair to call O'Connell to order. She was breaking the rules.
"Oh, boo hoo. Get over it," she said. Really? Is this the level of childish tantrums we've reached in the hallowed halls of Parliament? Throw your toys out of the crib opposition politicians demand your government be held to account.
You can be sure if a majority of politicians suspected of feeding information to foreign governments were Conservative, the Liberals would have already read their names into Hansard, the record of Parliament.
Earlier in the week, the committee released a report saying some MPs were "wittingly assisting" foreign state actors after they were elected, and that Canada's slow response to such interference could impact our government for years to come.
The report by security agencies, including CSIS and RCMP, also revealed the government has denied more than 1,000 documents, in whole or in part, requested by the committee.
Let's not forget, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau triggered the latest round of accusations about foreign interference into Canada's national affairs when, in September 2023, he accused India's government of involvement in the shooting death of Canadian Sikh leader Hardeep Singh Nijjar in June of last year, before anyone had been arrested.
Pressed at the NSICOP committee to reveal names, LeBlanc said the information was uncorroborated.
"I think it's important for Canadians to understand that these names are contained in intelligence reports. In some cases, it's uncorroborated or unverified intelligence," LeBlanc said.
How can Canadians vote in the next election after these revelations? How can we have faith in the integrity of our politicians without vital information - that being the names of those who may have betrayed this country.
Boo hoo to you too, Ms. O'Connell. Show more respect for Parliament, for Canadians and for the democratic process we hold dear.

Subject: Committees; Politics; Politicians

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: commentary; furious; discussion; revelations; working; foreign

Document 413 of 500

Tycoon built auto parts biz into company worth billions

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tycoon-built-auto-parts-biz-into-company-worth/docview/3065655583/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tycoon+built+auto+parts+biz+into+company+worth+billions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frank Stronach, the auto parts magnate facing five charges including sexual assault and rape, arrived in Canada with very little money but big dreams.
Stronach, born in Austria in 1932, grew up in a working class family while the country was under Nazi rule. He left school at 14 to work as a tool and die maker. He was also semi-professional soccer player.
In his early 20s, Stronach immigrated to Canada with around $200 and three years later, in 1957, opened a one-man auto parts venture in a rented garage in Toronto that would be later named Magna International.
The company, which grew rapidly after securing a contract from General Motors two years later, is one of the largest automotive suppliers in the world with more than 179,000 employees in 28 countries and sales of more than C$58 billion.
Stronach is also regarded as a champion thoroughbred owner and breeder.
In 1988, Stronach had his first taste of politics as a federal Liberal Party candidate in the York-Simcoe riding, but lost to John Cole of the Progressive Conservatives.
In 2011, he returned to his native Austria to enter the political fray, suggesting reforms to taxes, health, and education.
That same year, Stronach and his daughter Belinda founded The Stronach Group, an entertainment and real estate company that included horse racing and a pari-mutuel wagering technology company.
However, in October 2018, five years after he resigned as trustee to officially run for office in Austria, the billionaire mogul and his wife Elfriede filed a $520-million lawsuit in Ontario Superior Court against his daughter Belinda Stronach - who he appointed to run the family business - her two children and chief executive at the time Alon Ossip.
Stronach alleged that, after he gave up control of the company, they mismanaged the family's assets and conspired to wrest ownership from him.
Less than two years later, the Stronach family announced a settlement that saw the company split up.
The business titan took control of the stallion and breeding business, all North American farms and all European assets.
Belinda remained chairwoman and president of The Stronach Group, which included its horse racing, gaming, real estate and related assets.
In 1999, Stronach was made a Member of the Order of Canada.

Subject: Sex crimes; Horse racing

Location: Canada; Austria

Company / organization: Name: Stronach Group; NAICS: 711212, 711219

Frank Stronach built auto parts business into company worth billions

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/frank-stronach-built-auto-parts-business-into/docview/3065621350/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Frank+Stronach+built+auto+parts+business+into+company+worth+billions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frank Stronach, the auto parts magnate facing five charges including sexual assault and rape, arrived in Canada with very little money but big dreams.
Stronach, born in Austria in 1932, grew up in a working class family while the country was under Nazi rule. He left school at 14 to work as a tool and die maker. He was also semi-professional soccer player.
In his early 20s, Stronach immigrated to Canada with around $200 and three years later, in 1957, opened a one-man auto parts venture in a rented garage in Toronto that would be later named Magna International.
The company, which grew rapidly after securing a contract from General Motors two years later, is one of the largest automotive suppliers in the world with more than 179,000 employees in 28 countries and sales of more than C$58 billion (US$42 billion).
Stronach is also regarded as a champion thoroughbred owner and breeder.
In 1988, Stronach had his first taste of politics as a federal Liberal Party candidate in the York-Simcoe riding, but lost to John Cole of the Progressive Conservatives.
In 2011, he returned to his native Austria to enter the political fray, suggesting reforms to taxes, health, and education.
That same year, Stronach and his daughter Belinda founded The Stronach Group, an entertainment and real estate company that included horse racing and a pari-mutuel wagering technology company.
WARMINGTON: Canadian business tycoon Frank Stronach accused of serious sex crimes
MANDEL: Canada's ongoing Succession drama with Stronachs back in court
SARIT electric vehicles to be used, tested, refined on Exhibition Place
However, in October 2018, five years after he resigned as trustee to officially run for office in Austria, the billionaire mogul and his wife Elfriede filed a $520-million lawsuit in Ontario Superior Court against his daughter Belinda Stronach - who he appointed to run the family business - her two children and chief executive at the time Alon Ossip.
Stronach alleged that, after he gave up control of the company, they mismanaged the family's assets and conspired to wrest ownership from him.
Less than two years later, the Stronach family announced a settlement that saw the company split up. The business titan took control of the stallion and breeding business, all North American farms and all European assets.
Belinda remained chairwoman and president of The Stronach Group, which included its horse racing, gaming, real estate and related assets.
In 1999, Stronach was made a Member of the Order of Canada.
In March, Stronach's wife of 60 years Elfriede, a dedicated horse advocate, died at the age of 80 after battling an illness.

Ford's words unhelpful

Author: Maw, John T

Publication info: Maw, John T. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-words-unhelpful/docview/3065527617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+words+unhelpful&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Maw%2C+John+T&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re "Ontario Premier Doug Ford just said what many have been thinking since Oct. 7" (Joe Warmington, May 31): A premier ought to know better than contrive a hysterically irrational statement that we have "immigrants behind Jewish school shooting"! New immigrants landed two weeks ago or those who arrived eons ago? In Mississauga, very much like the rest of the GTA, I hardly come across First Nations, Metis and Inuit people in and around Square One or the progenies of the Dominion of Canada of 1867. Let us not repeat what we hear south of the border: They babble Chicken Little's dictum "The sky is falling" to egg on citizens that 'felons, prisoners and mental institution patients'are crossing the border! No matter where they were born, our folks are civilized, educated, upstanding and unarmed citizens. For sure, shooting should never happen anywhere to harm innocent people including war-torn corners of our world. The snipers could well be new Canadians; however, let our hard-working law enforcement officers identify and inform us who these bad actors or meddlers are before we tar all immigrants with the same brush.
(Sure, his words could have been better-chosen, but at least Ford has said something. The ongoing anti-Semitism and violence against the Jewish community has thus far gone unchecked. And if you're more angry about some words from a politician than the fact a Jewish school was shot at, you're part of the problem)

Document 417 of 500

Graduating to energy; Ford shuffles Lecce out of education to take on Trudeau in Ottawa

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/graduating-energy-ford-shuffles-lecce-out/docview/3065527600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Graduating+to+energy%3B+Ford+shuffles+Lecce+out+of+education+to+take+on+Trudeau+in+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The big change in the Ontario cabinet shuffle is that Stephen Lecce is moving from education to energy and Todd Smith is moving from energy to education. Beyond that, Premier Doug Ford made some tweaks to his cabinet that most people won't even notice.
In Ontario politics, the big three portfolios are health, education and finance, and Ford has left Sylvia Jones at health and Peter Bethlenfalvy at finance but is moving Lecce. As Meat Loaf might sing, two out of three ain't bad.
"We need someone a little more aggressive in this role," a top Ford adviser said of Lecce moving to energy.
Meanwhile, Smith being moved to education is seen as an attempt to calm the waters. The contracts with the education unions are pretty much wrapped up and Smith is seen as someone who can go in and make people forget there were ever any problems in the file.
That's the role he took on in 2019 when he was appointed as minister of children, community and social services. Smith took over what has often been called "the Ministry of Tears" at Queen's Park and made problems disappear.
Lecce, though, is seen as an aggressive communicator who will be able to go toe to toe with the Trudeau government in Ottawa on the carbon tax and the need to build more electrical generation on the province. Lecce's new official title is minister of energy and electrification, meaning he has responsibility for building out the grid for the move to electric vehicles, which will involve moves that will annoy the Trudeau Liberals.
It's doubtful that Ford and Lecce will go to war with the Trudeau Liberals but there will be a more aggressive stance on this file and obvious friction. Don't expect Lecce to call for EVs to be powered by a cluster of new windmills across the province.
Stan Cho moves from being minister of long-term care to minister of tourism, culture and gaming, with responsibility for OLG. Meanwhile, Natalia Kusendova-Bashta moves from being a parliamentary assistant to taking on the role of minister of long-term care.
Paul Calandra, who has been Ford's go-to guy on several files, keeps his role as municipal affairs and housing but loses his spot as government house leader. Steve Clark, who left cabinet over the Greenbelt controversy last year, becomes government house leader.
Despite rampant speculation that newly elected MPP Zee Hamid would take over as minister of red tape reduction, that role goes to Kitchener-Conestoga MPP Mike Harris. Rob Flack, who represents Elgin-Middlesex-London, takes over as minister of farming, agriculture and agribusiness which splits the role with Lisa Thompson who was at Agriculture but keeps the rural affairs portfolio.
There were several moves in this shuffle, but health, finance, transportation, economic development, labour, colleges and universities all remain the same.
With two years to go until the next election, unless they go sooner, this cabinet shuffle is about giving the Ford government a facelift. It's not a complete restructuring, but it is an attempt to freshen things up and put something different in front of voters.
The most interesting thing to watch will be the new stance the Ford government takes when it comes to the energy file and their relationship with the Trudeau Liberals in Ottawa. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Appointments & personnel changes; Energy; Long term health care

Business indexing term: Subject: Appointments & personnel changes

Title: Graduating to energy; Ford shuffles Lecce out of education to take on Trudeau in Ottawa

Document 418 of 500

Community besieged; Jews have to take unreal steps to feel safe

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/community-besieged-jews-have-take-unreal-steps/docview/3065526932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Community+besieged%3B+Jews+have+to+take+unreal+steps+to+feel+safe&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine having to corral a dozen or more cops to hold a news conference in public on a busy Toronto street - or having to ensure that police and private security are at every single community event you hold.
That's the reality for Toronto's Jewish community.
It was like this, to a degree, before Oct. 7, but it has only increased since then.
I'm not sure people who aren't Jewish can understand the lengths their fellow citizens need to go to in order to feel safe these days. As a non-Jew who has been to several events, synagogues and community meetings over the last several months, it's quite shocking.
A point I keep making is that I've been going to Mass at Catholic churches across the country my entire life, I've never seen a cop at Mass unless they were attending. Most Canadians don't see cops in their places of worship, but for Canada's Jews it's now standard - people don't think about it but they should.
On Monday, several Jewish groups held a news conference to announce they were seeking intervenor status in the court case to remove the pro-Hamas encampment at the University of Toronto. Standing on Harbord St., I could easily count a dozen Toronto Police officers in my line of sight and there were more on the side streets and behind the building.
"I think it's difficult for people outside of the Jewish community to appreciate the sense of vulnerability that we have," Rabbi Seth Goren said when asked about the large police presence.
"When our places of worship are attacked, when our schools have been shot up, when we have people who have been physically assaulted, it raises concerns."
Goren called it a "sad sign of the times" and "disturbing but necessary" for these security measures to take place.
The news conference was just over a week after a Jewish girl's school in the north end of Toronto had several bullets shot into it. There have been multiple synagogues attacked, Jewish businesses attacked and that is on top of the attacks on individuals ranging from young students to senior citizens.
Last week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow were all asked what concrete steps they were taking to deal with anti-Semitism. While Trudeau and Ford spoke in general terms about their efforts, Chow got down to the nitty gritty.
She spoke of her roundtable on safety, local community safety walks, and more.
"Whether it is shatterproof glass, fencing, lighting, or trimming of bushes, or cameras. We assist because we have the knowledge and best practices," Chow said.
Her list of assistance brought out a strong response from Premier Ford.
"You just heard the mayor say shatterproof glass. Really?" Ford asked. "We have to have this in schools, in Ontario? Where have we gone?" Ford wasn't criticizing Chow, or her program, he was criticizing the idea that any school in Ontario should have to consider shatterproof glass on the windows, lest someone shoot through it.
That's the reality of the Jewish community in Canada now.
As someone who has been close to the Jewish community in several cities across this country for more than 20 years, I can say as an outsider that it wasn't always like this.
Now though, when there are events in the Jewish community, it is often the case that everyone must register ahead of time, everyone must present government ID to get in, and the location of the event is only revealed within 24 hours before it takes place.
This weekend, the United Jewish Appeal is hosting a Walk with Israel event to raise money for those impacted by the war including evacuees. That event is under threat by people who don't want Jews to have a place in public life in Canada.
Anti-Semitism is more than slogans and hate speech, it's making our fellow citizens feel unsafe, that they aren't welcome, in their own community, their own country. blilley@postmedia.com

Letters to the Editor, June 7, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-june-7-2024/docview/3065467609/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+June+7%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: JUST LIKE THE REST OF US
So, Bryan Adams comments that he wrote to the Liberal government and was totally ignored - never even received a response ("No laughing matter," Joe Warmington, June 2). It's somewhat comforting to learn that celebrities are treated with the same arrogant indifference that us ordinary Canadians are by our elected representatives.
Tom Newell
Niagara Falls
(If Adams was heaping praise on the Liberals he would have received a response. This government is ridiculous)
HATE CRIME
In the last couple of weeks, a Jewish girls elementary school was shot at and a synagogue in Kensington was vandalized and in both cases the police were going to investigate whether it was a hate crime. What else could it have been?
Allan Tweyman
Toronto
(Hard to imagine what else it would have been)
FORD'S WORDS UNHELPFUL
Re "Ontario Premier Doug Ford just said what many have been thinking since Oct. 7" (Joe Warmington, May 31): A premier ought to know better than contrive a hysterically irrational statement that we have "immigrants behind Jewish school shooting"! New immigrants landed two weeks ago or those who arrived eons ago? In Mississauga, very much like the rest of the GTA, I hardly come across First Nations, Metis and Inuit people in and around Square One or the progenies of the Dominion of Canada of 1867. Let us not repeat what we hear south of the border: They babble Chicken Little's dictum "The sky is falling" to egg on citizens that 'felons, prisoners and mental institution patients' are crossing the border! No matter where they were born, our folks are civilized, educated, upstanding and unarmed citizens. For sure, shooting should never happen anywhere to harm innocent people including war-torn corners of our world. The snipers could well be new Canadians; however, let our hard-working law enforcement officers identify and inform us who these bad actors or meddlers are before we tar all immigrants with the same brush.
Rev. Dr. John T. Mathew
Mississauga
(Sure, his words could have been chosen better but at least Ford has said something. The ongoing anti-Semitism and violence against the Jewish community has thus far gone unchecked. And if you're more angry about some words from a politician than the fact a Jewish school was shot at, you're part of the problem)

Document 420 of 500

LILLEY: Ford shuffles Lecce to energy to take on Trudeau in Ottawa

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-shuffles-lecce-energy-take-on-trudeau/docview/3065464340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+shuffles+Lecce+to+energy+to+take+on+Trudeau+in+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The big change in the Ontario cabinet shuffle is that Stephen Lecce is moving from education to energy and Todd Smith is moving from energy to education. Beyond that, Premier Doug Ford made some tweaks to his cabinet that most people won't even notice.
In Ontario politics, the big three portfolios are health, education and finance and Ford has left Sylvia Jones at health and Peter Bethlenfalvy at finance but is moving Lecce. As Meatloaf might sing, two out of three ain't bad.
"We need someone a little more aggressive in this role," a top Ford adviser said of Lecce moving to energy.
Meanwhile, Smith being moved to education is seen as an attempt to calm the waters at education. The contracts with the education unions are pretty much wrapped up and Smith is seen as someone who can go in and make people forget there were ever any problems in the file.
That's the role he took on in 2019 when he was appointed as Minister of Children, Community and Social Services. Smith took over what has often been called "the Ministry of Tears" at Queen's Park and made problems disappear.
Lecce, though, is seen as an aggressive communicator who will be able to go toe to toe with the Trudeau government in Ottawa on the carbon tax and the need to build more electrical generation on the province. Lecce's new official title is Minister of Energy and Electrification, meaning he has responsibility for building out the grid for the move to electric vehicles, which will involve moves that will annoy the Trudeau Liberals.
It's doubtful that Ford and Lecce will go to war with the Trudeau Liberals but there will be a more aggressive stance on this file and obvious friction. Don't expect Lecce to call for EVs to be powered by a cluster of new windmills across the province.
Stan Cho moves from being Minister of Long Term Care to being the Minister of Tourism, Culture and Gaming, with responsibility for OLG. Meanwhile, Natalia Kusendova-Bashta moves from being a parliamentary assistant to taking on the role of Minister of Long Term Care.
Paul Calandra, who has been Ford's go-to guy on several files, keeps his role as Municipal Affairs and Housing but loses his spot as Government House Leader. Steve Clark, who left cabinet over the Greenbelt controversy last year, becomes Government House Leader.
Despite rampant speculation that newly elected MPP Zee Hamid would take over as Minister of Red Tape Reduction, that role goes to Kitchener-Conestoga MPP Mike Harris. Rob Flack, who represents Elgin-Middlesex-London takes over as Minister of Farming, Agriculture and Agribusiness which splits the role with Lisa Thompson who was at Agriculture but keeps the Rural Affairs portfolio.
LILLEY: Doug Ford reduces surgical wait times, opposition complains
LILLEY: Lecce looks to put controls on speakers at Ontario schools
There were several moves in this shuffle but health, finance, transportation, economic development, labour, colleges and universities all remain the same.
With two years to go until the next election, unless they go sooner, this cabinet shuffle is about giving the Ford government a face lift. It's not a complete restructuring but it is an attempt to freshen things up and put something fresh in front of voters.
The most interesting thing to watch will be the new stance the Ford government takes when it comes to the energy file and their relationship with the Trudeau Liberals in Ottawa.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Appointments & personnel changes; Energy; Long term health care

LILLEY: Canadian Jews have to take unreal steps to feel safe, that's wrong

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-canadian-jews-have-take-unreal-steps-feel/docview/3065456146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Canadian+Jews+have+to+take+unreal+steps+to+feel+safe%2C+that%27s+wrong&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine having to corral a dozen or more cops to hold a news conference in public on a busy Toronto street - or having to ensure that police and private security are at every single community event you hold.
That's the reality for Toronto's Jewish community.
It was like this, to a degree, before Oct. 7, but it has only increased since then.
I'm not sure people who aren't Jewish can understand the lengths their fellow citizens need to go to in order to feel safe these days. As a non-Jew who has been to several events, synagogues and community meetings over the last several months, it's quite shocking.
A point I keep making is that I've been going to Mass at Catholic churches across the country my entire life, I've never seen a cop at Mass unless they were attending. Most Canadians don't see cops in their places of worship, but for Canada's Jews it's now standard - people don't think about it but they should.
LILLEY: Shooting at Jewish school and Hamas propaganda in the streets
WARMINGTON: Only U of T encampment allowed is 'Little Gaza' while one supporting Israel is removed
40 pro-Palestinian encampment protesters removed from York University
On Monday, several Jewish groups held a news conference to announce they were seeking intervenor status in the court case to remove the pro-Hamas encampment at the University of Toronto. Standing on Harbord St., I could easily count a dozen Toronto Police officers in my line of sight and there were more on the side streets and behind the building.
"I think it's difficult for people outside of the Jewish community to appreciate the sense of vulnerability that we have," Rabbi Seth Goren said when asked about the large police presence.
"When our places of worship are attacked, when our schools have been shot up, when we have people who have been physically assaulted, it raises concerns."
Goren called it a "sad sign of the times" and "disturbing but necessary" for these security measures to take place.
A pro-Palestinian encampment is seen inside the fenced off section of King's College Circle at the University of Toronto, Friday, May 24, 2024.
The news conference was just over a week after a Jewish girl's school in the north end of Toronto had several bullets shot into it. There have been multiple synagogues attacked, Jewish businesses attacked and that is on top of the attacks on individuals ranging from young students to senior citizens.
Last week, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow were all asked what concrete steps they were taking to deal with anti-Semitism. While Trudeau and Ford spoke in general terms about their efforts, Chow got down to the nitty gritty.
She spoke of her roundtable on safety, local community safety walks, and more.
"Whether it is shatterproof glass, fencing, lighting, or trimming of bushes, or cameras. We assist because we have the knowledge and best practices," Chow said.
Her list of assistance brought out a strong response from Premier Ford.
"You just heard the mayor say shatterproof glass. Really?" Ford asked. "We have to have this in schools, in Ontario? Where have we gone?"
Ford wasn't criticizing Chow, or her program, he was criticizing the idea that any school in Ontario should have to consider shatterproof glass on the windows, lest someone shoot through it.
That's the reality of the Jewish community in Canada now.
As someone who has been close to the Jewish community in several cities across this country for more than 20 years, I can say as an outsider that it wasn't always like this.
Now though, when there are events in the Jewish community, it is often the case that everyone must register ahead of time, everyone must present government ID to get in, and the location of the event is only revealed within 24 hours before it takes place.
This weekend, the United Jewish Appeal is hosting a Walk with Israel event to raise money for those impacted by the war including evacuees. That event is under threat by people who don't want Jews to have a place in public life in Canada.
Anti-Semitism is more than slogans and hate speech, it's making our fellow citizens feel unsafe, that they aren't welcome, in their own community, their own country.
blilley@postmedia.com

Document 422 of 500

LILLEY: Ontario taxpayers paying millions in political party subsidies

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ontario-taxpayers-paying-millions/docview/3065229648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ontario+taxpayers+paying+millions+in+political+party+subsidies&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario taxpayers forked over $13 million to the four political parties represented at Queen's Park last year.
It's time for the political welfare to stop.
Doug Ford promised to end the per-vote subsidy, first brought in by Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government in 2017. The measure came as Liberal fundraising from average citizens was drying up and after Wynne's government was caught in cash-for-access fundraisers with business executives.
"I do not believe the government should be taking money from hard-working taxpayers and giving it to political parties," Doug Ford posted to Facebook when he was campaigning for the PC leadership in early 2018.
In February 2021, Ford's government introduced legislation to extend the per vote subsidy rather than wind it down as promised.
"This subsidy is being extended due to the financial impact of COVID-19," the government said in a news release at the time.
There is no doubt that COVID and the restrictions imposed by the Ford government at the time severely impacted the fundraising abilities for all parties involved. The plan was, however, not to immediately eliminate the per-vote subsidy, but to phase it out gradually.
The rate had been $2.54 per year for each vote a party received in the previous election. By 2021, the per-vote subsidy had decreased to $1.80 annually. Ford not only kept the subsidy in place, he boosted it back up to $2.54 until the end of 2024 when all subsidies are scheduled to end.
Recommended video
In 2023, the PC Party of Ontario received a subsidy of $5.1 million from taxpayers, the NDP received $3.6 million, the Ontario Liberal Party, nearly $3.6 million and the Greens around $700,000. Another $243,000 was paid out to the New Blue Party of Ontario, a party that isn't represented in the legislature but met the threshold of receiving 2% of all ballots cast in the 2022 campaign.
Do the parties really need this money?
The easy answer is no, and that's why it needs to stop.
The PC Party raised just shy of $8 million last year, the NDP, $3.2 million, the Liberals claim $3.1 million - higher than the $2.1 million reported to Elections Ontario - and the Greens raised $170,000.
Each party is also sitting on a significant surplus.
The PC Party had $8 million in the bank at the end of 2023; the Liberals, $2.5 million; the NDP, $2.1 million; and the Greens, $525,000.
These parties clearly don't need money from taxpayers.
Supporters of per-vote subsidies will always claim it is about supporting democracy and taking big money out of politics. The reality is, big money has already been taken out of politics in Ontario.
It has been illegal for the last seven years for companies or unions to donate to a political party. There was a time when both big labour and big business threw their money and weight around in Ontario politics.
In some elections, prior to the ban, unions and union-funded groups would far outspend any single political party.
The current maximum that any single individual can donate is $3,375 per year. Politicians are not changing their votes or their policy positions for a $3,375 donation.
The problem with per-vote subsidies is that it rewards parties based on their past performance, not current performance. If Ford made moves that were wildly unpopular, especially with his voting base, he would hear about it quickly and donations would dry up.
With a guaranteed $5 million per year coming in from taxpayers, he's less concerned.
Per-vote subsidies always help the party in power most, and not those trying to offer the public an alternative. Parties need to go out and earn your vote to be able to campaign, and they should have to earn your donation, as well.
The per-vote subsidy is scheduled to end on Dec. 31; let's hope the government sticks to the plan and stops taking our tax dollars to fill party coffers.
LILLEY: Is Ontario run by elected officials or appointed bureaucrats?
LILLEY: No, Doug Ford isn't about to call an early election

Subject: Fund raising; Donations; Subsidies; Elections; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Donations Subsidies

Identifier / keyword: ontario; taxpayers; forked; million; political; parties

Title: LILLEY: Ontario taxpayers paying millions in political party subsidies

Publication year: 2024

Document 423 of 500

LILLEY: Is Ontario run by elected officials or appointed bureaucrats?

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-is-ontario-run-elected-officials-appointed/docview/3064879176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Is+Ontario+run+by+elected+officials+or+appointed+bureaucrats%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Who is in charge in Ontario? Is it the elected officials, the MPPs and the government they form or the unelected, appointed officers of the legislature.
If it were up to the opposition, the province would be run by the Auditor General, the Financial Accountability Officer and the Integrity Commissioner. The opposition parties have demanded investigations into the Ontario Place redevelopment, into the school system, municipal regulation and more.
It seems that there is no government decision that the provincial NDP and Liberals don't think should go forward without an investigation by one of these Parliamentary watchdogs. This week the Ontario Liberals are celebrating that the FAO is investigating the government's decision to open nine Service Ontario locations inside Staples stores.
Let's put that in perspective. There are 273 Service Ontario locations across the province. There are 80 government-operated locations and 193 private locations including four per cent, or eight locations in retail outlets.
The government proposal is to close 11 locations currently operated by private-sector operators and replace them with nine inside Staples stores. The 11 outlets not having their contracts renewed didn't meet provincial standards or had signaled that they were looking to close or retire.
That's 5.6% of all private-sector operators not being renewed. It's not like they would have been replaced by a government-operated location.
The government currently operates inside retail locations of Canadian Tire, Home Hardware and IDA. They are expanding to Staples and some Walmart locations and are looking for other retails partners that meet the criteria.
The goal is to offer Ontario residents locations with longer hours, easier parking and improved customer service. Most people would say those are good things, but the Ontario Liberal Party sees only bad things happening.
In her letter to the FAO requesting an investigation, Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman said that "these contracts will provide significant value to these large American companies."
There you have it, the private sector, American companies, all the evils of the world wrapped up in one!
Bowman's complaints are two-fold, that the government is paying to set-up the locations in Staples and that Staples might sell you some pens or printer paper when you show up to renew your driver's licence. As the government has explained, they pay to ensure the consistent look and feel of the branding of all locations be it a government location, a retail location or one of the office locales operated by a small business.
As for picking up something from the store as you leave, what's the downside for Ontario residents?
There are two Service Ontario locations near Queen's Park - one a government location at 777 Bay St. and another a bit further away inside a Canadian Tire store. As someone who works at Queen's Park and lives nearby, I can attest that the Canadian Tire location provides faster service by far and yes, I've picked out a few products on the way out.
LILLEY: Doug Ford reduces surgical wait times, opposition complains
LILLEY: No, Doug Ford isn't about to call an early election
This deal is such small ball that it's insane that the FAO is launching an investigation at this stage. Perhaps in a year, as the pilot project has progressed, it might make sense but at this point, it is just the FAO being used by the opposition to second guess government decisions.
If this type of investigation for every decision continues, we will have paralysis by analysis within the provincial government. No decisions will be made because they will be afraid of the looming second guessing.
Voters choose who they want to run the government, and when the party they put in charge goes astray, voters can throw the bums out.
In the meantime, the FAO should stick to trying to shed light on how our tax dollars are actually being spent. That's why the office was created, not to run interference for the opposition parties.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Subject: Opposition parties; Voters; Public officials

Identifier / keyword: charge; ontario; elected; officials; government; unelected

Title: LILLEY: Is Ontario run by elected officials or appointed bureaucrats?

WARMINGTON: Parrish feels heat in Mississauga's hotly-contested mayoral race

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-parrish-feels-heat-mississaugas-hotly/docview/3064714817/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Parrish+feels+heat+in+Mississauga%27s+hotly-contested+mayoral+race&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This battle to become Mississauga's next mayor is burning hot.
Not since the city's famous train derailment decades ago has Mississauga experienced such heat.
For the 36 years, Hurricane Hazel McCallion was mayor - between 1978 and 2014. McCallion was so popular, she didn't even bother to put out election signs or campaign much when asking voters to mark her name on ballots.
But Mississauga - Canada's seventh-largest city with a population of 829,000 - has matured and is a different city than the one McCallion presided over. Like many communities in Canada, residents are struggling with traffic, crimes like carjacking and housing shortages.
The latest polls indicate Carolyn Parrish, a councillor and former Liberal MP, is the front-runner in a pack of 20 candidates vying for Mississauga's top municipal job.
Voters go to the polls in a byelection next Monday to replace former incumbent Bonnie Crombie, who's now leader of Ontario's Liberal Party.
Once again, misinformation is being spread by other parties! ?? I DO NOT support a Mississauga Land Transfer Tax. It is a provincial issue and cannot be imposed locally. Together, let's build a more affordable Mississauga. Vote Parrish for Mayor today & tomorrow, June 1 & 2,...
- Carolyn Parrish for Mayor of Mississauga (@carolynhparrish) June 1, 2024
Current councillors Dipika Damerla, Stephen Dasko and Alvin Tedjo, as well as the former mayor's ex-husband, Brian Crombie, are among those challenging Parrish. They are all good candidates.
This image was taken from a City of Mississauga X posting April 10, 2024. The city said it would be lighting up Mississauga City Hall's clock tower to mark International Day of Pink.
All eyes are in the by-election for Mississauga mayor - Joe Warmington photo
During this campaign, Parrish drew criticism after refusing to participate in a number of debates, saying she made the decision because she had been "personally threatened."
"This campaign has been the ugliest I have ever been in," admitted Parrish, who is no stranger to controversy.
An MP from1993-2004, Parrish was turfed from the Liberal caucus and forced to sit as an independent.
Parrish was ejected from the Liberal caucus after criticizing then-U.S. president George W. Bush and told then-prime minister Paul Martin that he could "go to hell" if he didn't like what she said.
Carolyn, you refused to rule out and say "no" to a new land transfer tax in an interview with the CBC yesterday.
We know the contempt you have for taxpayers and their ability to pay.
Will you now deny you said this about raising taxes?
- Dipika Damerla (@DipikaDamerla) June 1, 2024
The Toronto Sun put some questions on key issues to Parrish:
What is the future of Mississauga's downtown?
"I really want to recreate the downtown of our city. There have been proposals with some of developers to do a conference centre and hotel on the Living Arts Centre (LAC) Land and modernizing it. We need to enliven the heart of our city, so our residents stay here rather than go to Toronto for entertainment."
The famous Marilyn Monroe buildings in downtown Mississauga are on example of the city's creative new skyline - Joe Warmington photo
Will Mississauga impose a land-transfer tax like Toronto?
"The public is strongly opposed. I will not be contemplating nor even discussing a MLTT (municipal land transfer tax."
Recommended video
What is the plan to deal with crippling traffic?
"A first-class transportation system is the backbone of any progressive and affordable city. Making transit an agreeable alternative to the car can reduce traffic congestion, but we need more progressive solutions. The Hurontario LRT is a start."
What about wait times for 911 calls?
"Wait time for 911 calls indicate that the police force needs more officers but also a different type of service. The police chief presents regularly to Regional Council and provides statistics that indicate a huge number of calls involve mental health issues and require mental health experts rather than persons specifically trained in basic police work."
Are there untapped lands that could be utilized to solve housing crisis?
"I have touched on this with the rezoning of Hurontario. Since Mississauga is built out to its boundaries except for isolated pieces of land, a creative approach to house the 300,000 persons we are expected to receive in the future, needs to be found. Rezoning stretches of large malls, while retaining stores in the bases of apartment buildings, creates shoppers for the mall owners and the added residential units we need."
This framegrab of Mississauga mayoral candidate Carolyn Parrish was taken from a video on her campaign website.
(https://www.parrishformayor.com)
Carolyn Parrish is the front runner in the Mississauga by-election for mayor - Joe Warmington photo
Will you be pushing for Mississauga to leave Peel Region?
"I believe separation from Peel was most practical 25 years ago when Mississauga was growing, was able to be self-sufficient and less locked into regional services. The Region is a $3.6-billion operation which handles a massive social service sector as well as core services like water, wastewater, garbage and recycling, a joint police force and paramedics. I believe the time to break up the Region has passed."
Why did you decide to no longer attend mayoral debates?
"Our office received threats after the first all candidates' meeting. I tend to ignore those as I received them in the Iraq War and other controversial times of my career ... On the advice of my very experienced campaign manager, I chose to decline debate invitations and booked events with the public every evening, sometimes several, where I could answer the questions that really matter."
