Hey Doug, what's the rush?; NDP, Grits aghast as waiting times for surgeries drop

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hey-doug-whats-rush-ndp-grits-aghast-as-waiting/docview/3064412373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hey+Doug%2C+what%27s+the+rush%3F%3B+NDP%2C+Grits+aghast+as+waiting+times+for+surgeries+drop&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the first year of Ontario expanding access to cataract procedures in community clinics, 32,000 extra people improved their eyesight with quicker access to the procedure. Now, the Ford government wants to take that success and expand it to MRI and CT scans to bring down waiting times.
The province launched a call for applications for new clinics on Monday with new licences to be issued this fall.
"When it comes to wait times for surgeries and procedures, the status quo is not acceptable," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said in a statement announcing the changes.
The Ford government's plan is to roll out faster access for patients who don't need access to a hospital by using community clinics. These clinics have so far been a mixture of public and private organizations, but the patient only pays with their OHIP card, not their credit card.
Still, the usual suspects are outraged that any private-sector involvement exists in health care. They would rather people wait and suffer with ill health than see the private sector involved in any way.
Both NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie claimed Ford was forcing people to pull out their credit card to access health services or to get to the front of the line. That's simply not true and these leaders should be celebrating the success of these programs, not making false claims to scare people away.
Last year, ahead of these changes coming into effect but after the licences for new cataract surgeries had been issued, I interviewed Dr. Anuj Bhargava. In addition to being the chief of ophthalmology at Ottawa's Monfort hospital, Bhargava performed cataract surgery at the Herzig Eye Institute.
"We have only one operating room at the Monfort hospital, which is shared by six surgeons in a week, so each of us has less than one day a week," Bhargava told me in January 2023.
Imagine, the head of eye surgery in a major city hospital gets less than one day of operating room time per week. He described how without getting their eyes fixed, his older patients were losing their independence such as their ability to drive.
Bhargava said expanding the surgeries to community clinics would reduce waiting times, and that is exactly what happened for 32,000 Ontario residents.
The opposition NDP and Liberals would prefer that those 32,000 Ontarians were still on the waiting list and that those behind them were forced to wait longer still for care. As the province looks to expand this model to MRI and CT scans, and eventually endoscopies and hip and knee surgeries, the NDP and Liberals have one goal - make you to wait longer for your care.
To them, keeping a pure but failing system is more important than allowing the private sector to partner with the public health system to deliver you timely care.
"Increasing the number of MRI and CT scans being done each year is the next step we're taking to reduce wait times for more publicly funded surgeries and procedures, ensuring people get the care they need when they need it," minister Jones said.
The goal of her plan is to reduce the time between when an MRI or CT scan is ordered and when the procedure happens to and average of 28 days in every region of the province. Right now, the average wait time for an MRI in Ontario is 90 days while the average for a CT scan is 81 days.
Both of those are unacceptable times to wait.
Ford and Jones are doing something to fix this problem while Stiles and Crombie chirp from the cheap seats to try to maintain the status quo.
Ignore the critics, embrace the change and get your health treatment not only faster, but in a medically acceptable period of time. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Cataracts; Medical imaging; Surgery; Indigent care

Identifier / keyword: first; ontario; expanding; access; cataract; procedures

Document 426 of 500

Conservatives appear to be steaming toward victory

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/conservatives-appear-be-steaming-toward-victory/docview/3064412045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservatives+appear+to+be+steaming+toward+victory&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It must be difficult for those on the left to look at public polling.
The left's dream choice, the handsome, modern, dreamy Justin Trudeau is so far behind Pierre Poilievre that the numbers - unchanged in almost a year - show the Liberals will be devastated in the next election.
The Conservatives are ahead 43% to 25%.
In Ontario, where it seems Conservative Premier Doug Ford careens from one issue to another, often lurching back after making a move, his party polls 11% ahead of the Liberals.
338Canada.com - a polling aggregator - predicts that, if the situation stays as it is federally, the Conservatives, needing 172 seats for a majority, would pick up 212.
Why? First, the economy; the unaffordability of life.
Explosive growth in the size of the federal government contributes to inflation and moves too high a percentage of people from the private sector, which generates all the taxes, to the public sector which is paid by taxes.
The carbon tax makes everything more expensive. But what do we get from the Liberals? A huge carbon tax, with plans to increase it.
I think the Liberals are losing right now because you can't tell average Canadians that the way to deal with the devastation of drug addiction and crime and social ills that come with it are solved by enabling addiction.
Ordinary people I think have had enough of so-called safe injection and supply and parks taken over by people who turn the them into an unsanitary, unsafe environment.
Trudeau enabled a huge number of people to come into the country illegally, winking to Quebec that he'd send them to Toronto. He sent refugees to live on the street in Toronto.
The border matters and immigration levels matter. Ordinary people know that.
The pro-Palestinian protests are rightly seen by many as anti-Semitic and hate-filled.
Yet lefty leaders - especially with the unions - are leaning in, swearing to fight the police and defend those who seem to be more interested in hating on Jews than anything else.
Jews are rightly afraid in Canada. While I believe that elections are generally won and lost on the economy, smaller issues pile up to drive a voter from a party they may have supported.
You can't tell mom and dad they have to accept a man with his male genitalia visible in the locker room and their nine-year-old daughter has to put up with it.
That used to be an arrestable offence.
Or tell them their daughter who worked to excel in a sport that it doesn't matter when confronted by a guy who would rather be number one in the women's category than number 412 in male events.
Ordinary people are smarter than that and peacock strutting by self-important liberals playing the Emperor Has No Clothes, doesn't change basic biology.
It does drive people away from liberalism.
People are tired of a turnstile justice system that seems to favour the criminal over public safety.
Liberals will fight reform in that area.
Again, it is mostly about the economy, and if Conservative Leaded Pierre Poilievre and Ford are smart - and I think they are - they will stick to housing, groceries and perhaps crime.
They should let the other stuff bubble along, making it all the easier for a person who might have voted Liberal in the past to go Conservative this time.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Conservatism; Liberalism; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Way to go doug

Letters to the Editor, June 4, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-june-4-2024/docview/3064307091/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+June+4%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: ALL FOR NOTHING
Just imagine what life will be like in 2035 with nothing but EV vehicles. Without siren noise and honks, living downtown will be a deafening silence. You could even hear a pin drop. Now for all you Formula One and NASCAR fans, picture this: Huge vehicles carrying huge lithium batteries, with not enough power to speed over 100 km/h, all in complete silence. At least you will be able to have a conversation with your neighbour, wife or husband without noise interference. Thank you, Justin, for nothing.
Sid Snider
Port Hope
(The silence could be appealing, but the facts still remain, EVs are out of price for many consumers. We cannot kill our oil and gas industry, and where exactly are all the parts coming from for all the EVs?)
LOTS OF PROBLEMS, NO SOLUTIONS
Being an average citizen, I see a couple of problems with our current Ontario and federal governments. The continuous spending in the millions of dollars, when our taxes are paying off just the outstanding interest and not touching one penny off the principal. Secondly, the current immigration rules are not working. Many people arriving do not possess any of the trades required to build the promised homes by both governments. This current action does not bode well for my four grandsons' future.
Wayne Smith
Scarborough
(We have lost count of just how many things are broken in this great country. With no political will to fix any of it, the future is problematic)
WAY TO GO DOUG
Re "Ford's on the case" (Brian Lilley, May 25): Good for Doug Ford. I, for one, will be raising a toast to him. Ford, whether you like him or dislike him, has openly tried to work towards making his promises happen. Some he hits and some he misses, but in this case, it took some creative strategies and a $225-million deal with The Beer Store, Labatt, Molson and Sleeman. So, raise a glass and enjoy responsibly, but let's not forget who it was that created this stranglehold on the selling of alcohol in your friendly neighbourhood stores. It was the wonderful Kathleen Wynne, another genius member of the Liberal Party. As the Toronto Sun's Steve Simmons would say, "And whatever happened to Kathleen Wynne ..."
Michael McNutt
Mississauga
(It was going to be a Herculean task to unwind the mess the Liberals created)

Ford pledges to tackle hospital wait times

Title: LILLEY: Doug Ford reduces surgical wait times, opposition complains

Document 430 of 500

AGAR: Conservatives appear to be steaming toward victory

Author: Pollard, Dave

Publication info: Pollard, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/agar-conservatives-appear-be-steaming-toward/docview/3064275701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=AGAR%3A+Conservatives+appear+to+be+steaming+toward+victory&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Pollard%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It must be difficult for those on the left to look at public polling.
The left's dream choice, the handsome, modern, dreamy Justin Trudeau is so far behind Pierre Poilievre that the numbers - unchanged in almost a year - show the Liberals will be devastated in the next election.
The Conservatives are ahead 43% to 25%.
In Ontario, where it seems Conservative Premier Doug Ford careens from one issue to another, often lurching back after making a move, his party polls 11% ahead of the Liberals.
338Canada.com - a polling aggregator - predicts that, if the situation stays as it is federally, the Conservatives, needing 172 seats for a majority, would pick up 212.
Why?
First, the economy; the unaffordability of life.
Explosive growth in the size of the federal government contributes to inflation and moves too high a percentage of people from the private sector, which generates all the taxes, to the public sector which is paid by taxes.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
The carbon tax makes everything more expensive.
But what do we get from the Liberals?
A huge carbon tax, with plans to increase it.
I think the Liberals are losing right now because you can't tell average Canadians that the way to deal with the devastation of drug addiction and crime and social ills that come with it are solved by enabling addiction.
Ordinary people I think have had enough of so-called safe injection and supply and parks taken over by people who turn the them into an unsanitary, unsafe environment.
Trudeau enabled a huge number of people to come into the country illegally, winking to Quebec that he'd send them to Toronto. He sent refugees to live on the street in Toronto.
The border matters and immigration levels matter. Ordinary people know that.
The pro-Palestinian protests are rightly seen by many as anti-Semitic and hate-filled.
Yet lefty leaders - especially with the unions - are leaning in, swearing to fight the police and defend those who seem to be more interested in hating on Jews than anything else.
Jews are rightly afraid in Canada.
While I believe that elections are generally won and lost on the economy, smaller issues pile up to drive a voter from a party they may have supported.
You can't tell mom and dad they have to accept a man with his male genitalia visible in the locker room and their nine-year-old daughter has to put up with it.
That used to be an arrestable offence.
Or tell them their daughter who worked to excel in a sport that it doesn't matter when confronted by a guy who would rather be number one in the women's category than number 412 in male events.
Ordinary people are smarter than that and peacock strutting by self-important liberals playing the Emperor Has No Clothes, doesn't change basic biology.
It does drive people away from liberalism.
People are tired of a turnstile justice system that seems to favour the criminal over public safety.
Liberals will fight reform in that area.
Again, it is mostly about the economy, and if Conservative Leaded Pierre Poilievre and Ford are smart - and I think they are - they will stick to housing, groceries and perhaps crime.
They should let the other stuff bubble along, making it all the easier for a person who might have voted Liberal in the past to go Conservative this time.

Subject: Conservatism; Liberalism; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 431 of 500

Voters know better; Mississaugans have 3 candidates to consider instead of Parrish

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-know-better-mississaugans-have-3/docview/3064016201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+know+better%3B+Mississaugans+have+3+candidates+to+consider+instead+of+Parrish&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Carolyn Parrish is elected mayor of Mississauga next week, it will be due only to name recognition.
Hopefully, one of the contenders challenging her is able to win the day, and the mayor's chair, by putting forward the kind of policies the city needs.
If Parrish is leading, it's only because she's been hanging around Mississauga politics for far too long. Mississauga needs a new voice and no one can claim that Parrish is a new, or welcome, voice.
She was the Liberal MP for the area from 1993 until 2004, when she was turfed from the Liberal caucus and forced to sit as an Independent. Parrish was ejected from the Liberal caucus after expressing harsh criticism of then-U.S. president George W. Bush and told then-prime minister Paul Martin that he could "go to hell" if he didn't like what she said.
She was a Mississauga councillor from 2006 until 2010, lost her re-election bid and then lost a 2011 byelection to Bonnie Crombie. In 2014, she won a council seat by replacing Crombie, who was elected mayor.
Parrish has the support of 29% of decided voters in the city, according to a survey by Liaison Strategies.
That's five points ahead of her nearest competitor, but is a drop from 37% support a month earlier.
There are also still 16% of voters who are undecided, and Parrish's nine-point drop shows voters aren't necessarily cemented in whom to vote for. With low voter turnout, the right candidate could turn things around and steal the day.
Dipika Damerla is the candidate currently in second, according to Liaison, with 24% support. She was a Liberal MPP and cabinet minister under Kathleen Wynne who ran successfully for council after losing in the 2018 provincial election.
Her big winning promise is to dump the bike lane extension on Bloor St. in Mississauga. That's likely to be seen as "controversial" among "experts" and the chattering classes, but popular with voters who live there and who have to drive through the area.
She's also appealing to NIMBY voters by saying that any growth in Mississauga must be the right type of growth. When most politicians are pitching to voters with a promise of more housing to tackle affordability, Damerla is pitching to existing homeowners worried about their home values.
Alvin Tedjo, in third with 19% of decided voters, is pitching to homeowners upset at the rising property tax rates. He is pledging to freeze property taxes until at least 2026 if elected mayor, calling it an affordability pledge.
Tedjo can already point to voting to cut taxes at city hall twice and a successful move to legalize playing street hockey and basketball during his short time on council.
Issues like playing street hockey or basketball may seem small, but the bans imposed by bureaucrats are the types of issues that drive residents crazy when it comes to dealing with city hall.
Stephen Dasko is the dark horse to defeat Parrish. According to Liaison, he has just 12% support. But given recent moves in polling and a high number of undecided voters, he has an outside chance.
Dasko is promising to work on affordability and community safety, specifically pointing to auto thefts, gun violence and street racing. He is promising to reduce the speed limit on residential streets and to review the Bloor St. bike lanes.
Like the others, Dasko has been around politics for a while. He worked for years at Queen's Park with the Progressive Conservatives but also has spent time in the private sector, including as chief operating officer for a tech firm.
With a week to go before the June 10 election, voters still have a chance to make up their minds. Any of the three other candidates - out of a total field of 20 - would make a far better choice for mayor than Parrish. It's time for voters to look at the candidates and see which one aligns with their priorities.
Voting for a candidate just because you remember their name - and for all the wrong reasons - isn't the best way to elect a mayor.
Parrish has name recognition, but not for the right reasons. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Provincial elections; Tax rates; Councils; Candidates; Voters; Elections; Voter behavior

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates

Identifier / keyword: carolyn; parrish; elected; mayor; mississauga; recognition

Title: Voters know better; Mississaugans have 3 candidates to consider instead of Parrish

Document 432 of 500

Next mayor's top targets? Affordability, housing, crime

Author: Spiro Papuckoski

Publication info: Spiro Papuckoski. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/next-mayors-top-targets-affordability-housing/docview/3064016149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Next+mayor%27s+top+targets%3F+Affordability%2C+housing%2C+crime&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Spiro+Papuckoski&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In one week's time, the residents of Mississauga will elect their third mayor in nearly half a century.
Following Hazel McCallion's 36 years in the mayor's chair and Bonnie Crombie's decade in office, residents will head to the polls June 10 in a special byelection. The vote is required because Crombie became leader of the Ontario Liberal Party in December and resigned from office in January.
Interest is high for the job as a record 20 candidates entered the race, including Carolyn Parrish, who resigned her council seat to run; current councillors Dipika Damerla, Stephen Dasko and Alvin Tedjo; and Brian Crombie, a real estate development company COO and exhusband of the former mayor.
According to a recent poll conducted by Liaison Strategies for the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada, Parrish is leading all candidates with 29% support of decided voters and 25% of all voters, Global News reports.
Damerla is closing in on Parrish with 24% of overall and 20% of decided voters backing her, with Tedjo and Dasko not far behind. The poll also shows support for Brian Crombie is in the high single digits.
The list of issues facing the city includes the affordability and housing crisis, rising vehicle thefts and traffic congestion.
Parrish says she aims to improve the lives of residents by keeping property tax increases at or below the cost of living.
She also pledges to work toward making Mississaugans feel safer by hiring more police officers when necessary, and to lobby for bail reform and harsher penalties for vehicle thefts.
"While still rated as one of Canada's safest large cities, the recent doubling of car thefts with the associated incidents of break-ins and assaults has made our citizens feel vulnerable," her campaign website states.
She also promises to improve public transportation and grow ridership, while holding consultations when streets are set to be repaved to evaluate the need for speed humps and cycling curbs.
Damerla's plan as mayor incorporates a focus on law and order that would include looking at bringing back community police stations, and a $10,000 Crime Stoppers reward for tips that lead to a conviction for auto theft. She also pledges to advocate for auto theft to be treated as organized crime and for bail reform.
Damerla also stresses the need for smarter growth in the city.
"Over the next decade, Mississauga is expected to grow rapidly as we address the acute housing shortage and build more homes," her campaign website says. "This growth comes with a risk. If it is done wrong, it will make our city unlivable. Growth done right, however, will make our city both affordable and livable."
Damerla also opposes the plan to remove vehicle lanes on Bloor St. to make way for bikes.
"Let me be very clear - I am not ideological about bike lanes," she says. "Bike lanes make sense in some places and don't make sense in others. Removing car lanes on this stretch of Bloor St. will create chaos and congestion."
A fellow councillor, Tedjo says his priority is to help make living in Mississauga more affordable by imposing a property tax freeze until 2026, to provide residents 65 years or older with a property tax rebate of $420 if their household income is lower than $60,000, and to reduce property taxes for small businesses by 15%.
"Young professionals looking to call Mississauga home, seniors looking to age in place, families with kids like mine, we are all feeling the price of everything go up and there does not seem to be any relief," Tedjo's campaign website states.
He has a plan to ease the housing crisis by advocating for missing middle housing, adding density in key core areas, allowing homes on underused lands, and cutting red tape to speed up planning approvals.
He also promises to create a municipal task force to fight auto theft, while working with other levels of government to crack down on crime.
Meanwhile, Dasko pledges to make Mississauga affordable, safe and livable.
"People are struggling with the cost of living and the city needs to do its part to restore affordability," his campaign website says.
He plans to reduce fees for community groups and seniors, keep tax increases below the rate of inflation, and audit city departments to ensure savings.
He also promises to create a permanent crime prevention committee, support Neighbourhood Watch programs, work to prevent auto thefts and put pressure on the federal government to stop repeat offenders from getting bail.
Brian Crombie says Mississauga is in a housing crisis because city councillors "have lacked the political courage to lead us in doing what is right."
He is campaigning on removing red tape, expediting approvals, increasing density around transit nodes and major arterials, and allowing for "missing middle" development.
Crombie also says the city is experiencing a crime spree, with burglaries, car thefts and gun violence on the rise.
Xiao Hua "Edward" Gong, who ran for mayor in Toronto last year with his ubiquitous campaign signs promising "to rescue Toronto," is also a candidate.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Automobile theft; Cost of living; Crime prevention; Cost control; Robbery; Voters; Property taxes; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living Cost control Property taxes Tax increases

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: residents; mississauga; elect; third; mayor; nearly

Title: Next mayor's top targets? Affordability, housing, crime

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 3, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document 434 of 500

LILLEY: Three candidates Mississauga voters should consider instead of Parrish

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-three-candidates-mississauga-voters-should/docview/3063818571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Three+candidates+Mississauga+voters+should+consider+instead+of+Parrish&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Carolyn Parrish is elected mayor of Mississauga next week, it will only be due to name recognition. Hopefully, one of the contenders challenging her is able to win the day, and the mayor's chair, by putting forward the kind of policies the city needs.
If Parrish is leading, it's only because she's been hanging around Mississauga politics for far too long. Mississauga needs a new voice and no one can claim that Parrish is a new, or welcome voice.
She was the Liberal MP for the area from 1993 until 2004 when she was turfed from the Liberal caucus and forced to sit as an independent. Parrish was ejected from the Liberal caucus after expressing harsh criticism of then-U.S. president George W. Bush and told then-prime minister Paul Martin that he could "go to hell" if he didn't like what she said.
She was a Mississauga councillor from 2006 until 2010, lost her re-election bid and then lost a 2011 byelection to Bonnie Crombie. In 2014, she won a council seat by replacing Crombie who was elected mayor.
Parrish has the support of 29% of decided voters in the city, according to a survey by Liaison Strategies. That's five points ahead of her nearest competitor, but is a drop from 37% support a month earlier.
There are also still 16% of voters who are undecided, and Parrish's nine-point drop shows that voters aren't necessarily cemented in whom to vote for. With low voter turnout, the right candidate could turn things around and steal the day.
Dipika Damerla is the candidate currently in second, according to Liaison, with 24% support. She was a Liberal MPP and cabinet minister under Kathleen Wynne who ran successfully for council after losing in the 2018 provincial election.
Her big winning promise is to dump the bike lane extension on Bloor St in Mississauga. That's likely to be seen as "controversial" among "experts" and the chattering classes, but popular with voters who live there and who have to drive through the area.
You have another chance to vote ahead of election day this weekend June 1-2.
If you need any assistance feel free to call my office at 905-274-7161
More information on poll locations can be found at:
- Dipika Damerla (@DipikaDamerla) June 1, 2024
She's also appealing to NIMBY voters by saying that any growth in Mississauga must be the right type of growth. When most politicians are pitching to voters with a promise of more housing to tackle affordability, Damerla is pitching to existing homeowners worried about their home values.
Alvin Tedjo, in third with 19% of decided voters, is pitching to homeowners upset at the rising property tax rates. He is pledging to freeze property taxes until at least 2026 if elected mayor, calling it an affordability pledge.
Tedjo can already point to voting to cut taxes at city hall twice and a successful move to legalize playing street hockey and basketball during his short time on council. Issues like playing street hockey or basketball may seem small, but the bans imposed by bureaucrats are the types of issues that drive residents crazy when it comes to dealing with city hall.
3 Hours Left to Vote Early!
We have a choice this election
I want to build a city where everyone can find a home
Where you have options in how to get around
Where property taxes are frozen for the next 2 years
- Alvin Tedjo (@AlvinTedjo) June 2, 2024
Stephen Dasko is the dark horse to defeat Parrish; according to Liaison, he has just 12% support. But given recent moves in polling and a high number of undecided voters, he has an outside chance.
Dasko is promising to work on affordability and community safety, specifically pointing to auto thefts, gun violence and street racing. He is promising to reduce the speed limit on residential streets and to review the Bloor St bike lanes.
Recommended video
Like the others, Dasko has been around politics for a while; he worked for years at Queen's Park with the Progressive Conservatives but also has spent time in the private sector, including as chief operating officer for a tech company.
With a week to go before the June 10 election, voters still have a chance to make up their minds.
Any of the three other candidates - out of a total field of 20 - would make a far better choice for mayor than Parrish. It's time for voters to look at the candidates and see which one aligns with their priorities.
Voting for a candidate just because you remember their name, for all the wrong reasons, isn't the best way to elect a mayor.
Parrish has name recognition, but not for the right reasons.

Glued To Youtube?; Try these 6 tips, tricks, hacks and secrets

Author: Saltzman, Marc

Publication info: Saltzman, Marc. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/glued-youtube-try-these-6-tips-tricks-hacks/docview/3063216267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Glued+To+Youtube%3F%3B+Try+these+6+tips%2C+tricks%2C+hacks+and+secrets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=Saltzman%2C+Marc&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's official: YouTube is now old enough to drink across Canada.
Turning 19 on Valentine's Day, YouTube - now with 2.7 billion monthly active users - has evolved into the second largest social media platform on the planet, slightly behind Facebook's alleged 2.9 billion users.
You're probably aware YouTube is owned by Google's parent company Alphabet and that it offers short and longform videos of just about anything, which you can watch on several devices.
You likely heard of (or maybe subscribe to) YouTube Premium (from $12.99/month), which lets you watch millions of videos without ads before and during a video.
Also included with the subscription is YouTube Music Premium, to stream more than 100 million songs ad-free, offline, plus you can also watch videos on the YouTube Kids app without ads.
But there's so much more to YouTube, including many tips and tricks to getting the most out of the service - whether it's "official" things you can do, such as the company's own shortcuts, or thirdparty "hacks" to enhance your binging sessions.
And so, the following are a halfdozen things to try. ENSURE YOU'RE LOGGED IN While not required, it's better to sign into YouTube (with a Google account) as any playlist you create will be synchronized between all devices.
Signing in also lets you like and comment on videos, share them to social media, upload your own content, subscribe to channels you like, and report or block videos you don't like. MASTER YOUTUBE SHORTCUTS If you watch YouTube on a computer, you can do more in less time by learning a few shortcuts.
There are nearly 25 of them, but here are some examples: n Spacebar: Play/Pause. n M: Mute/Unmute. n Left/right arrow: Seek backwards/forward five seconds. n > and <: Speed up or slowdown playback. n Up/down arrow: Increase/decrease volume by 5%.
There are some YouTube shortcuts on a mobile device, too, such as double-tapping on the left or right side of the video on a phone or tablet to skip forward and back 10 seconds, respectively. DOWNLOAD YOUTUBE VIDEOS If you know you're going to be offline for a while - such as a long flight and you don't want to pay for Wi-Fi - you can download videos to watch later.
The official way to do this is to join the above mentioned YouTube Premium, as downloading videos is one of the benefits to the service.
Not endorsed by Google, there are several websites and apps that let you download YouTube videos to view offline.
One of the fastest ways is to simply add "pp" after the word "YouTube" in a web browser, which takes you to a website to download the video.
Note that if you download a video to watch offline, content creators and/or rights holders cannot monetize your views. CREATE A .GIF FROM A VIDEO Another unofficial hack is to create an animated .gif from any part of a YouTube video.
As you likely know, .gifs, which are silent and played in a loop, are fun to post on social media.
Go to MakeaGif.com and click on "YouTube to Gif."
Now, copy and paste the YouTube video link into the search window.
After it finishes processing, you can choose the quality, length, speed and what the start and end time is.
While free, an account is required to create a .gif using this tool, and you'll have a small watermark unless you pay for a Premium account.
Please respect copyrights, and only use your own YouTube videos to create .gifs.
SHARE VIDEO FROM SPECIFIC SPOT If you want to share a YouTube video at a precise point, it's easy to do this on a PC or Mac.
Click the Share button and look for the "Starts at" box below the link and it will show the time at which you currently have the video stopped (such as 1:15).
Choose this or select another time you want - when the recipient gets the link and clicks "Play," it will start exactly where you wanted it to. SEARCH - BEFORE YOU GET THERE Why do something that takes two steps when you can do it in one? Rather than going to YouTube. com and then searching for something to watch using YouTube's search window, do it all in your favourite web browser to watch the same video.
For instance, in Google Search, Bing, Yahoo! or DuckDuckGo, type something like A Quiet Place: Day One trailer YouTube, and you'll see the video thumbnail in your search engine's results - which you can click to play it right then and there.
@marc_saltzman

Subject: Web browsers; Video recordings; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Identifier / keyword: official; youtube; enough; drink; canada; turning

Title: Glued To Youtube?; Try these 6 tips, tricks, hacks and secrets

Document 436 of 500

Liberals resorting to 'climate porn'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-resorting-climate-porn/docview/3063216266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+resorting+to+%27climate+porn%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's okay, kids, just ignore what Canada's uber-scary health minister said last week.
In no way, will you be destroying the planet if you go on a road trip with your parents this summer.
This unhinged rhetorical outburst by Health Minister Mark Holland in the House of Commons in response to a question about high gasoline prices from the Conservatives, simply reveals what desperate times these are for the federal Liberals.
Far behind in the polls, their latest tactic is to accuse anyone who opposes their carbon tax of wanting to let the planet burn or, as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau puts it, to let the country burn.
But whether you agree with Trudeau's carbon tax, it isn't going to stop the planet or Canada from burning, to use the Liberals'silly rhetoric.
That's because Canada's contribution of industrial greenhouse gas - about 1.6% of the global total - isn't significant enough to materially impact climate change.
For that to happen, the world's largest emitters such as China, the U.S. and Russia will have to co-operate globally to reduce emissions.
There's a name for what Trudeau, Holland and other Liberals are doing.
They're spouting "climate porn", a term first used in August, 2006 by the UK's Institute for Public Policy and Research, a progressive think tank.
In their paper, "Warm Words: How are we telling the climate change story and can we tell it better?" authors Gill Ereaut and Nat Segnit concluded: "Climate change is most commonly constructed through the alarmist repertoire - as awesome, terrible, immense and beyond human control. This repertoire is seen everywhere and is used or drawn on from across the ideological spectrum, in broadsheets and tabloids, in popular magazines and in campaign literature from government initiatives and environmental groups. It is typified by an inflated or extreme lexicon, incorporating an urgent tone and cinematic codes.
"It employs a quasi-religious register of death and doom, and it uses language of acceleration and irreversibility. The difficulty with it is that the scale of the problem as it is shown excludes the possibility of real action ... It contains an implicit counsel of despair - 'the problem is just too big for us to take on'."
That's why the Liberals should stop scaring the children.

Subject: Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: ignore; health; minister; destroying; planet; parents

Full text: What this unidentified woman was holding in her hand at the Little Gaza encampment on the University of Toronto's downtown campus is unknown at this time.
However, Toronto Police say they are "aware" of a strange video from Friday showing a woman pulling down posters from the fence of the encampment and then appearing to display what onlookers described as a knife.
Police have not said it was determined to be knife and at this point no charges have been laid.
"We are aware of this occurrence," Const. Caroline de Kloet said Saturday. "No charges have been laid at this time. This is an ongoing investigation."
The video was posted to X by the National Council of Canadian Muslims who wrote "a knife was brandished by a pro-Israel protester against those standing for Palestinian human rights at the University of Toronto. This is hate. This is violence. This is criminal. This needs to stop."

The woman can then be seen placing whatever was in her hand into her purse before a U of T security person and a Toronto Police officer pull her away to speak with her.

SALTZMAN: Watch YouTube? Try these 6 tips, tricks, hacks, and secrets

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/saltzman-watch-youtube-try-these-6-tips-tricks/docview/3063092674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SALTZMAN%3A+Watch+YouTube%3F+Try+these+6+tips%2C+tricks%2C+hacks%2C+and+secrets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's official: YouTube is now old enough to drink across Canada.
Turning 19 on Valentine's Day, YouTube - now with 2.7 billion monthly active users - has evolved into the second largest social media platform on the planet, slightly behind Facebook's alleged 2.9 billion users and <: Speed up or slowdown playback
Up/down arrow: Increase/decrease volume by 5%
There are some YouTube shortcuts on a mobile device, too, such as double-tapping on the left or right side of the video on a phone or tablet to skip forward and back 10 seconds, respectively.
YouTube offers users short and longform videos of just about anything that can be viewed on various devices.
DOWNLOAD YOUTUBE VIDEOS
If you know you're going to be offline for a while - such as a long flight and you don't want to pay for Wi-Fi - you can download videos to watch later.
The official way to do this is to join the abovementioned YouTube Premium, as downloading videos is one of the benefits to the service.
Not endorsed by Google, there are several websites and apps that let you download YouTube videos to view offline. One of the fastest ways is to simply add "pp" after the word "youtube" in a web browser, which takes you to a website to download the video.
For example, YUNGBLUD's impressive Kiss cover for "I Was Made For Lovin' You" is at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gaD55NqPJA0, but it would then become https://www.youtubepp.com/watch?v=gaD55NqPJA0 Note that if you download a video to watch offline, content creators and/or rights holders cannot monetize your views.
YouTube offers users short and longform videos of just about anything that can be viewed on various devices.
CREATE A .GIF FROM A YOUTUBE VIDEO
Another unofficial hack is to create an animated .gif from any part of a YouTube video.
As you likely know, .gifs, which are silent and played in a loop, are fun to post on social media.
Go to MakeaGif.com and click on "YouTube to Gif." Now, copy and paste the YouTube video link into the search window. After it finishes processing, you can choose the quality, length, speed, and what the start and end time is.
While free, an account is required to create a .gif using this tool, and you'll have a small watermark unless you pay for a Premium account.
Please respect copyrights, and only use your own YouTube videos to create .gifs.
YouTube offers users short and longform videos of just about anything that can be viewed on various devices.
SHARE VIDEO FROM A SPECIFIC SPOT
If you want to share a YouTube video at a precise point, it's easy to do this on a PC or Mac.
Click the Share button and look for the "Starts at" box below the link and it will show the time at which you currently have the video stopped (such as 1:15).
Choose this or select another time you want - when the recipient gets the link and clicks "Play," it will start exactly where you wanted it to.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
SEARCH YOUTUBE - BEFORE YOU GET THERE
Why do something that takes two steps when you can do it in one?
Rather than going to YouTube.com and then searching for something to watch using YouTube's search window, do it all in your favourite web browser to watch the same video.
For instance, in Google Search, Bing, Yahoo!, or DuckDuckGo, type something like "A Quiet Place: Day One trailer YouTube," and you'll see the video thumbnail in your search engine's results - which you can click to play it right then and there.

Subject: Web browsers; Video recordings; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Identifier / keyword: official; youtube; enough; drink; canada; turning

Document 440 of 500

Bonnie's in-tax-icated; Liberal booze claims include $300M pink elephant

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bonnies-tax-icated-liberal-booze-claims-include/docview/3062799179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie%27s+in-tax-icated%3B+Liberal+booze+claims+include+%24300M+pink+elephant&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Bonnie Crombie had her way, we wouldn't be moving to beer and wine in corner and grocery stores. Or, if we did, she'd impose taxes and fees so steep as to make the system unaffordable and doomed to fail.
The Liberals put out a news release on Monday making the claim that Premier Doug Ford's plan to put beer and wine in convenience stores is going to cost the province $1 billion. A quick look at Crombie's claim and her breakdown of the costs shows it's not true, but she keeps repeating it.
Apparently, Crombie comes from the Trudeau Liberal school of communications.
"If you repeat it, if you say it louder, if that is your talking point, people will totally believe it!" Catherine McKenna, Trudeau's former environment minister, once said.
Crombie is following the same pattern of saying it louder, repeating it and making it her talking point with this $1-billion claim.
The Liberal leader is claiming the province will lose $375 million because a fee the LCBO imposed when the Wynne Liberals put beer into 450 grocery stores won't be extended to beer sold in the estimated 8,500 other stores. The fee was invented out of whole cloth by the LCBO and wasn't needed. Crombie's claim of $375 million lost is based on a media report that extrapolates that fee across all new stores.
Dropping this unnecessary fee was a key ask of the craft brewing industry, which also lobbied hard for getting their products into convenience stores while Molson and Labatt opposed the idea.
She also claims the province will lose $150 million by telling the LCBO to offer a 10% wholesale discount to non-government retailers. In many instances, that 10% discount will result in you getting beer or wine at cheaper prices. In other cases, it will mean the local retailer can make a living.
Either way, Crombie is demanding higher prices by insisting there be no discount to help get this new sales chain off the ground.
Finally, Crombie claims, out of nowhere, that the province is forgoing $300 million in revenue by not charging a licence fee for convenience stores or grocers to carry booze in their stores. The province is charging a licensing fee for any new retailer selling alcohol, but it's not as big as Crombie wants.
To "prove" her point, Crombie points to Saskatchewan, where licence fees went for as much as $450,000. I'm not sure that Crombie has spent much time in Saskatchewan, but the government there closed all their liquor stores as part of privatization.
Is she asking the Ford government to shut down the LCBO and let the private sector run all aspects of this sector? Given her tax-and-spend comments, that is doubtful.
Crombie is advocating that independently owned convenience stores pay as much as $50,000 to the government to be able to sell beer and wine and that grocery stores pay as much as $100,000. A fee that size would make it unaffordable for the independent owners of these stores to enter the market, meaning we'd be left with the status quo.
The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario is required by law to charge licensing fees on a cost recovery basis, but Crombie wants to use this government agency as a profit centre.
There is enough to be upset about with the Ford government paying The Beer Store $225 million without inventing new piles of money to create the false idea that the province is losing $1 billion.
Crombie's claims, repeated without any fact check by many in the media, also don't look at new revenue being generated.
The Convenience Industry Council of Canada projects 7,000 to 7,500 new jobs will be created by this change. The study also estimates $213 million in annual tax payments being generated with 69%, or $147 million annually, going to the provincial government.
The province estimates the LCBO will gain somewhere between $895 million to $1.2 billion from being the exclusive wholesaler to these 8,500 new stores.
We can have a debate about the merits of Ford's plan, including the timing and the $225 million to The Beer Store, without resorting to spreading false and exaggerated claims. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: New store openings; Beer; Convenience stores; Cost recovery; Fees & charges; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: New store openings Convenience stores Cost recovery Grocery stores

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; crombie; moving; corner; grocery; stores

Document 441 of 500

Terrorizers, 'don't come'; Preem speaks passionately, plainly - and correctly

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/terrorizers-dont-come-preem-speaks-passionately/docview/3062799161/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Terrorizers%2C+%27don%27t+come%27%3B+Preem+speaks+passionately%2C+plainly+-+and+correctly&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford just said what many have been thinking since Oct. 7 - but the usual suspects shouting racism didn't waste a second to pounce.
Of course, there was nothing racist about Ford's words. In fact, it was the opposite. He talked about protecting all Ontario citizens from all backgrounds.
"Folks, let's cut to the chase here," he said Thursday at the opening of the $800-million Sanofi vaccine manufacturing plant with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Mayor Olivia Chow. "What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? That's just unacceptable."
He got that right. "Imagine a little kid in a school because they are of the Jewish faith (and) someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows," Ford said. "These guys need to be caught. They need to be punished. They need to be thrown in jail."
"I will tell you we have zero tolerance for this anywhere in Ontario. It doesn't matter what race, what creed, what religion you are from. I would be saying the exact same thing if it were another community as well. Enough is enough."
He nailed that part too. But this quote brought the pitchforks out: "You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing it to Ontario, and you're going after other Canadians - as the prime minister said, it's unacceptable," said Ford. "I got an idea: before you plan on moving to Canada, don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this. Simple as that."
"You want to come to Canada? You want to be a resident of Ontario? You get along with everyone. I don't care what background, what race you come from," the premier added.
Trudeau said the same thing, but in more politically correct language.
"The core thing that we have continued to call for is an end to hatred and intolerance in Canada," the prime minister said. "Whether it's anti-Semitism or Islamophobia, we are seeing events overseas that are devastating and incredibly difficult to watch and shouldn't be spilling over to Canadians hating on other Canadians (and) we shouldn't be attacking each other."
Fair comments from both leaders.
There's nothing wrong with reminding people coming to live in this great country that there is no tolerance for shooting up a Jewish school or an Islamic mosque, both of which this country has, regrettably, endured.
Somehow, it was conflated in media reports that Ford was suggesting immigrants were responsible for shooting the windows of the Bais Chaya Mushka elementary school for girls. He didn't say that.
"They were different thoughts," his spokesman Ivana Yelich explained. "That's the premier. He was talking about his frustration with the violence and was just venting."
When you have months of Jewish targets being attacked - a bookstore, grocery store, comedy club, restaurant, schools - it's understandable the premier would be fed up.
But many didn't like it, and they seem to be more upset about what the premier said than they were by the actual shooting.
"Fighting hate with hate has never worked," Opposition NDP Leader Marit Stiles posted to X. "Fighting anti-Semitism with xenophobia won't keep communities safe. I'm appalled by the premier's racist remarks. He should apologize."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said: "He's inflaming anti-immigrant sentiment without any proof to back up the claims he's making today. That is completely irresponsible. It's beneath the dignity of a premier, and I believe the premier should apologize."
Restaurateur and philanthropist Mohamad Fakih posted to X: "This type of xenophobic nonsense is nuts" and Ford's "implication here on those you stereotype as responsible is dangerous and has real life consequences on the safety of members of my community" and "you must withdraw your cowardly and racist comment."
These are low blows from political opponents who know Ford doesn't have a racist bone in his body and said nothing racist. Most news stories also cut out a lot of what Ford said. Watch the video on torontosun.com and judge for yourself.
"Diversity is our No. 1 selling tool around the world," Ford said at that same podium, adding there are "110 nationalities around Ontario and 200 languages being spoken and guess what, 99% of the people get along."
"There's wars going on all around the world, but we still get along. I have just had it up to here," he added.
Reacting to Chow earnestly talking about installing "shatterproof" glass, Ford made the excellent point asking, "We have to have this in Ontario in schools? Like, where have we gone? Again, we are all fed up with this nonsense, I will tell you that. It should not be this way."
Under fire, Ford took to X to clarify his comments.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us," the premier posted. "While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province."
He's right - but he was right the first time, too. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Antisemitism; Racism; Schools; Race; Xenophobia; Prime ministers

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: ontario; premier; thinking; usual; suspects; shouting

Abstract: None available.

The province is charging a licensing fee for any new retailer selling alcohol but it's not as big as Crombie wants.
To "prove" her point, Crombie points to Saskatchewan, where licence fees went for as much as $450,000. I'm not sure that Crombie has spent much time in Saskatchewan, but the government there closed all their liqour stores as part of privatization.
Is she asking the Ford government to shut down the LCBO and let the private sector run all aspects of this sector? Given her tax and spend comments, that is doubtful.
Crombie is advocating that independently owned convenience stores pay as much as $50,000 to the government to be able to sell beer and wine and that grocery stores pay as much as $100,000. A fee that size would make it unaffordable for the independent owners of these stores to enter the market, meaning we'd be left with the status quo.
The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario is required by law to charge licensing fees on a cost recovery basis, Crombie wants to use this government agency as a profit centre.
There is enough to be upset about with the Ford government paying The Beer Store $225 million without inventing new piles of money to create the false idea that the province is losing $1 billion.
Crombie's claims, repeated without any fact check by many in the media, also don't look at new revenue being generated.
The Convenience Industry Council of Canada projects 7,000 to 7,500 new jobs will be created by this change. The study also estimates $213 million in annual tax payments being generated with 69%, or $147 million annually, going to the provincial government.
The province estimates that the LCBO will gain somewhere between $895 billion to $1.2 billion from being the exclusive wholesaler to these 8,500 new stores.
We can have a debate about the merits of Ford's plan, including the timing and the $225 million to The Beer Store, without resorting to spreading false and exaggerated claims.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: New store openings; Beer; Convenience stores; Cost recovery; Fees & charges; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: New store openings Convenience stores Cost recovery Grocery stores

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; crombie; moving; corner; grocery; stores

Document 445 of 500

Poilievre pulls ahead; Tories lead Liberals in Quebec: Poll

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-pulls-ahead-tories-lead-liberals-quebec/docview/3062321008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+pulls+ahead%3B+Tories+lead+Liberals+in+Quebec%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are tied for first place among voters in Quebec and ahead of Justin Trudeau's Liberals? Ben voyons donc! It may sound impossible, but the latest Leger poll for Postmedia not only gives Poilievre's Tories a massive lead nationally but a position they haven't held in Quebec in decades.
According to Leger, the most accurate pollster in the last two elections, the Conservatives and Bloc Quebecois are tied at 29% voter support in Quebec, with the Liberals behind at 26%.
In the last federal election, the Tories took just 19% of the vote and 10 seats; that's the same number of seats they took in 2006 with 25% of the vote.
The Conservative support is most often concentrated in the Quebec City region and places like the Saguenay, but could see them land extra seats if they were to land 29% support and come out ahead of the Liberals.
By comparison, the Liberals took about 34% of the vote in Quebec in the last election and took 35 seats.
Quebec has been a stronghold for Trudeau's Liberal party since he first won power in 2015.
It was Quebec that first turned to him when voters decided to vote out the Stephen Harper Conservatives and as Quebec put support behind Trudeau, leftleaning voters in the rest of the country followed suit.
Nationally, the Leger poll puts the Tories at 42% support to 23% for the Liberals and 18% for the New Democratic Party.
The Conservatives lead in this poll among men and women, in every age bracket and among rural, suburban and even urban voters.
While being tied for first in Quebec is new and noteworthy, the more important result in the poll is the Tory lead in Ontario, British Columbia and Atlantic Canada. All three regions have been very kind to Trudeau since 2015 and now have turned to the Conservatives.
In Atlantic Canada, where the Trudeau Liberals took 24 of 32 seats in 2021, the Tories are now polling at 50% support.
In B.C., which sent 15 Liberals to Ottawa in the last election, the Conservatives are polling at 46% compared to 23% for the NDP and 20% for the Liberals.
Ontario, home to nearly half the Liberal caucus, is set to go blue in a big way - at least if an election were held now.
In 2021, the Conservatives pulled in about 35% of the vote and 37 seats compared to the Liberals, who took about 39% of the vote and 78 seats.
Leger now has the Tories polling at 42% to the Liberals'24% and NDP's 22%.
With numbers like that, Poilievre and his team would likely get more than 80 seats in Ontario alone, while also picking up seats in B.C., Atlantic Canada and even Quebec.
They already hold every seat in Saskatchewan, but would add a few more in Manitoba and likely pick up one or two of the seats they don't hold in Alberta.
Few Canadians are satisfied with the Trudeau government anymore, with just 27% saying they're satisfied with the government's performance - and just 4% very satisfied and 23% somewhat satisfied. As for best prime minister, Poilievre is chosen by almost twice as many respondents as Trudeau or NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.
Leger also asked voters why they think Trudeau isn't ready to step aside and just 23% believe his claim that he has more policies he wants to implement.
Meanwhile, 47% believe he just likes being PM and doesn't want to leave.
It's an interesting poll that is in line with the national lead for the Conservatives that several polling firms have shown.
What we need to watch for is whether what Leger picked up in Quebec turns into a trend going forward. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Voters

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: pierre; conservatives; first; place; voters; quebec

Document 446 of 500

Catastrophic drug results; T.O.'s overdose numbers bad, city's solutions worse

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catastrophic-drug-results-t-o-s-overdose-numbers/docview/3062320979/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catastrophic+drug+results%3B+T.O.%27s+overdose+numbers+bad%2C+city%27s+solutions+worse&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto saw 523 overdose deaths in 2023. That's an OD death every 36 hours. We've seen an increase in OD deaths of 74% during the last five years and a 280% increase over the past decade.
It's a terrible tragedy and something has to change, but not in the way the people at Toronto Public Health are advocating.
The city's public health officials issued their report for last year and lamented the horrors of the "unregulated drug supply" and "the drug toxicity crisis" while calling for "collaborative solutions."
"This is more than a public health issue - it's a human tragedy that demands we respond with empathy, care and compassion," Dr. Eileen de Villa, Toronto's medical officer of health, said in a statement.
Yes, we should respond with empathy, care and compassion, but the main solutions advocated by de Villa have been a disaster where they've been tried.
It was just four weeks ago that de Villa was still advocating that Toronto seek permission for decriminalization of all hard drugs. She made that call after British Columbia's NDP government had declared its experiment with decriminalization a disaster.
She has also fully embraced so-called safer supply, which - despite opposition from the Ford government at Queen's Park - became a reality in Toronto because of funding from the Trudeau Liberals.
"We are very pleased to have the funding to operate this important program, especially during this time of escalating overdose deaths in the City of Toronto, and believe that it will save lives," de Villa said in April 2021.
Since 2020, the Trudeau government has funded five locations in the city, providing $19 million to operate them. The idea behind safer supply is addicts are given prescription-grade opioids to replace street drugs.
The problem, as has been well documented, is that addicts often sell these drugs to buy stronger drugs like fentanyl and the pills are sold on the street, often to younger users.
In B.C., where decriminalization and safer supply were embraced - similar to what de Villa wants - the results have been catastrophic. Despite embracing every measure sold by the advocates to reduce ODs, deaths from drug consumption have gone up every year, but one, over the past decade.
Last year, B.C. saw in excess of 2,500 OD deaths, more than Ontario, despite having one-third of the population. Between Jan. 1 and March 31, the province of 5 million saw 527 OD deaths, more than Toronto saw all of last year with a population of just over 3 million.
On a per capita basis, B.C. sees roughly three times as many OD deaths as Toronto. Following in B.C.'s footsteps - as de Villa and her colleagues at city hall want - is ludicrous.
Yet, it's there in their statements and even in the language used in this latest report. The activists in charge of drug policy in this city want further liberalization.
"The continued loss of life to the ongoing drug toxicity epidemic is devastating," de Villa said on Wednesday.
Mayor Olivia Chow blamed the OD deaths on the "increased risks of the toxic unregulated drug supply," while Councillor Chris Moise, who chairs the health board, said the city needs to tackle the "drug toxicity epidemic."
The specific choice of those words shows they don't view drug use and addiction as part of the problem, they see the toxic nature of the drugs as the problem. It's not that someone is using heroin or fentanyl, it's that they use dirty street drugs.
This is the language used by those who want a regulated supply of opioids, provided free of charge but paid for by you and me. That's a policy no resident of the city should be willing to accept.
Now that de Villa has said she's stepping down as Toronto's medical officer, it's time for the city to recruit someone who'll base decisions on facts and evidence, not fear and ideology as de Villa has done. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Drugs; Decriminalization; Public health; Toxicity; Decades; Fentanyl; Narcotics

Identifier / keyword: toronto; overdose; deaths; death; hours; increase

Title: Catastrophic drug results; T.O.'s overdose numbers bad, city's solutions worse

Document 447 of 500

Cherry wood; Great big Grapes brings huge smiles to Trans-Canada - Sir John A. next?

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cherry-wood-great-big-grapes-brings-huge-smiles/docview/3062320715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cherry+wood%3B+Great+big+Grapes+brings+huge+smiles+to+Trans-Canada+-+Sir+John+A.+next%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You can't miss the coach on this corner - Blue either.
It's Hwy. 1, the Trans-Canada, at Mortlach, Sask., 40 km west of Moose Jaw, where a 4.6-metre wooden sculpture, complete with a 1.5-metre replica of his beloved dog, stand for all to cherish.
It's nice to see Grapes back in the spotlight, where he should be.
"When I was putting on the finishing touches, all I could hear from the highway was honking horns," artist Darren Jones said. "Everybody knows who Don Cherry is, and people love him."
Don't look now, but banished Rob Ford has been let out of the penalty box, with Toronto deciding to rename a football stadium after him - and Don Cherry is bigger than life on a highway out west.
Could Sir John A. Macdonald be next? "Why not!" said Iron Mike Beaudoin, who heads up this amazing patriotic effort in this tiny hamlet of 350 people. "This is all about artistic freedom.
His idea is to gather artists of differing political stripes and from all walks of life and set up a Mortlach bohemian marketplace along the highway.
"We were looking to put something up that people would notice and maybe turn into to see the great artists'works and support them, too," Beaudoin said. "We knew it was his 90th birthday, and we thought Don Cherry and Blue would be perfect."
Was he ever right. "We are not even near set up the way we hope to be soon, and already a couple of hundred people a day are stopping in," he said.
Cherry was the first wooden carving placed there, but Beaudoin hopes there will be more in the future - and there are no rules on who that might be. No one is cancelled out there in Saskatchewan for woke reasons when it comes to art.
"The way we looked at it is, Don Cherry was cancelled for being Don Cherry," he said. "He didn't deserve what happened to him."
Jones, who is an oil rigger directional driller when not pursuing his art during off hours, thought a giant sculpture of Grapes would be the best for the first statue.
"There was a Manitoba maple tree in town they were taking down, so I said, 'Let's use that,'" he said. "Blue was carved out of a big piece of maple that was in a wood pile."
Iron Mike said he and his fellow Mortlach pal Terry Bittle were thrilled to watch Jones work his magic. These guys - like Cherry, Ford and Macdonald - are legends themselves. This is stunning work.
"It went from a tree to Don Cherry in just 11 days," Beaudoin said.
Cherry reached out to me when he saw recently retired CBC reporter extraordinaire Simon Dingley's tweet about this statue because he wanted to "thank them personally" for the effort.
They were thrilled to hear that. "Don is for the troops, and we are for the troops," Jones said. "It was ridiculous. He was fired from the hockey broadcasts because he was encouraging everyone to remember the fallen troops by buying a poppy for Remembrance Day."
It was spun for political reasons, just like the chain of events that ensnared Rob Ford. An unreasonable complaint about raising $3,100 on city letterhead for a football foundation charity for underprivileged kids resulted in a judge taking his job away from him before it was weeks later overruled on appeal. Rob told me it was those weeks of melancholy that led to his drug problems.
Ironically, now, there has been a change of heart and a football stadium has been named after him.
So, you never know. Rob's brother, Premier Doug Ford, has the power to pull down the wooden planks surrounding Canada's first prime minister at Queen's Park.
Fingers are crossed he will, because erasing history means erasing people from the history books.
Regardless, this country, which has seen Sir John A. Macdonald statues torn down and boarded up, may soon see a wood carving of the politician along the highway in Saskatchewan. We don't want to give way any secrets, but "one is just in need of painting to be finished."
Who knows? Maybe there will one day be likeness of a truck with Tamara Lich at the wheel. The sky is the limit in freedom-loving Mortlach, where there's a bit of a refreshing counterculture movement, contrasting our rapacious cancel culture. But these are not political players, but artists expressing themselves and sharing their dreams.
This is not Jones's first foray into hockey stars.
"I did one in Calahoo, Alta., when Craig Berube was the coach of the St. Louis Blues and he won the Stanley Cup," he said. "It's big, like Don's is."
It's a beauty, with the Cup held over his head. Lord knows, Mayor Olivia Chow may want to get his phone number for another one of those when Berube - now coach of the Leafs - brings that Stanley Cup to Toronto.
It's time to stop cancelling our heroes and start celebrating them. Everybody makes mistakes sometime - even artist Jones.
"I put the poppy on the right side of Don's chest when it wasn't supposed to be on the right," he said.
It wasn't done on purpose, but as Rex Murphy used to say, people are often bigger than their flaws.
You can see it yourself until they put the art away for winter. But Don, Blue, and perhaps some new resurrected cancellation pals will be back out on the highway once spring comes around again. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Football; Art; Sculpture; Professional hockey

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

People: Cherry, Don; Ford, Rob

Identifier / keyword: coach; corner; mortlach; moose; wooden; sculpture

Title: Cherry wood; Great big Grapes brings huge smiles to Trans-Canada - Sir John A. next?

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.13

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 30, 2024

column: Opinion

Section: News

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document 449 of 500

WARMINGTON: Grapes in the spotlight again, John A. Macdonald next?

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-grapes-spotlight-again-john-macdonald/docview/3062235014/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Grapes+in+the+spotlight+again%2C+John+A.+Macdonald+next%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You can't miss the coach on this corner.
Blue, either.
Or the famous shirt collars and the beautiful poppy, too.
It's Hwy. 1, the Trans Canada, at Mortlach, Sask., 40 kilometres west of Moose Jaw, where a 4.6-metre wooden sculpture, complete with a 1.5-metre replica of his beloved white dog, stand for all to cherish.
It's nice to see Grapes back in the spotlight where he should be.

Artist Darren Jones working on sculpture of Don Cherry - picture courtesy of Mike Beaudoin
Don Cherry and Blue on Hwy. 1 in Saskatchewan
Don't look now, but banished Rob Ford has been let out of the penalty box, with Toronto deciding to rename a football stadium after him - and Don Cherry is bigger than life on a highway out west.
