Cops investigating gunshot at Jewish girls school in North York

Author: Connor, Kevin

Publication info: Connor, Kevin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cops-investigating-gunshot-at-jewish-girls-school/docview/3060155421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cops+investigating+gunshot+at+Jewish+girls+school+in+North+York&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Connor%2C+Kevin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto Police have begun an investigation after a Jewish elementary school in North York was struck by a bullet early Saturday morning.
Police were called at 9 a.m. to Bais Chaya Mushka School for Girls, in the Dufferin St. and Finch Ave. W. area, after a bullet hole was found in a window.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk, of the Guns and Gangs Task Force, spoke Saturday afternoon outside the school and said it is too early in the investigation to call this incident a hate crime.
"Police found what they believe was evidence of a firearm discharge at the school and confirmed a firearm discharge did occur," Krawczyk said. "At 4:50 a.m., the suspects arrived at the school in a dark-coloured vehicle and exited the vehicle and opened fire causing damage to the front of the school."
Another attack on a Jewish institution today in Toronto. A Jewish girl's school is the latest target. Two men opened fire, shooting 5 rounds at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School. Can we be surprised that violence escalates when there is no deterrence following antisemitic...
- CG Idit Shamir ??? (@ShamirIdit) May 25, 2024
Krawczyk added there were no injuries reported.
In recent months, there have been protests and demonstrations demanding an end to Israel's war in Gaza that have targeted Jewish neighbourhoods in the city.
"I completely understand this can cause concern, fear and anxiety in the community, especially when it happens at a school like this," Krawczyk said.
There will be an increased police presence at schools and places of worship, he said, adding that community safety is a top priority for police.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
Officers will also be onsite at the school to support staff and students.
Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center (FSWC) issued a statement early Saturday evening, saying it is "horrified" to learn that shots were fired at the Jewish school.
"This shocking escalation of violence directed at innocent Jewish children must serve as a wake-up call for political leaders in our city and across our country," said Michael Levitt, President and CEO of FSWC.
"It is time for our leaders to stop with the sympathetic words and instead to take the decisive actions that are necessary to confront the escalating hatred that is plaguing our communities. Jews in this country will not hide or cower in fear in the face of this brazen and cowardly act."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow called the gunfire targeting the school a "despicable antisemitic act."
"Jewish children and families should not be made to fear for their safety," Chow said on social media "Toronto Police are increasing their presence around religious schools and synagogues. I am in contact with the Chief about the plan to keep children and families safe."
Premier Doug Ford said the shooting incident was a "gross display of antisemitism."
"It's beyond belief that anyone could be this hateful," Ford said "Every student deserves to feel safe at school."
Krawczyk is asking members of the public with any video of the incident not to post it on social media but to turn it over to police.
Anyone with information is asked to contact police at 416-808-3200, Crime Stoppers anonymously at 416-222-TIPS (8477), or online at 222tips.com
CRIME SCENE: Two suspects sought for robbery in Riverside
1 dead, 3 others injured after wheel strikes coach bus passenger on QEW: OPP

Document 470 of 500

Ford's on the case; Preem delivers on beer and wine promise - but with a catch

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-on-case-preem-delivers-beer-wine-promise/docview/3060096306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+on+the+case%3B+Preem+delivers+on+beer+and+wine+promise+-+but+with+a+catch&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You'll be able to get beer, wine and cider in corner stores by the end of the summer, Premier Doug Ford said Friday morning.
The announcement moves up the changes by a significant margin, with the province having previously only said the move to expanded sales would happen before Jan. 1, 2026.
The cost of that move, though, is raising questions, but proponents say the benefits are clear - more jobs and more tax revenue for the government.
Starting Aug. 1, grocery stores that already sell booze will be able to expand their offerings including ready-to-drink cocktails like a Caesar in a can and larger cases of beer.
After Sept. 5, convenience stores will be able to do the same, while new grocery stores and big-box stores like Costco can come online as of Oct. 31.
One thing raising questions is a pledge to pay The Beer Store up to $225 million over the next 19 months for what are described as "added costs."
Those added costs will include ramping up distribution and recycling facilities to deal with all the new stores.
A new agreement between the province and TBS states that it will "facilitate the use of TBS and its efficient recycling and distribution systems for the benefit of Ontario on terms acceptable to the Parties."
The agreement also specifically states that TBS must keep 386 retail stores open until at least Jan. 1, 2026.
In part this $225 million is for services TBS will be providing to the province, like recycling.
It will also in part be about subsidizing unprofitable stores and preserving jobs for the next 19 months.
Some of these costs were going to be incurred no matter when the expansion of booze sales happened.
The province already pays TBS to run the recycling program for them, so those costs would have to be paid but clearly some of this is paying off The Beer Store for ending its contract early.
Supporters of the plan to expand sales say the financial impact will be significant, including increased taxes for government.
A study on the economic impact of these changes conducted for the Convenience Industry Council of Canada predicts between 7,000 and 7,500 jobs created.
Most of those would be in retail but they also project 500-800 in what are described as "middle income jobs."
The study also projects $165 million spent on installing coolers and other infrastructure. As for taxes, the study estimates more than $200 million in annual payments being generated, with 69% going to the provincial government.
"Our plan will create new growth opportunities for local brewers, wineries, retailers and small businesses. It's going to support local jobs and, most importantly, it's going to give people more choice and convenience," Ford said.
The move to expand alcohol sales was fought for fiercely by the convenience store industry and by craft brewers who see this as a real opportunity for expansion.
By contrast, the changes have been fought for years by The Beer Store and its owners Labatt, Molson and Sleeman.
Right now, Ontario has the fewest retail alcohol stores per capita, excluding bars and restaurants, in all of Canada.
There are just 2.4 stores per 10,000 population in Ontario, compared to 21.1 in Newfoundland and Labrador, and 12.2 in Quebec.
After these changes, the province expects there to be 9.4 retail stores per 10,000 population.
Ontario is finally catching up to other provinces and many states south of the border. It's a change that many politicians have promised but never delivered on.
Ford now gets to claim credit for doing what others couldn't.
Whether the public will raise a toast to him or have a sour taste in their mouths over the $225-million payment is the only open question. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores; Costs; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Costs Grocery stores

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: cider; corner; stores; summer; premier; morning

LILLEY: LCBO picks fight with distillers and you end up paying more

Document 472 of 500

Preem, give 'em the hard stuff; Leader should take even firmer stance on 'safe supply'

Author: Zivo, Adam

Publication info: Zivo, Adam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/preem-give-em-hard-stuff-leader-should-take-even/docview/3059707267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preem%2C+give+%27em+the+hard+stuff%3B+Leader+should+take+even+firmer+stance+on+%27safe+supply%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Zivo%2C+Adam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government stood aside for years while "safer supply" drug programs ravaged Ontario's communities - but the province is now finally clamping down and giving these programs the scrutiny they deserve.
This is long overdue, but Premier Doug Ford needs to go further if he wants to reverse the harms of this failed "harm reduction" experiment.
Safer supply refers to the practice of prescribing free addictive drugs as an alternative to riskier street substances. That typically means distributing hydromorphone, an opioid as potent as heroin, as an alternative to illicit fentanyl.
While advocates claim safer supply "saves lives," the truth is that an overwhelming majority of recipients - estimates hover around 80 to 90% - "divert" (sell or trade) their hydromorphone on the black market to acquire stronger substances. Diverted hydromorphone subsequently floods into surrounding communities, where it creates new addictions and relapses while enriching gangs.
The expansion of safer supply was generally forced upon the provinces by the federal government, which unilaterally funds "pilot programs" through grants overseen by Health Canada's substance use and addictions program.
In an open letter sent to the federal government last Thursday, Ford asked that the operations of these programs be reviewed, and that the approval of new sites require provincial support - a move that would effectively pause the federal expansion of safer supply.
"In Ontario, due to Health Canada's siloed approval process, the province is completely in the dark about where these federally approved sites are operating and the quantity of controlled and illegal substances they dispense. This is frankly unacceptable, given its adverse effects on our communities," Ford wrote.
The premier is absolutely justified in making this request.
As mentioned in his letter, the head of the B.C. Association of Chiefs of Police recently testified in Parliament that half of the hydromorphone seized in the province can be attributed to diverted safer supply. There is also strong evidence that youth access these drugs, too.
These community harms are indefensible - especially as there is no evidence that safer supply works. Studies supporting the experiment are usually produced by activist "researchers" who rely on shoddy methodologies (i.e. asking drug users if they benefit from getting free opioids, then treating their answers as objective truth) or data that has been cherry-picked.
It is imperative that safer supply be significantly reformed or abolished. But while the federal government has been reckless, the provinces are not blameless either.
Yes, the federal government funds many safer supply programs, but the truth is that safer supply can be prescribed by any doctor or nurse practitioner in Ontario. Clients then fill their prescriptions at any pharmacy, as with most other drugs, with the provincial government footing the bill.
If Ford really wants to clamp down on safer supply, he needs to introduce legislation that effectively bans prescribers from providing it. This is what Alberta did years ago - which is why safer supply is almost entirely unavailable there. It would also help if Ontario introduced legislation that strips doctors of their medical licences if they are caught prescribing safer supply drugs that one can reasonably expect to be diverted.
As hydromorphone is covered by OHIP, the provincial government should have detailed data on how much of the drug is dispensed, and where. This data needs to be made public immediately. It is unclear why Ford claimed in his letter that it is unavailable.
There is no need to wait for the federal government to investigate safer supply programs. The province should launch its own public inquiry and vigorously investigate safer supply prescribers for malpractice - particularly those who have a reputation for recklessly harming surrounding communities.
Zivo is a freelance writer and executive director of the Centre For Responsible Drug Policy

Subject: Federal government; Prescription drugs

Doors Open to spring excitement, other fun things to explore this weekend

Author: Wilford, Denette

Publication info: Wilford, Denette. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doors-open-spring-excitement-other-fun-things/docview/3059459154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doors+Open+to+spring+excitement%2C+other+fun+things+to+explore+this+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilford%2C+Denette&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're wondering how the last weekend of May is already upon us, get in line because time is flying by.
As the weather warms up, that means there are more outdoor events to take advantage of, from concerts and sporting events to festivals, but there's lots to see and do inside as well.
Basically, the city is your oyster!
Here's what you need to know to make the necessary plans.
Doors Open
It's an architecture lover's dream as more than 160 buildings and sites in the city open their doors, allowing visitors to explore and get a behind-the-scenes look at some of the city's most historical, cultural and socially significant locales.
The adventure is on Saturday and Sunday from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. unless otherwise indicated.
For a full lineup of buildings, performances, tours and talks, click here
A post shared by City of Toronto (@cityofto)
ByBlacks Restaurant Week
Dive into the food festival featuring Black-owned Canadian, African, Caribbean and fusion businesses, caterers, food trucks and pop-ups.
The event runs until Sunday. To find out which restaurants are taking part or for more information, click here
Canadian Air Guitar Championships
Prepare for an epic showdown of invisible strings and striking swagger with an electrifying event that promises high-flying riffs, wild antics and air-shredding performances.
Rock out at The Velvet Underground at 508 Queen St. W. on Saturday at 9 p.m. For tickets and more information, click here
A post shared by Air Guitar Canada (@airguitarcanada)
SING! The Toronto International Vocal Arts Festival
The a cappella festival will feature folk, pop, classical, jazz, R&B, rap, beatboxing and so much more, showing how the human voice is capable of just about anything.
The performances take place at a number of venues across the city with virtual events also available.
The fest kicks off on Saturday and runs until June 2. For the full schedule, tickets and more information, click here
10XTO Indoor Doubles Championship
Ontario's best men's and women's national-level juniors and semi-professional players are put to the test as they play in this doubles-focused, Ontario Tennis Association-sanctioned tournament.
The five-day tourney wraps up on Sunday at 10XTO at 107 Princes' Blvd. For more information, click here
RECOMMENDED VIDEO Buddha Jayanti
Experience the rich traditions, captivating performances and fun activities designed to honour the birth of Buddha and explore the Hyolmo culture.
It's all at Yonge-Dundas Square on Saturday from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. For more information, click here
Gluten Free Garage
Savour the best food festival in Toronto, particularly for those who live a GF lifestyle, showcasing the best finds from across the province and beyond.
Eat up at ArtHubs Wychwood Barns at 601 Christie St. on Sunday from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m.
For tickets and more information, click here
A post shared by Gluten Free Garage Fest (@glutenfreegarage)
TV Show Set Sale
The Toronto Film Industry is running what might be its largest TV show set sale to date, turning over a high volume of props from two massive sci-fi series including spacey furniture and equipment, costumes and space suits, green screens and pelican cases and so much more.
Check it all out at 254 Bartley Dr., just off Bermondsey Rd. in North York from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. daily.
For more information, click here
Tostitos Tasting Window
Inspired by the wine windows of Florence and brought to life by Chef Susur Lee, Canadians can sample a series of delicious bites that showcase the local flavours of Toronto and Vancouver with Tostitos tortilla chips as the base.
Savour it all at 147 Spadina Ave. until Sunday from noon to 8 p.m. For more information, click here
LILLEY: Eglinton to be ripped up for bike lanes after LRT construction
Neil Young and Crazy Horse open Budweiser Stage's summer season
Slash's blues festival among Toronto's new summer live music offerings
MS Walk
MS Canada's annual walk brings thousands of Canadians together to have fun, raise money and show support for people living with multiple sclerosis.
This year's MS Walk will take place on Sunday in several communities across Ontario including Toronto, York Region, Peel-Halton, Hamilton-Burlington and more.
For locations, to donate and for more information, click here
The Bentway Summer Opening Party
OK, it's not technically summer, but who says an early start to the season is a bad thing? Kick things off with a celebration of food, drinks and music as you mix, mingle and dance to beats from local DJs or check out the new public art exhibition, Softer City.
Get in on the fun at The Bentway on Friday from 7 p.m. to 11 p.m. For tickets and more information, click here
A post shared by The Bentway ??Toronto (@thebentway)

ZIVO: Doug Ford's pushback on 'safer supply' should go farther

Author: McLeod, Cynthia

Publication info: McLeod, Cynthia. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/zivo-doug-fords-pushback-on-safer-supply-should/docview/3059316108/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ZIVO%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+pushback+on+%27safer+supply%27+should+go+farther&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=McLeod%2C+Cynthia&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government stood aside for years while "safer supply" drug programs ravaged Ontario's communities -?but the province is now finally clamping down and giving these programs the scrutiny they deserve.

It would also help if Ontario introduced legislation that strips doctors of their medical licences if they are caught prescribing safer supply drugs that one can reasonably expect to be diverted.

Document 475 of 500

Travelling in circles; Ford gov't blocks release of info on UP Express reversal

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/travelling-circles-ford-govt-blocks-release-info/docview/3058508930/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Travelling+in+circles%3B+Ford+gov%27t+blocks+release+of+info+on+UP+Express+reversal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On April 15, at 9 a.m., Premier Doug Ford announced that his government would make every other UP Express train from Union Station to Pearson Airport a true express. Less than 36 hours later, the government had walked back that policy and apologized for upsetting people.
It took the Cabinet Office, effectively Premier Doug Ford's department, 31 days to tell me that it needed another 35 days to look for documents to explain what happened. At least it isn't demanding search fees to look for these documents, the way the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx are.
Something happened to prompt the decision by the Ford government to make every other train an express and something prompted the reversal. The public deserves to know something of what happened and why each decision was made.
But, as it always does when it comes to Metrolinx, the Ford government is being secretive.
There are parts of the story that are easy to figure out.
The province announced it was expanding GO Train service to Kitchener, which runs along the same line and provides service to the Bloor and Weston stations. Clearly, it thought people would switch from using UP Express to GO Train service for those stops.
When the public backlash occurred, Councillor Frances Nunziata, an ally of Ford, was inundated with complaints. So, too, was MPP, cabinet minister and the premier's nephew, Michael Ford, who represents the same area as Nunziata.
I'd hate to think that Ford was making policy decisions based on how his nephew and a political ally were feeling, but, without evidence, that's the most plausible answer - and it's not a good one.
After the reversal was made, a reversal that was whiplashinducing even for a government known to reverse course, several Freedom of Information Act requests were made. Ford's Cabinet Office, the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx were all asked for the same thing.
"Provide copies of any documents providing statistics on Metrolinx ridership and/or analysis in support of UP Express plans," the request read.
Metrolinx responded first on May 13 with a demand for $225 in search fees for the documents. Next was the Ministry of Transportation on May 15, which demanded two different search fees, one for $120 and another for $105, to look for documents about UP Express plans.
On Friday at 5:16 p.m., we got the final response from Cabinet Office. It shoved it out the door as it shut down for the Victoria Day long weekend.
"We wish to advise you that the time has been extended in accordance with Section 27(1) (b) of the Act for an additional five weeks to June 21 ...," the letter from Cabinet Office read.
That extension, until June 21, means that we won't receive anything from Ford's department until the deadline has passed for paying the search fees on the three other demands. If those fees aren't paid by June 12 and 14 respectively, they'll be deemed abandoned and any attempts to get at the information on why this decision was made will need to start all over again.
It's all too cute by half.
The Ford government is once again covering up for Metrolinx, as it has done for years. This is a troubled agency, which can't lay tracks on the Eglinton Crosstown properly and can't deliver projects on time - but one that Ford, and his office, run regular interference for.
There is also the problem that Metrolinx just decides not to release information, claiming it's secret advice to government.
Last year, when it was forced to admit the Crosstown's tracks were laid improperly, Metrolinx released 16 pages with almost all information redacted. The Ministry of Transportation and Cabinet Office did the same thing.
The public deserves to know what is going on; we're paying for the mess that is Metrolinx.
If Doug Ford believes his own schtick - that he's in it for the little guy - he'll tell his officials to release the information quickly and freely.
@brianlilley blilley@postmedia.com

Document 476 of 500

Cancelled royalty; PM, GG snub Queen Victoria's birthday

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cancelled-royalty-pm-gg-snub-queen-victorias/docview/3058508877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cancelled+royalty%3B+PM%2C+GG+snub+Queen+Victoria%27s+birthday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While Victoria Day may have been celebrated by most in Canada, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Gov. Gen. Mary Simon weren't among the celebrants.
Simon dedicated her energies to planting trees to help fight climate change, while Trudeau used the day to promise Canadians they'll get free contraceptives.
First celebrated in 1845, Victoria Day is Canada's oldest public holiday. Maybe Victoria's secret is that the legendary queen of Canada in 1867 - the time of Confederation - has already been unofficially cancelled.
While there is no announcement to this end found on Government of Canada social media sites, there was also no noticeable acknowledgement that Monday marked the celebration of her birthday.
Many call it the May Two-Four weekend, and, for most, it's the annual starting line for summer. Many enjoyed the holiday, but if they were looking at federal government official sources, they may not know which holiday it was.
Special thanks to the Royal Canadian Legion who posted Queen Victoria's picture and wished her a happy birthday. It was something King Charles'representative in Canada and the current PM failed to do.
Victoria, who served as the British Commonwealth's head of state from 1837 until 1901, received a royal snub from the King's stand-in. One would think Simon should know about the significance of the date and be required to highlight it.
Instead, the GG posted on X Tuesday: "This past weekend, #GGSimon and a group of friends planted 500 saplings to help restore the forested area damaged by Hurricane Fiona in Nova Scotia."
On Friday, Simon posted about receiving an honorary degree at the Royal Military College in Kingston where she was "delighted to meet with students participating in the Indigenous Leadership Opportunity Year."
Noble efforts, but no mention of the holiday.
Trudeau was busy, too. While he didn't reference Victoria Day, he did post a video in which he spoke with a woman about the right to choose an abortion.
"Being pro-choice means believing women should have the freedom to choose if or how to start a family. So, we're going to make contraceptives - including emergency contraceptives - free in Canada," he said on X.
He also talked about his government's plans to combat car theft. Trudeau in the past weeks has commemorated on X about many holidays, including Passover and Vaisakhi. But just as he failed to publicly mark Good Friday, he also didn't commemorate Victoria Day on social media.
Some things are a priority for him, and others are not.
Just last week, Trudeau tweeted out a happy birthday to comedian Mark Critch, which was a nice gesture. But his lack of a message on Victoria Day sends a loud message to those who believe Canada's historical roots are being erased brick by brick.
People can't come to any other conclusion when they see the removal of Sir John A. Macdonald statues, his name dropped from schools and his likeness erased from currency.
Trudeau, who has received forgiveness and a second chance for wearing black face - and a whole lot of other things - has failed to stand up for a former PM or Victoria, who has also had her statues removed or vandalized in different parts of the country.
All of this is on the premise that there are the bodies of missing Indigenous children buried on the property of Catholic Residential schools. Despite no remains having been found so far, the blame remains tagged to Macdonald and Victoria, who had no direct involvement in any of these schools.
They shouldn't have the power to write Victoria, Macdonald, Egerton Ryerson, James McGill or Henry Dundas out of Canada's history books. Whatever their views, the positions of PM and GG belong to Canadians. As individuals, these leaders can have their own point of view, but as public office holders representing all Canadians, they should respect the service of Canada's first PM and the sovereign of the British Commonwealth.
Why do they get to decide on the history? And after seeing Yonge-Dundas Square renamed Sankofa Square, Canadians realize historical figures will be replaced by themes, however benign, that have nothing to do with Canada and are chosen without giving them a say.
But Trudeau and Simon aren't alone in missing Victoria's birthday.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow on X didn't mention the birthday while tweeting about many other important community initiatives, explaining that "Trans Pride Toronto is a collective of trans and two-spirited people that supports the needs of marginalized, racialized and sex working community members" and on the "Native Cultural Centre (addressing) the needs of the Toronto urban Indigenous population with counselling, material assistance and essential services to empower members of the Indigenous community."
Premier Doug Ford was the one who got it right - acknowledging Victoria Day weekend with a warm, unpolitical video, encouraging people to be safe and have fun.
It was better than nothing - which is what Victoria got from our other leaders.
On that note, Happy Birthday, Queen Victoria - belated. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Community; Queens; Birthdays; Birth control; Royalty; Social networks

Document 477 of 500

WARMINGTON: Trudeau, Governor General snub Queen Victoria's birthday

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/warmington-trudeau-governor-general-snub-queen/docview/3058295756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=WARMINGTON%3A+Trudeau%2C+Governor+General+snub+Queen+Victoria%27s+birthday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While Victoria Day may have been celebrated by most in Canada, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Gov. Gen. Mary Simon were not among the celebrants.
Simon dedicated her energies to planting trees to help fight climate change, while Trudeau used the day to promise Canadians they'll get free contraceptives.
First celebrated in 1845, Victoria Day is Canada's oldest public holiday. Maybe Victoria's secret is that the legendary queen of Canada in 1867 - the time of Confederation - has already been unofficially cancelled.
While there is no announcement to this end found on Government of Canada social media sites, there was also no noticeable acknowledgement that Monday marked the celebration of her birthday.
Today, we honour the birth of Queen Victoria. Wishing everyone a safe and happy Victoria Day!
- The Royal Canadian Legion (@RoyalCdnLegion) May 20, 2024
Many call it the May Two-Four weekend, and for most, it's the annual starting line for summer. Many enjoyed the holiday, but if they were looking at federal government official sources, they may not know which holiday it was.
Special thanks to the Royal Canadian Legion who posted Queen Victoria's picture and wished her a happy birthday. It was something the King's representative in Canada and the current prime minister failed to do.
Queen Victoria, who served as the British Commonwealth's from 1837 until 1901, received a royal snub from the King's stand-in. One would think Simon should know about the significance of the date and be required to highlight it.
This past weekend, #GGSimon and a group of friends planted 500 saplings to help restore the forested area damaged by Hurricane Fiona in Nova Scotia.#2BillionTrees
- Governor General of Canada (@GGCanada) May 21, 2024
Instead, the Governor General posted to X on Tuesday: "This past weekend, #GGSimon and a group of friends planted 500 saplings to help restore the forested area damaged by Hurricane Fiona in Nova Scotia."
On Friday, Simon posted about receiving an honourary degree at the Royal Military College in Kingston where she was "delighted to meet with students participating in the Indigenous Leadership Opportunity Year."
Noble efforts, but no mention of the holiday.
#GGSimon was delighted to meet with students participating in the Indigenous Leadership Opportunity Year at the @RMCCanadaCMR This unique program provides cultural experiences that help build leadership and life skills.
- Governor General of Canada (@GGCanada) May 17, 2024
Trudeau was busy, too. While he didn't reference Victoria Day, he did post a video in which he spoke with a woman about the right to chose an abortion.
"Being pro-choice means believing women should have the freedom to choose if or how to start a family. So, we're going to make contraceptives - including emergency contraceptives - free in Canada," he said on X.
Being pro-choice means believing women should have the freedom to choose if or how to start a family. So, we're going to make contraceptives-including emergency contraceptives-free in Canada.
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) May 20, 2024
He also talked about his government's plans to combat car theft. Trudeau in the past weeks has commemorated on X about many holidays, including Passover and Vaisakhi. But just as he failed to publicly mark Good Friday, he also did not commemorate Victoria Day on social media.
Some things are a priority for him, and others are not.
Recommended video
Just last week, Trudeau tweeted out a happy birthday to comedian Mark Critch, which was a nice gesture. But his lack of a message on Victoria Day sends a loud message to those who believe Canada's historical roots are being erased brick by brick.
People can't come to any other conclusion when they see the removal of Sir John A. Macdonald statues, his name dropped from schools and his likeness erased from currency.
Happy birthday, Mark! Hoping 50 brings you more balance.
- Justin Trudeau (@JustinTrudeau) May 14, 2024
Trudeau, who has received forgiveness and a second chance for wearing black face - and a whole lot of other things - has failed to stand up for a former prime minister or Queen Victoria, who has also had her statues removed or vandalized in different parts of the country.
All of this is on the premise there are the bodies of missing Indigenous children buried on the property of Catholic Residential schools. Despite no remains have been found so far, the blame remains tagged to Macdonald and Queen Victoria, who had no direct involvement in any of these schools.
They should not have the power to write Queen Victoria, John A. Macdonald, Egerton Ryerson, James McGill or Henry Dundas out of Canada's history books. Whatever their views, the positions of prime minister and governor general belong to Canadians. As individuals, these leaders can have their own point of view, but as public office holders representing all Canadians, they should respect the service of Canada's first prime minister and the sovereign of the British Commonwealth.
Why do they get to decide on the history?
And after seeing Yonge-Dundas Square renamed Sankofa Square, Canadians realize historical figures will be replaced by themes, however benign, that have nothing to do with Canada and are chosen without giving them a say.
Trans Pride Toronto is a collective of trans and two-spirited people that supports the needs of marginalized, racialized, and sex-working community members. They advocate for improving the inclusion and relationships with trans and two-spirited people in many spaces. 25/28
- Mayor Olivia Chow (@MayorOliviaChow) May 20, 2024
But Trudeau and Simon are not alone in missing Victoria's birthday.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow on X did not mention the birthday while tweeting about many other important community initiatives, explaining that "Trans Pride Toronto is a collective of trans and two-spirited people that supports the needs of marginalized, racialized, and sex working community members" and on the "Native Cultural Centre (addressing) the needs of the Toronto urban Indigenous population with counselling, material assistance, and essential services to empower members of the Indigenous community."
Happy Victoria Day long weekend, Ontario!
Wherever you're celebrating in our beautiful province, I wish you a safe and fun weekend.
- Doug Ford (@fordnation) May 18, 2024
Premier Doug Ford was the one who got it right - acknowledging Victoria Day weekend with a warm, unpolitical video, encouraging people to be safe and have fun.
It was better than nothing - which is what Queen Victoria got from our other leaders.
On that note, Happy Birthday, Queen Victoria - belated.

WARMINGTON: Sir John A. Macdonald's home reopens with rewritten history

LILLEY: Ford government blocks release of info on UP Express reversal

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-government-blocks-release-info-on-up/docview/3057957372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+government+blocks+release+of+info+on+UP+Express+reversal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On April 15, at 9 a.m., Premier Doug Ford announced that his government would make every other UP Express train from Union Station to Pearson Airport a true express. Less than 36 hours later, the government had walked back that policy and apologized for upsetting people.
It took the Cabinet Office, effective Premier Doug Ford's department, 31 days to tell me that they needed another 35 days to look for documents to explain what happened. At least they aren't demanding search fees to look for these documents, the way the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx are.
Something happened to prompt the decision by the Ford government to make every other train an express and something prompted the reversal. The public deserves to know something of what happened and why each decision was made.
But, as they always do when it comes to Metrolinx, the Ford government is being secretive.
There are parts of the story that are easy to figure out.
The province announced they were expanding GO Train service to Kitchener, which runs along the same line and provides service to the Bloor and Weston Stations. Clearly, they thought people would switch from using UP Express to GO Train service for those stops.
When the public backlash occurred, Toronto Councillor Frances Nunziata, an ally of Ford, was inundated with complaints. So, too, was MPP, cabinet minister and the premier's nephew, Michael Ford, who represents the same area as Nunziata.
I'd hate to think that Ford was making policy decisions based on how his nephew and a political ally were feeling, but without evidence, that's the most plausible answer - and it's not a good one.
After the reversal was made, a reversal that was whiplash inducing even for a government known to reverse course, several Freedom of Information Act requests were made. Ford's Cabinet Office, the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx were all asked for the same thing.
Recommended video
"Provide copies of any documents providing statistics on Metrolinx ridership and/or analysis in support of UP Express plans," the request read.
Metrolinx responded first on May 13 with a demand for $225 in search fees for the documents. Next was the Ministry of Transportation on May 15 which demanded two different search fees, one for $120 and another for $105 to look for documents about UP Express plans.
On Friday at 5:16 p.m., we got the final response from Cabinet Office. They shoved it out the door as they shut down for the Victoria Day long weekend.
"We wish to advise you that the time has been extended in accordance with Sect. 27(1)(b) of the Act for an additional five weeks to June 21, 2024," the letter from Cabinet Office read.
Why did the Ford government plan to change the UP Express and why did they reverse so quickly.
We tried to find out but once again, when it comes to Metrolinx, @fordnation and his government are opting for secrecy.
It's unacceptable.
Read & RT #onpoli
- Brian Lilley (@brianlilley) May 21, 2024
That extension, until June 21, means that we won't receive anything from Ford's department until the deadline has passed for paying the search fees on the three other demands. If those fees aren't paid by June 12 and June 14 respectively, they will be deemed abandoned and any attempts to get at the information on why this decision was made will need to start all over again.
It's all too cute by half.
The Ford government is once again covering up for Metrolinx, as they have done for years. This is a troubled agency, which can't lay tracks on the Eglinton Crosstown properly and can't deliver projects on time - but one that Ford, and his office, run regular interference for.
There is also the problem that Metrolinx just decides not to release information, claiming it is secret advice to government.
Last year, when they were forced to admit the Crosstown's tracks were laid improperly, Metrolinx released 16 pages with almost all information redacted. The Ministry of Transportation and Cabinet Office did the same thing.
The public deserves to know what is going on; we are paying for the mess that is Metrolinx.
If Doug Ford believes his own schtick - that he's in it for the little guy - he will tell his officials to release the information quickly and freely.
Sadly, the record shows that Ford and those around him are more interested in protecting Metrolinx CEO Phil Verster than being honest with taxpayers.
LILLEY: Trudeau Liberals trying to scare voters to protect convicts

Letters to the Editor, May 21, 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/letters-editor-may-21-2024/docview/3057374739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+Editor%2C+May+21%2C+2024&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: BAG DROP
Re "Folding on paper" (Brian Lilley, April 8): Whatever Premier Doug Ford's reasons may be behind his letter to the LCBO telling it to reverse its decision that eliminated paper bags, his statement that this is about affordability is insulting. If Ford was truly interested in helping families who are struggling in making ends meet, he would reduce or eliminate the excessive provincial taxes on alcohol sales.
Karl Greene Markham
(Drink less and save money)
SILENCE ISN'T GOLDEN
Re "Mass murder, rape and Jew hatred must be condemned" (Warren Kinsella, May 4): I am fully onboard with Warren's column. I am disgusted with our federal government's position, which looks to me like it supports Hamas. I fully support our Jewish communities across Canada and I apologize for how you are being treated by some Canadians attacking your synagogues, your schools, your businesses, the places you live, and now at Canadian universities. The worst part of this is the silence and actions from our government leaders and our police. Thank you, my Jewish friends. Your contributions to our world in fields like medicine, science and humanity will never be forgotten.
Gord Peters Chestermere, Alta.
(The outrageous attacks against Jewish institutions and businesses are a disgrace)
FOOD FOR THOUGHT
Re "Decoding discrepancies in Canada's food price data" (Sylvain Charlebois, April 9): He confirms what I have thought all along. StatsCan is given a final inflation number to report by the powers that be and, as such, they work backwards to arrive at the watered-down inflation rate. What I find most annoying is that they think the majority of Canadians will buy into their contrived reports. Give us more credit than that. All one has to do is to look at their weekly grocery bill to see that despite a so-called reduction in new cellular phone rates and a momentary drop at the gas pumps, the inflation rate is nowhere near the 3% mark. It's time to report some unbiased statistics.
Keith J. Hennick Wasaga Beach
('Watered down' is an understatement)

Document 481 of 500

Summer set to sizzle; Slash's blues fest among T.O.'s new live music offerings

Author: Stevenson, Jane

Publication info: Stevenson, Jane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/summer-set-sizzle-slashs-blues-fest-among-t-o-s/docview/3056554587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Summer+set+to+sizzle%3B+Slash%27s+blues+fest+among+T.O.%27s+new+live+music+offerings&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Stevenson%2C+Jane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hot town, summer's officially in our city.
Toronto's outdoor live music season kicks off Monday night when Canadian rocker Neil Young and Crazy Horse open the Budweiser Stage's outdoor amphitheatre before returning to the same venue July 8.
But that's just one of a record 80 shows at Budweiser Stage, which celebrates its 30th anniversary this year.
"When (Neil Young) came up, it was one of those things that just felt perfect," said Erik Hoffman, Live Nation Canada's president of music. "It's very appropriate to have one of the most legendary artists of all time (open the season). It's a hot ticket, so it's great he was able to come back (in July)."
Hoffman said nothing will be physically different inside the 16,000-seat capacity Budweiser Stage, but with construction at Ontario Place there will be no parking on the lakeshore side and likely more traffic - due to the narrowing of the Gardiner Expressway - so he encourages concertgoers to use public transportation and bicycles.
Parking will still be available on the CNE side and over at Liberty Village.
"It's the toughest on traffic probably in the summer for Toronto in a long time, but it's the biggest concert year that we will ever have had there," Hoffman said. "It's going to be about 80 shows this year. Last year was 66. So it's very significant. It's definitely cemented us as the No. 1 large amphitheatre in North America."
Of those, Hoffman said 35 are brand-new Budweiser Stage headliners, including Canadian acts like Tate McCrae on July 27 and The Beaches on Aug. 22.
"Almost half the season will be new acts headlining for the first time, which is to me how you stay as relevant as ever," Hoffman said.
Among the fast-selling shows of this summer across all venues are many two-night stands, including 21 Savage's May 25 and 27 at Budweiser Stage, One Direction's Niall Horan June 28-29 at Scotiabank Arena, Alanis Morissette July 13-14 at Budweiser Stage, Jennifer Lopez Aug. 2-3 at Scotiabank Arena, Imagine Dragons Aug. 8-9 at Budweiser Stage, Avril Lavigne on Aug. 12 at Scotiabank Arena and Aug. 16 at Budweiser Stage, New Kids On The Block Aug. 17-18 at Budweiser Stage, and finally Sting with a whopping five nights at Massey Hall on Sept. 20-22 and 24-25.
"I just think people's relationship with live music has changed," Hoffman said. "You could say because of social media and seeing these things online. And like the FOMO (fear of missing out) effect, people are taking in more shows than ever."
There's also the annual summer music festivals returning, like Canadian Music Week (June 1-8), Luminato (June 5-16), NXNE (June 12-16), TD Toronto Jazz Fest (June 21-30), Born and Raised Festival in Montebello Park, St. Catharines (July 5-6), Veld Music Festival at Downsview Park (Aug. 2-4) and Boots and Hearts (Aug. 8-11) at Burl's Creek in Oro-Medonte, the latter in its 12th year.
"I think the experience has a lot to do with (the longevity)," said Brooke Dunford, talent buyer for Republic Live, owner of Burl's Creek, which can accommodate up to 70,000 people. "They come and camp for four days and we've seen our day tickets grow. It's just about embracing what we call Boots Life of the country scene and it's just a unique experience that we're able to offer fans. Each year, it just keeps getting bigger and bigger."
Meanwhile, All Your Friends Fest is a new pop-punk festival, including camping, with headliners Billy Talent on Aug. 23 and Fall Out Boy on Aug. 23-24, also at Burl's Creek.
"Similar to Boots, we really love celebrating a community of fans," Dunford said. "So based on artist availability and what fans are listening to right now, I think there's just a need in the market. And we were excited to have enough bands come together to really celebrate that nostalgic era and they're doing it in the U.S. and we're seeing successful festivals run with this genre, so it was just something that was the right time, the right place."
Closer to home at Budweiser Stage, there's Slash's new blues-oriented SERPENT festival on July 28.
SERPENT stands for Solidarity, Engagement, Restore, Peace, Equality N'Tolerance and will feature the GNR guitarist playing with an allstar blues lineup, including Keb'Mo'and Robert Randolph.
"It's a cool concept because he's obviously a guitar hero and it's kind of a crazy list of names," Hoffman said. "It's not who you might think."
And what about Drake's OVO fest, often a staple over the August long weekend at Budweiser Stage? So far, his OVO label artist PartyNextDoor has been announced for Aug. 2 at the venue, so who knows? "I don't ever know about OVO until I know," Hoffman said. "We love Drake more than anything. He's delivered the gift of OVO so many times and I have no idea."
There's also a brand-new outdoor venue this summer, with Barenaked Ladies, Sam Roberts and KT Tunstall on June 27 opening the 9,000-capacity The Bowl at Sobeys Stadium at York University.
Shaggy performs there on June 29 and The Tea Party, Headstones, I Mother Earth, Treble Charger and Bif Naked on July 6.
"Located near major highways and the subway, accessing the venue is hassle free, with thousands of parking spaces available," a news release boasts of the new open-air venue.
Indoor highlights this summer include Snoop Dogg on June 12 at Scotiabank Arena, Roger Daltrey on June 14 at the OLG Stage at Fallsview Casino, and such Rogers Centre shows as Def Leppard with Journey and Heart on Aug. 2 and Blink 182 with Alexisonfire on Aug. 15.
"This summer's going to be quite something," Hoffman said. jstevenson@postmedia.com Here's Jane's picks of 20 concerts to see over the next
GET READY TO ROCK few months: n Neil Young and Crazy Horse, May 20 and July 8, Budweiser Stage n Echo and the Bunnymen, May 23, History n Pixies and Modest Mouse with Cat Power, June 8, Budweiser Stage n Keith Urban, June 22, OLG Stage at Fallsview Casino n Santana and Counting Crows, June 26, Budweiser Stage n Andre 3000, June 28, Massey Hall n Wilco, July 2-3, Massey Hall n Daryl Hall and Elvis Costello and the Imposters with Charlie Sexton, July 4, Budweiser Stage n Red Hot Chili Peppers, July 15 and 17, Budweiser Stage n Sammy Hagar, Michael Anthony, Joe Satriani, Jason Bonham with Loverboy, July 31, Budweiser Stage n Green Day with Smashing Pumpkins, Rancid and The Linda Lindas, Aug. 1, Rogers Centre n Pink with Sheryl Crow, Aug. 14, Rogers Centre n Missy Elliott with Busta Rhymes, Ciara and Timbaland, Aug. 19-20, Scotiabank Arena n Crowded House, Sept. 8, OLG Stage at Fallsview Casino n Jeff Lynne's ELO (final Toronto show), Sept. 9, Scotiabank Arena n Pulp, Sept. 10-11, History n The Killers, Sept. 10-11, Budweiser Stage n Paul Weller, Sept.

Document 482 of 500

The obvious problem in decriminalizing drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/obvious-problem-decriminalizing-drugs/docview/3056554422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+obvious+problem+in+decriminalizing+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that the NDP government in British Columbia and the Liberal government in Ottawa have abandoned their disastrous experiment in decriminalizing the use of hard drugs in public places, the question is why it happened in the first place? Why did no one ask the obvious question before the federal government granted B.C.'s request to amend the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act to allow it?
That question is, or was, that "if we decriminalize the use of up to 2.5 grams of drugs such as fetanyl, crack, methamphetamines and ecstasy for adults in public places, what are we going to do when the drug uscrs actually do it? "What are we going to do in the face of the inevitable explosion of drug use in public places such as hospitals, on public transit, and at parks, beaches and sports fields, that will occur?" When that predictably happened, the B.C. government asked Ottawa to recriminalize the use of these drugs in public places in the face of public fury in the province that was provoked by the impact of the decriminalization policy.
Drug users in B.C are still allowed to take them in private residences, in legal shelters, at overdose prevention, drug checking and supervised consumption sites, and at outpatient addiction services like rapid access addiction clinics.
That plan is scheduled to last for three years, having started on Jan. 31, 2023.
The problem isn't going away. The problem is the policy of allowing drug use in public wasn't saving lives.
Meanwhile, Toronto's public health department continued to lobby for decriminalization in the wake of B.C.'s reversal.
That was only killed off last week when Premier Doug Ford said the Ontario government would oppose Toronto's application and the federal government said it would not act without Ontario's consent.
Supporters of decriminalization argued the problems that allowing drug use in public created were a result of a lack of treatment programs and facilities.
But that's not what the B.C. government said when it launched it.
It said B.C. was "taking a critical step toward reducing the shame and fear associated with substance use" in conjunction with other harm reduction programs.
Now it's back to the drawing board.

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug use

Identifier / keyword: government; british; columbia; liberal; ottawa; abandoned

Title: The obvious problem in decriminalizing drugs

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

Document 483 of 500

Canadians living the low life

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-living-low-life/docview/3056554388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+living+the+low+life&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians'standard of living has declined to a level not seen for nearly 40 years. The average Canadian is now only as well off as when another Trudeau was in the prime minister's office.
The last time our standard of living was this low, the top movies were Footloose, Terminator and Ghostbusters and the top pop hits were When Doves Cry, What's Love Got to Do with It and Karma Chameleon. (Remember Boy George?) The cause of this disturbing decline is Liberal government economic illiteracy.
One of the clearest examples of what I mean is liquefied natural gas (LNG). Our PM, Justin Trudeau, who'd prefer to virtue signal about trendy causes, repeatedly says there is no "business case" for Canada to sell LNG to the world.
Forget the fact the PM wouldn't know a business case if it fell out of an overhead bin onto his head. In the time Trudeau has been PM, the Americans have built more than 20 LNG export facilities.
Canada has built none. Is that because Americans are too dumb to see there is no business case? Liberal policy and tax failures have driven down productivity, investment, incomes and the dollar while driving up the cost of nearly everything - especially food, housing and energy.
Under the Liberals, the cost of mortgages and rents have doubled.
Where it once took 44% of the average Canadian income to afford a house, it now takes 63%.
The Fraser Institute - the source of the calculation on our declining living standard - this week also released statistics showing that over the past 20 years, Ontario was ninth out of the 10 provinces in economic growth.
It was also dead last in inflation-adjusted wage growth.
The rest of the country may like to complain about Ontario, at least until the Leafs'inevitable early exit from the Stanley Cup playoffs each season. (I was in Toronto last weekend and chided a friend who lives there that Scotiabank Arena, home of the Leafs and Raptors, looks so good because there is always so much time between seasons to spruce the place up.)
Anyway, as much as the rest of us might shrug off Ontario's economic sluggishness, it's not good for the country when one of its economic engines is sputtering so badly.
University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe has pointed out that Ontario's per capita GDP is now equivalent to that of the state of Alabama.
This is the direct result of Liberal wokeness that has driven up inflation and driven down growth.
This past week, Statistics Canada announced that median income in Canada had increased slightly to $34.95 per hour.
That seems pretty good compared to a median income in the States of $34.75 per hour, until you consider the exchange rate.
In U.S. dollars, the median Canadian income is just $25.70, or about a quarter less than in America.
And on a purchasing-power basis, Canadians make about onethird less.
And I'm sorry, we're not going to grow our economy by taxing away everyone's shrinking incomes to subsidize international car-making giants to site EV battery plants and assembly lines here.
I am reluctant to bring up immigration again.
I'm not a nativist or xenophobe, but the math is unavoidable.
The best estimate stateside is that about 1.6 million illegals enter every year and a million legal immigrants.
That's 2.6 million into a country of 330 million.
The Canadian equivalent, per capita, would be 323,000 immigrants a year.
Yet, last year under the Liberals we took in 1.4 million - more than four times the number per capita of the U.S. - and our economy and housing markets aren't currently as robust as theirs.
This isn't bigotry, it's mathematics. And we aren't doing the newcomers any favours, either, by bringing them into a country with declining living standards.

Subject: Standard of living; Liquefied natural gas; Per capita

Business indexing term: Subject: Standard of living Per capita

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: living; declined; level; nearly; years; average

Title: Canadians living the low life

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.15

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 19, 2024

Section: Comment

GUNTER: Canadians living the low life

Author: Chau, Eddie

Publication info: Chau, Eddie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gunter-canadians-living-low-life/docview/3056473249/se-2?accountid=15090

EDITORIAL: The obvious problem in decriminalizing drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/editorial-obvious-problem-decriminalizing-drugs/docview/3056473247/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=EDITORIAL%3A+The+obvious+problem+in+decriminalizing+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 486 of 500

LILLEY: Trudeau's ongoing abortion rhetoric a sign of true desperation

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-trudeaus-ongoing-abortion-rhetoric-sign/docview/3056469377/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Trudeau%27s+ongoing+abortion+rhetoric+a+sign+of+true+desperation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If we're talking about abortion, it must be the month of May in Canada.
Each May, on the Thursday before Mother's Day, pro-life activists march to call for an end to abortion and at the same time, Liberals go on a rampage about how only they can protect abortion.
Happy Mother's Day all.
This year, trailing badly in the polls, Justin Trudeau is keeping up the pro-abortion rhetoric, warning women that voting for Conservatives will put them at risk. He did that Thursday at a campaign-style stop in New Brunswick to push the government's plan for a national school food program.
"We will keep fighting for women's rights," Trudeau said.
He said that as if our Supreme Court had ruled abortion a right, as it once had in the United States. That never happened in Canada, we simply have an absence of a law. But Trudeau loves to import American political issue into Canada while decrying American-style politics.
But I digress, what really has Trudeau riled up about abortion in New Brunswick is that they stopped funding abortion at a private clinic.
"The shutting down of health and reproductive services offered by Clinic 554 for the unwillingness to engage in allowing women to actually choose what happens to their future, their bodies is a disgrace," Trudeau said.
LILLEY: Ford tells Trudeau Ontario won't follow B.C.'s lead on drugs
LILLEY: Ghost of Iggy haunts the Liberals and Mark Carney's ambitions
LILLEY: Nothing 'cheap' about tying family's death in Hwy. 401 crash to justice system flaws
Does anyone else find it odd that Liberals are against private health care delivery if it's for knee replacement surgery or cataracts but are all in favour of private abortion clinics? Once again, if the Trudeau Liberals didn't have double standards, they wouldn't have any at all.
Officials with the Government of New Brunswick say roughly two-thirds of all abortions in the province are carried out with the abortion pill known as Mifegymiso. That has resulted in less demand for surgical abortions, which New Brunswick still funds in hospital settings.
Not good enough for Trudeau, he wants private clinics to provide abortions using public money.
Here's the thing about this annual fight over abortion that happens around Mother's Day, it doesn't matter, nothing is going to change.
Over the years there have been countless polls on the issue of abortion and what it shows, if you read these polls honestly, is that the Canadian public has a complex relationship with this issue. Most don't want abortion banned, but many are open to restrictions being place on abortion at some point in the pregnancy.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO
In Canada, there is no limit on when an abortion can take place. If you can get a doctor to provide an abortion in the ninth month of pregnancy it is perfectly legal and paid for - we have zero restrictions on abortion.
In Germany there are some limits placed on abortion after 12 weeks, in Italy after 13 weeks and in France after 16 weeks. The United Kingdom imposes some restrictions at 24 weeks and countries like Ireland, Portugal, Japan, Sweden and Spain all have varying levels of restrictions based on weeks of gestation.
We haven't had a law on abortion in Canada since the Supreme Court struck down the old one in the Morgentaler decision in 1988. We aren't likely to get one any time soon, despite the overheated rhetoric from the Liberals about electing a Conservative government.
Here's a dirty little secret of Canadian politics on this issue, a free vote on banning abortion that only took the votes of Conservative Party MPs would not succeed.
Even in the Conservative Party there is no appetite to ban abortion, not among the majority. A free vote held in the Commons on a bill to restrict abortion after the sixth month mark wouldn't even pass.
All of the posturing, the lecturing, and the social media posts on this issue from the Trudeau Liberals and their allies in the media is simply a sign of their desperation as Liberal poll numbers fall further.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Rhetoric; Womens health; Clinics; Abortion; Political parties

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: talking; abortion; month; canada; before; activists

Title: LILLEY: Trudeau's ongoing abortion rhetoric a sign of true desperation

Publication title: The Toronto Sun (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 18, 2024

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document 487 of 500

Slash's blues festival among Toronto's new summer live music offerings

Author: Stevenson, Jane

Publication info: Stevenson, Jane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/slashs-blues-festival-among-torontos-new-summer/docview/3056465967/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Slash%27s+blues+festival+among+Toronto%27s+new+summer+live+music+offerings&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Stevenson%2C+Jane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hot town, summer's officially in our city.
Toronto's outdoor live music season kicks off Monday night when Canadian rocker Neil Young and Crazy Horse open the Budweiser Stage's outdoor amphitheatre before returning to the same venue on July 8.
But that's just one of a record 80 shows at Budweiser Stage, which is celebrating its 30th anniversary this year.
"When (Neil Young) came up it was one of those things that just felt perfect," Live Nation Canada's President of Music Erik Hoffman said.
"It's very appropriate to have one of the most legendary artists of all time (open the season). It's a hot ticket so it's great he was able to come back (in July)."
Hoffman said nothing will be physically different inside the 16,000-seat capacity Budweiser Stage, but with construction going on the Ontario Place site there will be no parking on the Lakeshore side and likely more traffic (due to the narrowing of the Gardiner Expressway), so he encourages concert-goers to use both public transportation and bicycles.
Parking will still be available on the CNE side and over at Liberty Village.
How much would you pay to attend your dream concert?
NOW FOR PLAN A: Effort renewed to rename Riverdale Park East after Gord Downie
'Water's very big with me,' says Ringo Starr of his Niagara Falls return
"It's the toughest on traffic probably in the summer for Toronto in a long time, but it's the biggest concert year that we will ever have had there," said Hoffman.
"It's going to be about 80 shows this year. Last year was 66. So it's very significant. It's definitely cemented us as the number one large amphitheatre in North America. "
Of those, Hoffman said 35 are brand new Budweiser Stage headliners, including Canadian acts like Tate McCrae on July 27 and The Beaches on Aug. 22.
"Almost half the season will be new acts headlining for the first time, which is to me how you stay as relevant as ever," said Hoffman.
Avril Lavigne performs during the Juno Awards in Toronto on Sunday, March 15, 2022.
Among the fast-selling shows of this summer across all venues are many two-night stands including 21 Savage's May 25 and 27 at Budweiser Stage, One Direction's Niall Horan, June 28-29 at Scotiabank Arena, Alanis Morissette, July 13-14 at Budweiser Stage, Jennifer Lopez, Aug. 2-3 at Scotiabank Arena, Imagine Dragons, Aug. 8-9 at Budweiser Stage, Avril Lavigne on Aug. 12 at Scotiabank Arena and Aug. 16 at Budweiser Stage, New Kids On The Block, Aug. 17-18 at Budweiser Stage, and finally Sting with a whopping five nights at Massey Hall on Sept. 20-22 and 24-25.
"I just think people's relationship with live music has changed," Hoffman said. "You could say because of social media and seeing these things online. And like the FOMO (fear of missing out) effect. People are taking in more shows than ever."
Alanis Morissette performs at Bourbon and Beyond Music Festival at Kentucky Exposition Center on Thursday, Sept. 15, 2022, in Louisville, Ky.
There's also the annual summer music festivals returning, like Canadian Music Week (June 1-8), Luminato (June 5-16), NXNE (June 12-16), TD Toronto Jazz Fest ( June 21-30), Born and Raised Festival in Montebello Park, St. Catharines, Ont. (July 5-6), Veld Music Festival at Downsview Park (Aug. 2-4) and Boots and Hearts ( Aug. 8-11) at Burl's Creek in Oro-Medonte, Ont., the latter in its 12th year.
"I think the experience has a lot to do with (the longevity)," said Brooke Dunford, the talent buyer for Republic Live, owner of Burl's Creek, which can accommodate up to 70,000 people.
"They come and camp for four days and we've seen our day tickets grow. It's just about embracing what we call Boots Life of the country scene and it's just a unique experience that we're able to offer fans. Each year, it just keeps getting bigger and bigger."
Billy Talent strike a pose as musical talent take to the red carpet at the Juno Awards at the Canadian Tire Centre.
Otherwise, All Your Friends Fest is a new pop-punk festival including camping with headliners Billy Talent on Aug. 23 and Fall Out Boy on Aug. 23-24, also at Burl's Creek.
"Similar to Boots, we really love celebrating a community of fans," Dunford said.
"So based on artist availability and what fans are listening to right now, I think there's just a need in the market. And we were excited to have enough bands come together to really celebrate that nostalgic era and they're doing it in the U.S. and we're seeing successful festivals run with this genre so it was just something that was the right time, the right place."
Slahs's S.E.R.P.E.N.T. Festival is among the new summer live music offerings in an already packed schedule in Toronto.
Closer to home at Budweiser Stage, there's Slash's brand new blues-oriented S.E.R.P.E.N.T festival on July 28.
S.E.R P.E.N.T. stands for Solidarity, Engagement, Restore, Peace, Equality N' Tolerance and will feature the GNR guitarist playing with an all-star blues lineup including Keb' 'Mo, and Robert Randolph.
"It's a cool concept because he's obviously a guitar hero and it's kind of a crazy list of names," Hoffman said. "It's not who you might think."
And what about Drake's OVO fest that's often a staple over the August long weekend at Budweiser Stage?
So far, his OVO label artist PartyNextDoor has been announced for Aug. 2 at the venue, so who knows?
"I don't ever know about OVO until I know," Hoffman said. "We love Drake more than anything. He's delivered the gift of OVO so many times and I have no idea."
Drake in front of his mock CN Tower at the OVO Fest held at the Budweiser stage in Toronto, Ont. on Monday, Aug. 7, 2017.
There's also a brand new outdoor venue this summer with Barenaked Ladies, Sam Roberts and KT Tunstall on June 27 opening the 9,000-capacity The Bowl at Sobey's Stadium at York University, which is a partnership between The Feldman Agency and Tennis Canada.
Following that, Shaggy performs there on June 29 and The Tea Party, Headstones, I Mother Earth, Treble Charger and Bif Naked on July 6.
"Located near major highways and the subway, accessing the venue is hassle-free, with thousands of parking spaces available," the initial news release stated of the new open air venue.
Indoor highlights this summer include Snoop Dogg on June 12 at Scotiabank Arena, Roger Daltrey on June 14 at the OLG Stage at Fallsview Casino, and Rogers Centre shows such as Def Leppard with Journey and Heart on Aug. 2 and Blink 182 with Alexisonfire on Aug. 15.
"This summer's going to be quite something," said Hoffman.
Snoop Dogg performs during halftime of the NFL Super Bowl 56 football game between the Los Angeles Rams and the Cincinnati Bengals in Inglewood, Calif., Sunday, Feb. 13, 2022. Another celebrity appears set to join in the bidding war for the NHL's Ottawa Senators.
