Document 488 of 500

Robinson remembered as 'dedicated public servant'

Author: Stevenson, Jane

Publication info: Stevenson, Jane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/robinson-remembered-as-dedicated-public-servant/docview/3056291174/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Robinson+remembered+as+%27dedicated+public+servant%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Stevenson%2C+Jane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tributes from the premier, fellow councillors and others poured in for Toronto Councillor Jaye Robinson on Friday upon learning of her death Thursday at the age of 61.
Robinson was first elected in 2010 and represented Ward 15 - Don Valley West - for nearly 14 years.
"We are deeply saddened to announce that Councillor Jay Robinson passed away last night in the presence of her family," her office said in a post on X.
Premier Doug Ford said in a social media post he was "devastated" to hear of Robinson's passing.
"She was a dedicated public servant who I was fortunate to work with at city hall and who was devoted to the people she served in Don Valley West," he said on X. "My thoughts and prayers are with her family and loved ones as they grieve this terrible loss."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow offered condolences.
"I want to extend my heartfelt condolences to Councillor Robinson's family, friends and loved ones. She was a dedicated public servant who cared deeply about her constituents and Toronto. Her voice will be dearly missed."
Councillor Brad Bradford said on X: "Jaye Robinson was a dear colleague. I learned a lot from her, and we spent a lot of time speaking on the phone over the past year. She represented her constituents fiercely to the very end, and her voice will be missed at council and across our city."
The cause of Robinson's death wasn't immediately known.
In 2019, she said she'd be taking time away from city hall to focus on her health after being diagnosed with breast cancer.
She resumed her council duties in November 2020 after undergoing chemotherapy.
"Councillor Robinson was a good friend to Sunnybrook and will be remembered as a champion of our North Toronto community," read a post from Sunnybrook hospital on X.

Document 489 of 500

LILLEY: Ford tells Trudeau Ontario won't follow B.C.'s lead on drugs

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-tells-trudeau-ontario-wont-follow-b-c/docview/3056161395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+tells+Trudeau+Ontario+won%27t+follow+B.C.%27s+lead+on+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government is telling the Trudeau Liberals to back off their plans for decriminalizing drugs and demanding a change on so-called safe supply.
Two letters were fired off Thursday that left no question about where the Ontario's Conservative government stands on liberalizing drug laws in the province.
The first letter was to Toronto's Medical Officer of Health Dr. Eileen de Villa. In a jointly signed missive, Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner told de Villa they would not support her call for effectively legalizing all hard drugs in the city.
"Ontario is 100 per cent opposed to your proposal. Under no circumstances will our government ever support your request, which would only add to crime and public drug use while doing nothing to support people struggling with addiction," the letter stated.
The ministers went on to say that given the disastrous results of British Columbia's experience with decriminalization that they were surprised Toronto had not already rescinded its application for decriminalization to the federal government.
In fact, Dr. de Villa, who thankfully announced she is stepping down soon, was defending Toronto's plan to ask for decriminalization just three weeks ago.
The Trudeau Liberals, meanwhile, have been playing semantic games around Toronto's application to follow B.C.'s deadly path, at times saying they wouldn't rule it out, and at other times saying there is no application before them - even though there definitely is.
In the second letter issued Thursday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford told Prime Minister Justin Trudeau that he not only doesn't want to follow B.C.'s path on decriminalization, he wants a review of the federal safe supply sites operating in the province.
"In Ontario, due to Health Canada's siloed approval process, the province is completely in the dark about where these federally approved sites are operating and the quantity of controlled and illegal substances they dispense. This is frankly unacceptable, given its adverse effects on our communities," Ford wrote to Trudeau.
LILLEY: B.C.'s horrific drug decriminalization experience serves as a warning
LILLEY: Regrettable B.C. drug policy may come to Toronto under Trudeau Liberals
FUREY: Eileen de Villa push to decriminalize hard drugs for kids and teens backed by Olivia Chow
Without provincial approval or input, the Trudeau government has set up 16 sites in Ontario listed on the federal government's website as offering so-called safer supply. In these programs, addicts are given hydromorphone, a pharmaceutical grade opioid that is supposed to help deal with their addiction by replacing dangerous street drugs with clean drugs.
As Ford pointed out to Trudeau, too often these prescribed drugs are sold or traded on the street for harder drugs like fentanyl with the hydromorphone pills being sold to new and younger users.
"It also indicated that diversion is contributing to higher youth opioid use rates and has led to individuals in successful treatment services relapsing due to easy access," Ford wrote to Trudeau.
It's unclear what the Trudeau government's response to this letter will be - they have been wedded to safe supply and liberalizing drug laws for years. As the side effects of their policies became evident over the last year or more, they have denied problems even exist.
When asked about diversion of safe supply drugs by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre a year ago, then Liberal minister for addictions Carolyn Bennett said that safe supply and B.C.'s move to decriminalization were saving lives.
But nothing could be further from the truth if you look at the facts.
In the first three months of this year, B.C. had 572 drug overdose deaths, more than the 529 they had in all of 2015 or the 370 recorded in 2014. In 2023, when the province, with federal approval, moved to decriminalize all hard drugs to lower drug deaths, the B.C. recorded a record 2,551 overdose deaths.
That's more than all of Ontario despite B.C. having a population that is one-third of Ontario's.
Ford finished off his letter to Trudeau calling for a partnership between the province and the federal government focused on public safety and treatment services for those who need it.
Looking at what happened in B.C., including the staggering number of overdose deaths, that would seem the wiser path.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug overdose; Drug policy; Narcotics

Identifier / keyword: government; telling; trudeau; liberals; plans; decriminalizing

Title: LILLEY: Ford tells Trudeau Ontario won't follow B.C.'s lead on drugs

What are they on?!; Ford tells PM, doc Ontario doesn't want their dopey fix

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-are-they-on-ford-tells-pm-doc-ontario-doesnt/docview/3056107207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+are+they+on%3F%21%3B+Ford+tells+PM%2C+doc+Ontario+doesn%27t+want+their+dopey+fix&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 491 of 500

GOLDSTEIN: Canadians' standard of living is on decline, report says

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goldstein-canadians-standard-living-is-on-decline/docview/3055866085/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GOLDSTEIN%3A+Canadians%27+standard+of+living+is+on+decline%2C+report+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are currently experiencing one of the worst and longest declines in their standard of living in decades, according to a new report by the Fraser Institute.
"Despite claims to the contrary, living standards are declining in Canada," study co-author Grady Munro says in the report by the fiscally conservative think tank, "Changes in Per-Person GDP (Income): 1985 to 2023."
The study says that from April 2019 to the end of 2023, inflation-adjusted, per-person GDP in Canada, a broad measure of living standards, fell from $59,905 annually to $58,111 - a 3% loss and the third-steepest decline in almost 40 years.
Only a 5.3% drop in real GDP Canadians experienced from 1989 to 1992 and a 5.2% drop from 2008 to 2009 were more severe.
The study also says the latest decline in living standards which lasted for 18 fiscal quarters from 2019 to 2023 is already the second-longest in almost 40 years, surpassed only by one that lasted 21 fiscal quarters from1989 to 1994, "and if not stabilized in 2024, this decline could be the steepest and longest in four decades."
"The severity of the decline in living standards should be a wakeup call to policymakers across Canada to immediately enact fundamental policy reforms to help spur economic growth and productivity," said study co-author Jason Clemens.
The Trudeau government has acknowledged that slow economic growth and low productivity is endangering the standard of living for Canadians, a problem it says long precedes the current Liberal government.
In her 2022 budget, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland described low productivity and innovation as "the Achilles heel of the Canadian economy."
"We are falling behind when it comes to economic productivity," Freeland said.
"Productivity matters because it is what guarantees the dream of every parent - that our children will be more prosperous than we are. This is a well-known Canadian problem-and an insidious one. It is time for Canada to tackle it."
A chart in the Liberals' 2022 budget projected that Canada was in danger of experiencing the lowest annual growth in real GDP per capita between 2020 and 2060 among 16 comparable countries that are members of the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development, as well as lower growth than the OECD average and lower than every other member of the G7 - U.S., U.K., Germany, France, Italy and Japan.
"The stakes are high," the Liberals' 2022 budget said.
"Most Canadian businesses have not invested at the same rate as their U.S. counterparts. Unless this changes, the OECD projects that Canada will have the lowest per-capita GDP growth among its member countries."
The Liberals promised to tackle the issue in part by creating "a new innovation and investment agency - drawing on international best practices from around the world - that will give companies across the country and across our economy the tools and incentives they need to create and invent, and to produce more with less."
Report card ranks Ontario elementary schools on reading, writing and math
GOLDSTEIN: Exaggerated claims about climate change costing us money, report says
Their 2022 budget also said the federal government would "encourage small Canadian companies to get bigger" and "help Canadians and Canadian companies develop new IP (intellectual property) - and turn their new ideas into new businesses and new jobs."
The problem, critics say, is that in the Liberals' 2024 budget released last month, its major themes - higher spending, higher deficits, higher debt and higher capital gains taxes - are all sure-fire ways to reduce business confidence in the economy, discouraging private sector investment aimed at increasing productivity and innovation.
RECOMMENDED VIDEO

Document 492 of 500

Ford's auto motive; Ontario government's car theft move aimed at Trudeau, not thieves

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-auto-motive-ontario-governments-car-theft/docview/3055263163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+auto+motive%3B+Ontario+government%27s+car+theft+move+aimed+at+Trudeau%2C+not+thieves&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government's announcement that they will give car thieves a 10-year ban on driving won't leave the crooks shaking in their boots. Then again, it's not meant to scare the thugs breaking into homes to steal cars, it's meant to put pressure on the federal government.
Auto theft in Ontario, especially in the Greater Toronto Area, Hamilton and Ottawa, has become a major problem. It's not just that cars get jacked, it's how it is happening. Far too often it involves home invasions carried out by brazen criminals who have no fear of the law.
"Car theft is a cowardly and often violent crime that can traumatize victims and communities who experience it," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said on Tuesday, while announcing the pending legislation.
If passed, this bill would see anyone convicted of auto theft get a 10-year licence suspension for a first offence, a 15-year licence suspension for a second conviction and a lifetime licence suspension for a third.
"Criminals who want to steal a car in Ontario need to know there will be severe consequences for doing so," Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said at the announcement.
Both ministers were talking tough, but let's be clear, no one stealing a car in Ontario is going to think twice about doing so based on these penalties. That's not to say this move is pointless. It gives police and prosecutors new tools to go after thieves who are caught and convicted.
Anyone caught driving with a 10-year suspension could face an additional six months in prison. Maybe not a big deterrent, but it's still a tool to try to control the criminal element.
The real focus of this announcement is the Trudeau government in Ottawa.
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau have often worked well together, and were even at an announcement together on Tuesday, the two political leaders have different takes on crime.
"I believe in mandatory sentences," Ford said about the violent home invasions carried out by car thieves.
"What we've seen here in Ontario is people committing crimes, heinous crimes, and they're out on bail not once, not twice, three, four, five times."
His message, and that ministers earlier in the day, of his ministers earlier in the day, was that the federal government needs to step up on this issue.
"We are calling on the federal government to continue to pursue tougher penalties on auto theft," Sarkaria said.
Kerzner, meanwhile, said the federal government can help Ontario deal with this issue in two important ways.
"No. 1, there has to be minimum consequences, minimum sentencing for people stealing cars," Kerzner said.
"And the second thing, equally important, is you must step up the inspections of outgoing cargo at the intermodals at the rail yards, and most importantly at the Port of Montreal."
Cars stolen in Ontario are often shipped out of the country through the Port of Montreal, which spends most of its time screening what comes into the country rather than what goes out. A police operation carried out between last December and this March saw 400 shipping containers inspected at the Port of Montreal and nearly 600 stolen vehicles recovered.
Most of the stolen vehicles were from the Toronto area.
The federal government knows what the problem is - it just doesn't want to act. For months, Opposition leader Pierre Poilievre has been hammering the Trudeau Liberals on the lack of scanners at the Port of Montreal for outgoing traffic. Rather than commit to installing more scanners, they have launched partisan attacks against the Conservatives.
Auto theft is a very real issue, costing billions at this point in lost vehicles, lost productivity, insurance claims and more. Investing in the people or technology to stop it is the right move.
Putting in stiffer penalties for stealing a car would also go a long way to keeping repeat offenders off the streets.
The Ford government's latest move won't solve anything, but if it pushes the Trudeau government in the right direction, it is a worthy effort. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 493 of 500

LILLEY: Ford government's auto theft move aimed at Trudeau, not thieves

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lilley-ford-governments-auto-theft-move-aimed-at/docview/3055159428/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LILLEY%3A+Ford+government%27s+auto+theft+move+aimed+at+Trudeau%2C+not+thieves&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government's announcement that they will give car thieves a 10-year ban on driving won't leave the crooks shaking in their boots. Then again, it's not meant to scare the thugs breaking into homes to steal cars, it's meant to put pressure on the federal government.
Auto theft in Ontario, especially in the Greater Toronto Area, Hamilton and Ottawa, has become a major problem. It's not just cars getting jacked, it's how it is happening. Far too often it involves home invasions carried out by brazen criminals who have no fear of the law.
"Car theft is a cowardly and often violent crime that can traumatize victims and communities who experience it," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said on Tuesday, while announcing the pending legislation
If passed, this new bill would see anyone convicted of auto theft receive a 10-year licence suspension for a first offence, a 15-year licence suspension for a second conviction and a lifetime licence suspension for a third.
"Criminals who want to steal a car in Ontario need to know there will be severe consequences for doing so," Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said at the announcement.
Both ministers were talking tough, but let's be clear, no one stealing a car in Ontario is going to think twice about doing so based on these penalties. That's not to say this move is pointless; it gives police and prosecutors new tools to go after thieves who are caught and convicted.
Anyone caught driving with a 10-year suspension could face an additional six months in prison. Maybe not a big deterrent, but it's still a tool to try and control the criminal element.
The real focus of this announcement is the Trudeau government in Ottawa.
Recommended video
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau have often worked well together, they were even at an announcement together on Tuesday, the two political leaders have different takes on crime.
"I believe in mandatory sentences," Ford said about the violent home invasions carried out by car thieves.
"What we've seen here in Ontario is people committing crimes, heinous crimes, and they're out on bail not once, not twice, three, four, five times."
His message - and that of his ministers earlier in the day - was that the federal government needs to step up on this issue.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau attend an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont., on Feb. 9, 2024. (The Canadian Press)
"We are calling on the federal government to continue to pursue tougher penalties on auto theft," Sarkaria said.
Kerzner, meanwhile, said the federal government can help Ontario deal with this issue in two important ways.
"Number one, there has to be minimum consequences, minimum sentencing for people stealing cars," Kerzner said.
"And the second thing - equally important - is you must step up the inspections of outgoing cargo at the intermodals at the rail yards, and most importantly, at the Port of Montreal."
Cars stolen in Ontario are often shipped out of the country through the Port of Montreal, which spends most of its time screening what comes into the country rather than what goes out. A police operation carried out between last December and this March saw 400 shipping containers inspected at the Port of Montreal and nearly 600 stolen vehicles recovered.
Most of the stolen vehicles were from the Toronto area.
The federal government knows what the problem is; it just doesn't want to act. For months, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre has been hammering the Trudeau Liberals on the lack of scanners at the Port of Montreal for outgoing traffic. Rather than commit to installing more scanners, they have launched partisan attacks against the Conservatives.
Auto theft is a very real issue, costing billions at this point in lost vehicles, lost productivity, insurance claims and more. Investing in the people or technology to stop it is the right move.
Putting in stiffer penalties for stealing a car would also go a long way to keeping repeat offenders off the streets.
The Ford government's latest move won't solve anything, but if it pushes the Trudeau government in the right direction, then it is a worthy effort.
LILLEY: Lecce looks to put controls on speakers at Ontario schools
LILLEY: Job growth in Canada led by increase in public sector workers

CAUGHT ON CAMERA: Asked about low poll numbers, Trudeau attacks Alberta's oil and gas sector

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/caught-on-camera-asked-about-low-poll-numbers/docview/3055129777/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CAUGHT+ON+CAMERA%3A+Asked+about+low+poll+numbers%2C+Trudeau+attacks+Alberta%27s+oil+and+gas+sector&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WATCH BELOW: At an announcement for a new EV battery facility in Ontario, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was asked about his low poll numbers. Another poll, this one from Nanos, shows Trudeau's Liberals trailing Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives by 20 points - 43% support to 23%. Trudeau didn't answer the question put to him, he went on a rant about how great he is and against the oil and gas industry.

Document 495 of 500

Questionable quartet; Fate put 4 unlikable people in harm's way of homicide

Author: Hunter, Brad

Publication info: Hunter, Brad. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/questionable-quartet-fate-put-4-unlikable-people/docview/3054679499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Questionable+quartet%3B+Fate+put+4+unlikable+people+in+harm%27s+way+of+homicide&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Hunter%2C+Brad&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The victim wasn't very likeable. Likewise his moneyloving stripper girlfriend.
Nor was the rich girl aspiring influencer who was the alleged triggerman's moll.
As for the alleged gunman himself? He is the least likeable of the lot. As the Hamilton firstdegree murder trial of Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li winds down - summations are slated for Thursday and Friday - the whole bloody affair leaves a sour aftertaste.
Evidence presented over the past five weeks showed a quartet of questionable characters looking for Fred Flintstone-like getrich-quick schemes. Their greed and stupidity on full display at every turn.
Karafa, 29, and his wife, one-time "influencer" Li, 26, are on trial for first-degree murder and attempted murder in the Feb. 28, 2021, slaying of B.C. cocaine dealer Tyler Pratt, 39, and the shooting of his pregnant fiancee Jordyn Romano.
Romano testified that the pair were attacked in an industrial area in Stoney Creek after a botched scheme to sell COVID-19-related medical equipment in Eastern Europe came off the rails.
That put Karafa - the scam's alleged author - into Pratt for a cool $500,000.
After the murder, Karafa and Li went to Montreal and then flew out of the country to Eastern Europe where they were arrested in Hungary in June 2021.
Both have pleaded not guilty. Pratt, 39, was a fairly big cocaine dealer in B.C. with reputed connections to the violent Wolf Pack gang and other nefarious characters. His plan was to relocate to Ontario with Romano and get cooking with some new schemes.
Pratt himself liked a taste of Bolivian marching powder with a chaser of muscle-boosting steroids. He was looking for easy money and thought he'd found it in the form of Oliver Karafa.
As for Romano, 29, she told court she had packed in her highly lucrative stripping career ($250,000 per annum) at the behest of Pratt. But money wouldn't be a problem, his cocaine trafficking operation brought in a lot of cash, she testified.
That dough kept her in Gucci, Versace, Louboutin, Birkin, exotic travel, $80,000 watches and $7,200-a-month Yorkville condos.
During her two days on the stand, her answers were punctuated with "likes," and her demeanour could be described as obtuse. Li's lawyer, Liam O'Connor, suggested that Romano's "moral compass is a little off-kilter."
And the surgically enhanced Li - whose wealthy mother has deep ties with the Liberal Party - comes off worse.
In five days on the witness stand, Li insisted she didn't know a murder was about to go down.
She told skeptical Hamilton homicide detectives that she had "a perfectly reasonable answer to what I thought was going on." And also, she wasn't happy with her extradition flight because she was used to flying first class.
Li called Pratt a "scary gang person" and told cops she didn't want anything to do with him or Romano. She, Lucy Li, was not involved in the two men's "business meetings." She added the couple were "not presentable people."
According to Romano, on the day before Karafa was supposed to pay Pratt back the dough from the ill-fated PPE mask venture, the B.C. man was shot to death, and she barely escaped with her life.
Li told the court that she missed a slew of "red flags" regarding the participants.
"I'm not OK with hanging out with drug dealers or gangsters ... and most importantly, I'd never be OK with a plan to commit murder," she told the court, adding it was only months later that Karafa allegedly told her he shot Pratt in self-defence and that the dope dealer was trying to kill them.
As for Karafa, we will not hear from him.
He has sat quietly in the court, unmovable as a stone, steadfast in his belief that he, Oliver Karafa, is the smartest guy in the room. bhunter@postmedia.com @HunterTOSun

Subject: Cocaine; Murders & murder attempts; Trials

Location: Eastern Europe

People: Li, Lucy

Document 497 of 500

Get your game on the go; 5 awesome - and free - apps to try your luck

Author: Saltzman, Marc

Publication info: Saltzman, Marc. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/get-your-game-on-go-5-awesome-free-apps-try-luck/docview/3053896497/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Get+your+game+on+the+go%3B+5+awesome+-+and+free+-+apps+to+try+your+luck&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=Saltzman%2C+Marc&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Have smartphone, will travel? You're not alone. About 96% of Canadians age 15 and older now own a smartphone, according to StatsCan, making it the most popular piece of technology we own - and something just as ideal to use away from home as in it.
In fact, now that warmer months are upon us, these pocket-sized companions are a smart travel accessory - great for staying in touch, navigating around unfamiliar cities and capturing memories with family and friends.
They're also ideal for staying entertained on planes, trains, and as passengers in automobiles.
Whether you're looking for something fun and free to download to your device - or ideas to reduce the dreaded "Are we there yet?" from the backseat - the following are five titles worth considering from the App Store (iPhone, iPad) or Google Play (Android devices).
Note: While free to play, most of the following games include optional in-app purchases for additional content. 1. PUZZLE PAGE If you like games like crossword puzzles, word searches, picture cross and sudoku, one freebie called Puzzle Page has it all in one place.
As the name suggests, it's similar to a page of head-scratchers in a newspaper, with a variety of games to play that day, and you get a fresh batch the following day. But at any time, go back through the in-game calendar to play a previous day's collection of digital diversions.
Appy Nation's suite of puzzles games are largely based on number, word and picture games (including jigsaws), plus there are daily challenges, unlockable "achievement" trophies, and rewards.
Puzzle Page is a must-install for fans of these kinds of puzzlers. 2. THE SILENT AGE If you prefer a more story-driven adventure, The Silent Age challenges you to save the world by traveling through time.
You start off as a janitor in the early 1970s. While mopping the floors of a building you stumble upon a mysterious man who warns you of a catastrophic event before he hands you a time travel device, and then dies.
By visiting the past, present and future, you're tasked with collecting items that can be used at the right time and place. For example, you'll need to pour poison on a plant so that it won't grow and block a passageway forty years from now.
Developed by Danish company House on Fire, the first episode is free to play and should take you a couple of hours to tap through, and you'll have the option to pay $7 to continue playing on to the next episode.
The hand-drawn animation, moody sound effects and music are all top-notch (headphones recommended), and it all adds nicely to the game's chilling atmosphere. 3. ROYAL MATCH If you like Candy Crush, you'll love Royal Match.
Published by Dream Games, this is a "Match 3" puzzler with a gameplay mechanic that should be familiar to Candy Crush (or Bejeweled) fans, as you must swap to match at least three of the same items on a board, which causes them to disappear.
Your goal is to break adjacent obstacles, unleash power-ups and unlock new levels as you help the hapless King Robert rebuild his castle (and surrounding areas) to its former glory.
The levels get increasingly difficult, just as new obstacles and objects are added as you progress.
While free, there are no ads to sit through, which is refreshing.
There are bonus levels, minigames, timed tournaments, and you can see how you fare against others via the global leaderboard. 4. DIABLO IMMORTAL One of the most influential (and addictive) computer games now has a popular offshoot for mobile devices.
Blizzard Entertainment and NetEase's Diablo Immortal is a free-to-play massively multiplayer game that fuses fast-paced hackand-slash action with role-playing game (RPG) mechanics, in a timeline nestled between the events of Diablo II and Diablo III.
You'll first choose a character class with its own unique attributes - Barbarian, Wizard, Crusader, Monk, Necromancer, Blood Knight, or Demon Hunter - and then set out across dangerous lands and subterranean dungeons to prevent the malevolent Skarn, the Lord of Damnation, from destroying the world.
Expect a similar look, sound and feel to its popular PC predecessors. 5. FARMVILLE 3 Zynga's FarmVille franchise is one of the more popular "sims" (simulations) you can play - and now FarmVille 3 has "cropped" up for players of all ages.
As you might expect, use your fingertip to create and maintain your own bustling farm.
Breed animals (hundreds of types to choose from), harvest what they produce (eggs, milk, wool, meat), trade or sell goods to complete orders for customers, and grow your farm into an expansive and sustainable business.
Much of the fun for kids (and kids at heart) is matching and mating your animals to raise babies, and discover new breeds - including rare ones - as well as customizing and designing your family ranch home with unique building styles, decorations, outfits and more.
There's also an option to join someone else playing FarmVille 3, online, to complete special co-op (cooperative) events to unlock new farm animals and special items.

Subject: Travel; Smartphones; Games

Business indexing term: Subject: Smartphones

Identifier / keyword: smartphone; travel; alone; canadians; older; according

Title: Get your game on the go; 5 awesome - and free - apps to try your luck

Publication title: The Toronto Sun; Toronto

First page: A.14

Publication year: 2024

SALTZMAN: Spring travellers, download these 5 awesome - and free - smartphone games

Author: Doucette, Chris

Publication info: Doucette, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/saltzman-spring-travellers-download-these-5/docview/3053789146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SALTZMAN%3A+Spring+travellers%2C+download+these+5+awesome+-+and+free+-+smartphone+games&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Doucette%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Have smartphone, will travel?
You're not alone.
About 96% of Canadians age 15 and older now own a smartphone, according to StatsCan, making it the most popular piece of technology we own - and something just as ideal to use away from home as in it.
In fact, now that warmer months are upon us, these pocket-sized companions are a smart travel accessory - great for staying in touch, navigating around unfamiliar cities and capturing memories with family and friends.
They're also ideal for staying entertained on planes, trains, and as passengers in automobiles.
Whether you're looking for something fun and free to download to your device - or ideas to reduce the dreaded "Are we there yet?" from the backseat - the following are five titles worth considering from the App Store (iPhone, iPad) or Google Play (Android devices).
Note: While free to play, most of the following games include optional in-app purchases for additional content.
Puzzle Page has crossword puzzles, word searches, picture cross and sudoku all in one place.
PUZZLE PAGE
If you like games like crossword puzzles, word searches, picture cross and sudoku, one freebie called Puzzle Page has it all in one place.
As the name suggests, it's similar to a page of head-scratchers in a newspaper, with a variety of games to play that day, and you get a fresh batch the following day. But at any time, go back through the in-game calendar to play a previous day's collection of digital diversions.
Appy Nation's suite of puzzles games are largely based on number, word and picture games (including jigsaws), plus there are daily challenges, unlockable "achievement" trophies, and rewards.
Puzzle Page is a must-install for fans of these kinds of puzzlers.
The Silent Age challenges you to save the world by traveling through time.
THE SILENT AGE
If you prefer a more story-driven adventure, The Silent Age challenges you to save the world by traveling through time.
You start off as a janitor in the early 1970s. While mopping the floors of a building you stumble upon a mysterious man who warns you of a catastrophic event before he hands you a time travel device, and then dies.
By visiting the past, present and future, you're tasked with collecting items that can be used at the right time and place. For example, you'll need to pour poison on a plant so that it won't grow and block a passageway forty years from now.
Developed by Danish company House on Fire, the first episode is free to play and should take you a couple of hours to tap through, and you'll have the option to pay $7 to continue playing on to the next episode.
The hand-drawn animation, moody sound effects and music are all top-notch (headphones recommended), and it all adds nicely to the game's chilling atmosphere.
Royal Match is a tile-matching video game similar to Candy Crush.
ROYAL MATCH
If you like Candy Crush, you'll love Royal Match
Published by Dream Games, this is a "Match 3" puzzler with a gameplay mechanic that should be familiar to Candy Crush (or Bejeweled) fans, as you must swap to match at least three of the same items on a board, which causes them to disappear. Your goal is to break adjacent obstacles, unleash power-ups and unlock new levels as you help the hapless King Robert rebuild his castle (and surrounding areas) to its former glory.
The levels get increasingly difficult, just as new obstacles and objects are added as you progress.
While free, there are no ads to sit through, which is refreshing.
There are bonus levels, mini-games, timed tournaments, and you can see how you fare against others via the global leaderboard.
Diablo Immortal fuses fast-paced hack-and-slash action with role-playing game (RPG) mechanics, in a timeline nestled between the events of Diablo II and Diablo III.
DIABLO IMMORTAL
One of the most influential (and addictive) computer games now has a popular offshoot for mobile devices.
Blizzard Entertainment and NetEase's Diablo Immortal is a free-to-play massively multiplayer game that fuses fast-paced hack-and-slash action with role-playing game (RPG) mechanics, in a timeline nestled between the events of Diablo II and Diablo III.
You'll first choose a character class with its own unique attributes - Barbarian, Wizard, Crusader, Monk, Necromancer, Blood Knight, or Demon Hunter - and then set out across dangerous lands and subterranean dungeons to prevent the malevolent Skarn, the Lord of Damnation, from destroying the world.
Expect a similar look, sound and feel to its popular PC predecessors.
FarmVille 3 is a sim that allows players to create and maintain their own bustling farm.
FARMVILLE 3
Zynga's FarmVille franchise is one of the more popular "sims" (simulations) you can play - and now FarmVille 3 has "cropped" up for players of all ages.
As you might expect, use your fingertip to create and maintain your own bustling farm.
Breed animals (hundreds of types to choose from), harvest what they produce (eggs, milk, wool, meat), trade or sell goods to complete orders for customers, and grow your farm into an expansive and sustainable business.
SALTZMAN: It's TV time ... but which one to buy?
SALTZMAN: Hit the road, Jack (or Jill)
SALTZMAN: Is your tech killing you?
Much of the fun for kids (and kids at heart) is matching and mating your animals to raise babies, and discover new breeds - including rare ones - as well as customizing and designing your family ranch home with unique building styles, decorations, outfits and more.
There's also an option to join someone else playing FarmVille 3, online, to complete special co-op (cooperative) events to unlock new farm animals and special items.
Assassin's Creed Mirage will be playable on the iPhone 15 Pro (and Pro Max) and any iPad with an M1 chip (or later) starting June 6, 2024.
ASSASSIN'S CREED MIRAGE COMING TO MOBILE
Already a hit on consoles and Windows PC, Ubisoft's Assassin's Creed Mirage will be playable on the iPhone 15 Pro (and Pro Max) and any iPad with an M1 chip (or later), starting June 6.
As proof smartphones and tablets are becoming more powerful, this is the first Assassin's Creed console and computer game that can also be played natively on mobile devices.
Explore 9th century Baghdad as Basim, a young thief who evolves into a Master Assassin, as you'll use your fingertips on the touchscreen or opt for a wireless gamepad.
Cross-platform saves are supported, so you can start the game on, say, an Xbox Series X or PlayStation 5 machine, and then continue where you left off on an iPhone. No word yet on Android support.
Available at the App Store starting June 6, Assassin's Creed Mirage will be free to play for 90 minutes but then will require a one-time purchase after that (price is TBD).

Subject: Crossword puzzles; Role playing; Travel; Computer & video games; Smartphones

Need more housing? Relief from HST a good place to start

Author: Lyall, Richard

Publication info: Lyall, Richard. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/need-more-housing-relief-hst-good-place-start/docview/3053674267/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Need+more+housing%3F+Relief+from+HST+a+good+place+to+start&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.6&amp;au=Lyall%2C+Richard&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Political leaders at all levels of government are now waking to the fact that building more housing - affordable housing in particular - is critical to keeping up with our rapidly growing population.
With 1.3 million new immigrants to Canada in the last three years and plans to welcome up to 542,000 in 2024 and 550,000 in 2025, it's clear that we need to ratchet up the number of new affordable housing units that are being built. It must be deemed a key priority going forward.
However, affordable housing has become a pipe dream for many. Young people are leaving our cities and seeking accommodation elsewhere - sometimes out of the country - due to lack of supply.
The crux of the matter is that interest rate increases, along with excessive taxes, fees and levies, along with red tape and bureaucracy are a big reason that housing has become unaffordable.Change must be made.
The federal and provincial governments have opted to exempt all forms of purpose-built rental, whether affordable or market, from the HST/GST. However, affordable ownership homes delivered by nonprofit organizations like Habitat for Humanity continue to be subject to the taxes. This will ultimately lead to fewer new affordable ownership units being built in Canada.
It is disappointing that, in their recent budgets, neither the provincial nor federal governments made a commitment to any HST/GST relief for non-profit affordable ownership homes.
In the Greater Toronto Area, the HST/GST is adding an average of $80,000 per home to the price that Habitat for Humanity incurs to deliver desperately needed new affordable ownership homes. For every 100 homes the organization delivers, they could provide 13 more if they weren't burdened with the additional expense.
Exempting or deferring these taxes on non-profit affordable ownership homes would represent a loss of only 0.1 to 0.5 per cent of total revenues from HST/GST on new homes. Seems there is plenty to gain in the way of affordable housing from making such a small sacrifice.
Both RESCON and Habitat for Humanity have raised the issue with the federal and provincial governments. We have written to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford in hopes of convincing them to provide HST/GST relief for non-profit affordable ownership homes.
We have urged both governments to move quickly to expand the HST/GST relief that's now in place for purpose-built rental to also include non-profit affordable ownership housing.
We would also encourage specific action to provide a full rebate of the HST/GST for first-time buyers of new homes. They're being taxed on new housing at rates which would've crushed their parents and grandparents. Governments must do more to help them get into the market.
The need for affordable housing has reached a tipping point.We must do something to turn the tide. Inaction is not an option.
Richard Lyall is president of the Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON). Contact him at media@rescon.com.

Subject: Affordable housing; Nonprofit organizations

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Raising bonds in Kensington Market: KMCLT has big plans

Author: Barker, Paul

Publication info: Barker, Paul. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/raising-bonds-kensington-market-kmclt-has-big/docview/3053673408/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Raising+bonds+in+Kensington+Market%3A+KMCLT+has+big+plans&amp;title=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;issn=08373175&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.8&amp;au=Barker%2C+Paul&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was an event aimed at not developers, but a neighbourhood organization, and it was all about an attempt to stop or at least slow down the gentrification of Kensington Market.
Late last month, local residents politicians and members of the Kensington Market Community Land Trust (KMCLT), a non-profit organization, gathered at Café Pamenar on Augusta Ave. to celebrate the March 25 launch of a fundraising initiative called Kensington Community Bonds.
The goal: Raise up to $2 million through the bonds in order for the KMCLT to acquire a second property in Kensington that it said will help it "preserve affordable housing and prevent further displacement of legacy businesses by taking more property off the not-for-profit real estate market."
Incorporated in 2017, it stated in in a release issued in March that "it has sought to keep housing affordable and accessible for everyone in the community. The organization aims to provide stability to longtime renters especially those who face gentrification and renoviction."
It notes that KMCLT's "current portfolio consists of one property in Kensington Market, which was purchased after a resident-led resistance to the previous landlord's illegal eviction process."
That move resulted in the city of Toronto giving the group $3 million to buy, build and renovate the property at 54-56 Kensington Ave. as affordable housing for a 99-year term. The building has 12 residential units as well as five retailers.
"The community land trust has kept its 12 residential units affordable and provided five diverse businesses with long-term lease stability. 54-56 Kensington Avenue's current residential rental rates average well under 80 per cent of Average Market Rent ($1,142 for a bachelor apartment, and $1,594 for a 2-bedroom) set by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC)," the organization says.
"The land trust will be using $200,000 raised from the bonds to pay off a portion of the mortgage on this existing property."
Dominique Russell, co-chair of the land trust, points out that the use of bonds with investment minimums starting at $500 and interest rates of up to 4.5 per cent, allows the group to use "community-sourced funding rather than relying on an external lender for a mortgage. The community bond model gives us control over our financing terms. And instead of paying interest to banks, we will be putting this money directly into the community we serve."
At the April launch event, speakers included Toronto mayor Olivia Chow, Russell, University-Rosedale city councillor Dr. Dianne Saxe, the last environmental commissioner of Ontario - the Ford government abolished her office in 2019, MPP for University-Rosedale Jessica Bell and Kevin Barrett, co-chair of the KMCLT.
"This community bond campaign is part of a larger vision for the community land trust, of investing in our community, and trapping resources in our community," Barrett said. "We spend an awful lot of time seeing an awful lot of mechanisms by which people put money into this community to take profit out. And that is what is creating a crisis in terms of the lack of affordable places to have a shop, and the lack of affordable places to live. We are looking to restructure that market completely, and the community bond campaign, I think, is a great way to put those pieces together."
Bell, the Ontario NDP critic for housing, said the Ford government, has not fixed the housing crisis because, "it has never been more expensive to rent, or own a home in Toronto or Ontario. And we need real solutions.
"Now it is my job as a provincial representative to stand up for strong rent control, to invest and build thousands of affordable homes and non-market homes on public land. And to make it make it easier for people to buy a home, which means making it harder for people to buy their 12th home or their 30th home. It is about human rights. And it is about making sure that housing is for people."
Bell added that Kensington residents and the KCMLT mean "business. They are not just going to rely on politicians to solve an issue. These folks are saying we are going to solve it here by taking action and by buying up property and turning it into affordable housing one building at a time. It is practical. It is brilliant."
Saxe noted that as the environmental commissioner, she traveled all over Ontario and "what I heard over and over again, is people want to invest in their community, see improvements in places that they love, and keep the money at home. And that is what this will do. (The bond) is such a great innovation. and I'm delighted to see it. We are working with you to get more improvements in Kensington."
Chow, meanwhile, urged local residents to "please invest because not only are you going to put a little bit more money in your pocket - not too much, but a little bit - you will feel good about it, because you are enhancing the livelihood of your neighbourhood."

Subject: Affordable housing; Gentrification
